The payment-auth path specifically uses the Groth16 AgentPolicy build. PlonkVerifier remains unchanged and unaffected.

new_components_needed.description: PaymentAuthPolicy circuit (Groth16 only): extends AgentPolicy with four additional public inputs — paymentAmountCents (uint64), recipientAddressHash (Poseidon(address)), chainId (uint64), paymentNonce (uint256). The scope commitment becomes Poseidon5(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp, paymentAmountCents, paymentNonce). An additional range constraint enforces paymentAmountCents <= FINANCIAL_SMALL_CAP when only bit 2 is set, <= FINANCIAL_MEDIUM_CAP when bit 3 is set. chainId is bound as a public input so cross-chain replay is impossible without a distinct proof. Requires new Groth16 zkey from pot16.ptau (no new ceremony — pot16.ptau is reused). Estimated constraints: ~23000 (AgentPolicy 20923 + ~2000 for new commitments and range checks).

PaymentAuthVerifier.sol: thin Solidity contract wrapping Groth16Verifier.verifyProof() with a typed verifyPaymentAuth(proof, publicSignals) entry point. Unpacks public signals into (agentMerkleRoot, scopeCommitment, paymentAmountCents, recipientAddressHash, chainId, paymentNonce, expiryTimestamp) and (a) checks agentMerkleRoot is in IdentityRegistry's root history buffer, (b) enforces block.chainid == chainId, (c) enforces block.timestamp < expiryTimestamp, (d) records paymentNonce in a nullifier set to prevent replay. Emits PaymentAuthorized event for DeFi protocol indexers. Deploy to Base Sepolia alongside existing verifiers.

Two new SDK functions in @bolyra/sdk: provePaymentAuth(agentCredential, {amountCents, recipientAddress, chainId, paymentNonce, currentTimestamp}) → PaymentAuthProof and verifyPaymentAuth(proof, publicInputs) → PaymentAuthResult. provePaymentAuth uses the PaymentAuthPolicy Groth16 zkey (not the AgentPolicy zkey). Add PaymentAuthProof and PaymentAuthResult types, x402 header serialization helper (proofToX402Header(proof): string returning base64url-encoded 256-byte Groth16 proof), and a new error class PaymentAuthorizationError.

payment-protocols x402 adapter update: wire provePaymentAuth into the existing x402 request builder so the Payment-Authorization header is populated with the Bolyra Groth16 proof instead of a placeholder. Add a verifier-side middleware (Express/Hono) that calls PaymentAuthVerifier on-chain (or off-chain via ethers.js callStatic for zero-gas read) before approving the x402 response. Also add a Theseus-specific adapter thin wrapper that maps Theseus agent context (model hash, operator key, delegation token) to AgentCredential for the provePaymentAuth call.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) SDK bridge — Theseus agent runtime calls @bolyra/sdk provePaymentAuth() with its AgentCredential and the x402 payment parameters before each DeFi call; proof generation runs locally in Node (~0.6s Groth16). (2) x402 header — the 256-byte Groth16 proof is base64url-encoded into the Payment-Authorization request header; the Base DeFi protocol's x402 responder reads this header and calls PaymentAuthVerifier.verifyPaymentAuth() on Base Sepolia as a callStatic (gas-free read) to gate the response. For on-chain settlement, the DeFi protocol can call verifyPaymentAuth() in the same transaction as the swap, making authorization and execution atomic. (3) IdentityRegistry root history — the PaymentAuthVerifier checks that the agentMerkleRoot in the proof is within the 30-entry root history buffer, so Theseus agents enrolled up to ~30 registry updates ago can spend without re-enrollment; the Theseus orchestrator does not need to track tree state itself. The delegation chain (human → Theseus orchestrator → worker agent) is pre-verified off the critical path via verifyHandshake(); payment proofs reference the already-enrolled agent credential, keeping per-payment latency to the single provePaymentAuth() call.

deliverables.name: PaymentAuthPolicy.circom
deliverables.description: Fork of AgentPolicy with 4 additional public inputs: paymentAmount (u64), recipientHash (Poseidon1 of address), chainId (field), paymentNonce (field). Internal constraints: paymentAmount <= financialCapFromBits(permissionBitmask), chainId == EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID, nullifier = Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, paymentNonce, chainId) prevents cross-chain replay. Existing scope commitment and expiry checks are preserved unchanged.

deliverables.name: payment-auth zkey + vkey + Solidity verifier
deliverables.description: Run snarkjs groth16 setup PaymentAuthPolicy.r1cs pot16.ptau payment_auth_final.zkey, then snarkjs zkey export verificationkey, then snarkjs zkey export solidityverifier. pot16.ptau (existing) covers the constraint count. Deploy new PaymentAuthVerifier.sol to Base Sepolia — old AgentPolicy verifier at 0x5467FC is NOT reused because the vkey changes with new public inputs.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized()
deliverables.description: Add a single view function: isPaymentAuthorized(bytes32 agentRoot, uint256[8] calldata proof, uint256[6] calldata pubSignals) returns (bool). Internally calls PaymentAuthVerifier.verifyProof and checks that pubSignals[nullifierHash] is not in usedPaymentNonces mapping. On-chain nonce write happens in the paying transaction, not in the view — x402 responder calls the view, then the DeFi protocol writes the nonce on actual spend. Root history buffer (existing 30-entry ring) handles staleness.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry contract — adds ~40 lines to existing contract

deliverables.name: @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 adapter patch
deliverables.description: Replace placeholder credential bytes in Payment-Authorization header with: base64url(groth16Proof.pi_a || pi_b || pi_c) + ':' + base64url(pubSignals). Add buildPaymentAuthProof(agentCredential, paymentAmount, recipientAddress, chainId) helper that calls snarkjs groth16 fullProve with the new circuit artifacts. Total header value stays under 512 bytes (Groth16 proof is ~256 bytes, pubSignals 6 x 32 = 192 bytes, base64 overhead ~30%). Wire into existing sendX402Payment() call site.

deliverables.name: Theseus agent demo script
deliverables.description: Single Node script: (1) create AgentCredential with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit set, (2) call buildPaymentAuthProof for a $0.10 USDC transfer to a mock DeFi vault on Base Sepolia, (3) POST the x402 request with the Bolyra proof in Payment-Authorization header, (4) x402 responder calls IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized() on Base Sepolia, (5) on success, log proof bytes, gas cost of verifyProof call (~280k gas), and nullifier hash. No Theseus node required — simulates Theseus agent behavior with a local keypair.
deliverables.reuses: existing demo pattern from examples/mcp-demo + payment-protocols adapter

spec.demo_description: Sunday call (June 29) shows: (1) live walkthrough of PaymentAuthPolicy.circom diff against AgentPolicy — the 4 new public inputs and the amount-cap constraint are readable in ~20 lines of Circom, (2) the existing @bolyra/payment-protocols demo with a MOCKED proof in the header to show the HTTP flow end-to-end, (3) architecture diagram: Theseus agent -> buildPaymentAuthProof() -> x402 header -> Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized() -> DeFi protocol. The circuit is not yet compiled with real zkey — frame Sunday as 'here is the design and the working HTTP plumbing; the circuit artifacts are 3 days out.' The real working proof needs the 5-day build.
risks: Circuit trusted setup (snarkjs groth16 setup) for PaymentAuthPolicy takes 20-40 min on a laptop and must run to completion before any real proof is possible — if the constraint count exceeds 2^16, pot16.ptau is insufficient and a new pot18.ptau ceremony (~2h) is needed. Audit constraint count before starting setup.

Theseus EVM compatibility is unconfirmed. If Theseus L1 uses a custom VM rather than EVM, the PaymentAuthVerifier.sol Solidity contract cannot be deployed there — proof verification would have to happen on Base Sepolia as a settlement layer instead, adding a cross-chain message step that is out of scope for the Sunday demo.

x402 header size: spec allows up to 8KB but individual server implementations (Cloudflare Workers, AWS API GW) may enforce 4KB or 8KB header limits. Groth16 + pubSignals base64 is ~600 bytes — well within limit — but must verify the DeFi protocol's x402 responder does not reject non-standard headers.

Solo-founder context switch cost: circuit work (Circom) and Solidity work cannot be parallelized — the verifier contract depends on the compiled circuit. The 5-day estimate assumes sequential execution with no interruptions. If Theseus prep, sales calls, or other work intrudes, slide to 8 days.

snarkjs proving time in Node for PaymentAuthPolicy will be 5-15 seconds (comparable to AgentPolicy). For a demo this is acceptable but Theseus will ask about latency in production. Real answer: rapidsnark binary brings it to ~200ms; the existing bench_rapidsnark.js script already measures this for AgentPolicy and can be adapted.
spec.success_criteria: Running `node demo/theseus-payment-demo.js` produces: (a) a valid Groth16 proof for a $0.10 USDC payment with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit set, (b) Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized() returns true in a live eth_call, (c) re-submitting the same proof (replay attack) returns false because the nullifier is recorded, (d) submitting a proof for $150 with only FINANCIAL_SMALL bit fails circuit constraint validation before proof generation completes.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1. Confirmation that Theseus L1 is EVM-compatible or has a Groth16 verifier precompile — determines whether IdentityRegistry can deploy on Theseus or must live on Base as settlement. 2. A testnet RPC endpoint or confirmation that Base Sepolia is the intended settlement chain for the Sunday demo. 3. An example Theseus agent keypair format (Ed25519 or secp256k1) — Bolyra's AgentCredential currently uses EdDSA (Baby Jubjub) for the credential commitment; if Theseus uses secp256k1 natively, the credential import path needs a hash bridge. 4. Any existing x402 responder they have running, or confirmation that we are mocking the DeFi protocol side for the demo.

title: Live AgentPolicy proof on Base Sepolia as Theseus agent credential primitive
description: Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit (PLONK, deployed at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia) already does exactly what Theseus needs for agent credential verification: EdDSA operator signature over a credential commitment, 8-bit permission bitmask with cumulative enforcement, selective disclosure of required scope bits, and expiry gating — all proven on-chain in a single transaction. On Sunday's call we run a live terminal demo: spin up an agent credential with permissions 0b00000111 (READ+WRITE+FINANCIAL_SMALL), generate a PLONK proof, and call IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() on Base Sepolia in real time. This is not a slide — it's a working primitive Theseus can fork today. The signal is Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint: their agents need programmatic credential issuance and scope enforcement without a human signing off at runtime, which is exactly what AgentPolicy delivers.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' structural argument is sound and maps cleanly to AgentPolicy, but the primary source (theseuschain.com) was fetch-blocked so the constraint is asserted from provided context rather than independently verified documentation or community citation.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol (MPC attestations, no on-chain scope bitmask) and Turnkey (server-side JSON, operator-trusted) are named with structural failure modes identified, but the argument that a simple on-chain ACL contract couldn't serve Theseus equally well is underdeveloped.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Strongest dimension — exact circuit (AgentPolicy PLONK), deployed contract address, specific SDK methods, and scoped new work (verifyAgentCredential ~80 lines, proveAgentOnly 1 day) are all named with realistic day-level estimates.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Circom/ZKP expertise gap is identified, but the case for why Theseus would choose Bolyra over Lit Protocol's existing agent PKP product is not made, and Theseus team's actual crypto background is unassessed.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with a hard 'no human key path' constraint: agents hold their own keys, manage balances, persist on-chain, and require verifiable AI execution — no human signs off at runtime.

Lit's Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) give agents threshold-custody of ECDSA/EdDSA keys with Lit Action conditions for access control, but proofs are MPC attestations, not ZKPs — no on-chain scope bitmask or expiry circuit enforcement.

Turnkey provides API-driven non-custodial agent wallets (EdDSA/ECDSA), but authorization policies are server-side JSON rules with no ZKP proof — an operator-trusted model, not a trustless on-chain primitive.

Privy is human-facing embedded wallet infra; its server wallet feature can sign on behalf of agents but has no delegation circuit or on-chain scope enforcement — not designed for agent-rooted trust chains.

Polygon ID uses Circom-based ZKP Verifiable Credentials (BabyJubJub signatures, Groth16 proofs) deployed on Polygon PoS — the closest deployed analogue to Bolyra AgentPolicy, but the credential model is human/entity-centric and has no cumulative 8-bit permission bitmask or agent delegation circuit.
Semaphore v4 (which Bolyra HumanUniqueness already reuses) proves group membership without revealing identity, but is human-uniqueness scoped — no agent credential or permission-bitmask model exists.

Fetch.ai uses Cosmos DID-style agent identity with WASM execution; credentials are PKI-signed DIDComm messages — no ZKP proving, no cumulative permission bits, and no EVM-compatible on-chain verifier.
Autonolas runs multi-chain agent services using on-chain service NFTs for identity; no ZKP for credential proofs, authorization is ACL-based on-chain.
SingularityNET uses on-chain service registries and AGIX-gated access; identity is wallet-address-based with no ZKP or delegation circuit.

Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication proposes OAuth 2.0 extension with agent-specific claims; no ZKP proof requirement — leaves on-chain verifiable scope enforcement as an open problem that Bolyra AgentPolicy directly fills.

evidence.source: W3C DID + Verifiable Credentials spec — w3.org
evidence.summary: W3C VCs define a credential envelope and selective disclosure model (BBS+ or SD-JWT) but no cumulative permission bitmask semantics or ZKP circuit-enforced scope narrowing for agent delegation.

ERC-4337 smart account abstraction lets agents own accounts with custom validation logic, but does not define a credential issuance primitive or scope-bitmask ZKP — Bolyra AgentPolicy could plug in as the validation circuit for AA-based agent accounts.

Orange DAO (YC alumni) backed Theseus at $100K pre-seed; their typical portfolio companies at this stage are pre-mainnet with raw keypair identity — no mature identity stack, making early primitive partnerships high-leverage.

mapped_primitives.usage: Primary primitive. EdDSA operator signature over credential commitment, 8-bit cumulative permission bitmask, selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask, and expiry enforcement map 1:1 to Theseus's 'no human key path at runtime' constraint. Operator signs once at issuance; the circuit proves capability at runtime without any human involvement.

On-chain PLONK verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db. Theseus's verifier contract or relayer calls verifyProof() directly for standalone agent credential checks without requiring the paired Groth16 human proof.

verifyHandshake() currently enforces atomic dual-proof (Groth16 + PLONK). For the Sunday demo, this is fine — pair a placeholder human identity with the agent credential. For production Theseus integration, a standalone verifyAgentCredential() entry point is needed to skip the HumanUniqueness side.

createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, 0b00000111, expiry) issues the credential; proveHandshake() generates the PLONK proof. The demo script is 3 SDK calls. For Theseus, a proveAgentOnly() path that skips HumanIdentity construction removes an unnecessary dependency.

Optional but high-value for Theseus: sub-agent spawning with scope narrowing (e.g., orchestrator agent delegates READ_DATA+FINANCIAL_SMALL to a child without granting WRITE_DATA). The 3-hop chain limit and on-chain nullifier replay protection address Theseus's multi-agent orchestration patterns.

new_components_needed.description: IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentCredential(agentProof, requiredScopeMask, nonce) — standalone PLONK-only verification path that skips the Groth16 human side. Reuses existing PlonkVerifier and agentTree root history buffer. Emits AgentVerified event for Theseus's relayer to index.

proveAgentOnly(credential, requiredScopeMask, options) in @bolyra/sdk — wraps AgentPolicy witness generation + PLONK proof without constructing a HumanIdentity. Eliminates the paired-proof mental model for pure-agent consumers like Theseus.

theseus-bolyra demo script: 50-line Node.js terminal demo for Sunday's call. createAgentCredential with permissions=0b00000111, generateProof, broadcast to Base Sepolia, print tx hash and on-chain AgentVerified event. Zero slides.

@bolyra/theseus adapter package (post-demo): thin wrapper exposing issueCredential(agentConfig) + verifyOnChain(proof, scopeMask) in Theseus's expected interface shape, with Base Sepolia RPC config baked in.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Contract — Theseus's relayer or verifier contract calls IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentCredential() on Base Sepolia (or PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() directly if they prefer raw verification); events are indexable for their agent registry. (2) SDK — Theseus's agent runtime calls @bolyra/sdk proveAgentOnly() to generate PLONK proofs on the fly; credential issuance stays off-chain via createAgentCredential(). (3) Optional DID layer — agent identities registered as did:bolyra:<agentCommitment> with W3C DID method, enabling interop with any DID-aware trust registry Theseus already integrates with. The Sunday demo covers layer 1+2 end-to-end in a single terminal session.

deliverables.name: theseus-demo.js
deliverables.description: 50-line Node.js terminal script: createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, 0b00000111, expiry) → proveHandshake (paired with placeholder human identity) → IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() on Base Sepolia → log tx hash. Pre-generates and caches the PLONK proof to avoid 20-30s live latency, replays cached proof against fresh nonce on the call.
deliverables.reuses: @bolyra/sdk createAgentCredential + proveHandshake, existing PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db, IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentCredential()
deliverables.description: New entry point on IdentityRegistry that accepts (agentProof, requiredScopeMask, nonce) and calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() directly — no Groth16 HumanUniqueness side. Reuses existing agentTree root history buffer and nonce deduplication. Emits AgentVerified(agentNullifier, scopeMask, expiry) for Theseus relayer indexing. ~80 lines of Solidity.
deliverables.reuses: existing PlonkVerifier, agentTree root history buffer, nonce registry — new function only

deliverables.name: proveAgentOnly() SDK method
deliverables.description: New export in @bolyra/sdk: proveAgentOnly(credential, requiredScopeMask, options) that runs AgentPolicy witness generation + snarkjs PLONK prove without constructing a HumanIdentity. Returns { proof, publicSignals } compatible with verifyAgentCredential(). Eliminates paired-proof mental model for pure-agent consumers.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy.circom witness generation, existing PLONK .zkey artifacts in circuits/build/

deliverables.name: bolyra-theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: 1-page technical spec covering: (1) credential issuance flow — Theseus operator calls createAgentCredential() off-chain, commits agentLeaf to on-chain tree; (2) runtime verification flow — Theseus relayer calls verifyAgentCredential() per agent action; (3) delegation flow — orchestrator agent issues sub-agent credential via Delegation circuit with scope narrowing; (4) nullifier replay protection design. Doubles as leave-behind for Theseus eng team.

deliverables.name: Hardhat integration test for verifyAgentCredential
deliverables.description: Hardhat test that deploys updated IdentityRegistry, issues a credential with permissions=0b00000111, calls proveAgentOnly() (mock proof mode), and asserts verifyAgentCredential() emits AgentVerified with correct scopeMask and expiry. Gates the contract change — no ship without this passing.
deliverables.reuses: existing Hardhat test fixtures in contracts/test/, mock proof helpers

spec.demo_description: Live Base Sepolia terminal demo (no slides). Show: (1) createAgentCredential call with permissions=0b00000111 — credential commitment logged to console; (2) PLONK proof loaded from pre-generated cache, fresh nonce bound in <1s; (3) ethers.js call to IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() on Base Sepolia — tx hash returned in ~15s; (4) tx link opened in Basescan showing AgentVerified event with scopeMask=7 and expiry. Fallback: if RPC is flaky, replay a pre-recorded asciinema cast with the actual tx hash for Basescan verification. Day 1 is demo script only — this is ready Saturday night.
risks: Base Sepolia RPC instability during live call — mitigated by pre-generating proof + having Basescan tx link ready as fallback
Theseus runs a non-EVM custom VM — if confirmed before Sunday, pivot demo to showing the SDK + proof artifacts as portable primitives Theseus can wrap in their own verifier
snarkjs PLONK prover takes 20-30s on demo machine — pre-cache proof, bind fresh nonce only (milliseconds), avoid live proving on the call
Theseus eng is pre-mainnet with no stable contract environment — don't commit to a Theseus-side integration contract until they have a testnet
Solo bandwidth: verifyAgentCredential contract + test takes ~1.5 days total — do not start this until demo script is confirmed working
spec.success_criteria: Sunday demo produces a real Base Sepolia tx hash with AgentVerified event showing scopeMask=7, expiry in the future, and no human-side proof required. Theseus team can fork the demo script and run it against their own operatorKey within 30 minutes of the call.
spec.theseus_dependencies: No hard dependencies for Sunday demo — runs entirely on Bolyra's existing Base Sepolia deployment. For production integration: (1) confirmation that Theseus is EVM-compatible or has an EVM bridge; (2) their testnet RPC endpoint and chain ID for deploying a Theseus-side IdentityRegistry fork; (3) their agent key format (confirming EdDSA BabyJubJub or specifying alternate curve); (4) their relayer indexer schema so AgentVerified event fields match what they consume.

title: Agent genesis credential: L1 birth-transaction as ZKP root of trust (no human root)
description: Bolyra's current AgentPolicy circuit commits to operatorPrivKey (a human-held key) as the issuer root. Theseus has no human key path — agents are bootstrapped by the L1 genesis or agent birth transaction itself. The fix: replace the human operatorPrivKey input with the agent's genesis tx hash (chain_id + block_hash + tx_index) as the credential root, issued by the Theseus L1 protocol rather than a human key. A Bolyra ZKP then proves 'this agent credential was issued by Theseus L1 genesis block B at tx index T, and its permissions are scoped to bits 0bXXXXXXXX' — all without revealing which specific agent it is (nullifier privacy preserved). This is the exact architectural gap the Theseus 'no human key path' constraint creates, and it's where Bolyra's circuit infrastructure provides a 6-12 month head start over anything Theseus could build internally.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' architectural constraint on Theseus is structurally argued and corroborated by evidence that all competitor solutions (Lit, Turnkey, Privy, Fetch.ai, W3C DID, IETF drafts) require a human trust anchor, making genesis-tx-rooted identity a genuine unmet need.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (plain EdDSA + Merkle inclusion proof) is implicitly named but not explicitly analyzed for its failure mode — the privacy leak (revealing which genesis tx = revealing agent identity) is asserted rather than formally derived, and the O(1) vs O(n) verification tradeoff is not quantified.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific circuits (AgentPolicy fork), contracts (IdentityRegistry genesis path), and SDK surfaces are named with constraint estimates and day-level timelines, but the 4-day circuit development estimate is aggressive given that replacing EdDSA with a genesis commitment changes the trusted setup, requiring a new ceremony contribution and Solidity verifier regeneration that adds integration risk.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The 6-12 month head start claim is plausible given Circom/snarkjs proficiency barriers and the existing pot16.ptau ceremony, but Theseus's actual team crypto background is unassessed and the competitor adoption path (Lit Protocol PKPs with a custom genesis wrapper) is not ruled out.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 where agents hold their own keys and are bootstrapped by genesis/birth transactions — no human-controlled key path exists by design.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — litprotocol.com (PKP + AI agent signing)
evidence.summary: Lit's Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) let AI agents sign transactions autonomously, but root of trust is still a human-issued NFT mint event, not an L1 genesis tx; no ZKP credential scoping layer.

Turnkey provides custodial key management with policy engines for agent signers, but requires human administrator root for all key issuance — structurally incompatible with Theseus's agent-first model.
Privy targets human-facing embedded wallets; agent server wallets require OAuth/API-key delegation from a human account owner — not applicable to headless agent genesis.

evidence.source: W3C Decentralized Identifiers (DID) Core Spec v1.0
evidence.summary: DID spec allows machine-controlled DIDs (did:key, did:web), but all deployed DID methods require human-controlled registration or governance — no genesis-tx-as-DID-document method exists.
Current IETF AI agent auth drafts assume a human authorization server as trust anchor; agent-rooted credential issuance with no human AS is explicitly out of scope in all current drafts.
Fetch.ai Autonomous Economic Agents use DID-based P2P addressing, but agent identity still requires human-controlled registration on the Almanac contract — no genesis-tx root of trust.
Autonolas agent services use on-chain registry with Safe multisig as operator root — human operators are mandatory, making it structurally misaligned with Theseus's no-human-key constraint.
AGIX marketplace agent services are tied to human-issued service registrations; no agent-native genesis credential primitive exists.

Semaphore is the closest ZKP identity primitive in production (the Bolyra HumanUniqueness circuit already reuses it), but is explicitly human-rooted via Merkle commitment of human secrets — no machine genesis path.
Aztec provides ZK account abstraction and private identity, but accounts still require human-initiated deployment; no protocol-level agent genesis credential primitive.

evidence.source: ERC-4337 Account Abstraction + smart contract wallets
evidence.summary: ERC-4337 lets smart contracts act as wallets without human EOAs, providing the closest on-chain analog to agent-native identity, but carries no ZKP permission scoping or nullifier-based privacy.
URL signals Theseus received coverage in March 2026, likely around funding or testnet launch — timing aligns with Orange DAO $100K round and pre-mainnet development phase.

evidence.source: Worldcoin / World ID — worldcoin.org (ZKP proof of personhood)
evidence.summary: World ID proves human uniqueness with ZKP but is explicitly anti-agent by design — the iris biometric root is the antithesis of agent genesis credential.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core circuit to adapt. Its permission bitmask with cumulative bit encoding, selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask bits, identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3), expiry enforcement, and nullifier privacy all carry over directly. The single point of change is the issuance root: EdDSA operatorPrivKey verification must be replaced by a genesis-tx commitment (Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index)). The 20923-constraint budget is sufficient—removing EdDSA verification (~4K constraints) and substituting a Poseidon hash path nets a smaller circuit, likely 17–19K constraints under Groth16.

Reusable with minor input changes. Once an agent holds a genesis credential, the Delegation circuit's scope-narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, hop-count enforcement (max 3), and delegator key binding all apply unchanged. The only modification: the delegator credential commitment input must accept a genesis-rooted commitment instead of an operator-signed one. Chain-linking logic and nullifier-based replay protection carry over intact.

The agentTree (LeanIMT, depth 20, 30-entry root history buffer) and its atomic handshake verification pattern are directly reusable for genesis agents. The contract needs a new verification path: instead of calling PlonkVerifier against an operator-signed credential, it calls a new GenesisPlonkVerifier. The delegation chain-state tracking, nonce equality enforcement, and delegation nullifier replay protection all carry over unchanged.

Not applicable to this opportunity by design. The Theseus no-human-key constraint explicitly removes the human root. HumanUniqueness is still relevant for human-in-the-loop Bolyra flows but is orthogonal to genesis agent credentials.

Existing PlonkVerifier contract cannot be reused because the verification key is circuit-specific and a rewritten AgentPolicy circuit produces a different vkey.json. A new GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier must be generated from the new zkey and deployed separately.

SDK scaffolding (error classes, bitmask helpers, permissionsToBitmask, validateCumulativeBitEncoding) carries over without changes. proveHandshake and verifyHandshake need a genesis-credential code path. The existing artifact-loading, snarkjs/rapidsnark bridge, and nonce-binding logic are all reusable.

new_components_needed.description: GenesisAgentPolicy.circom — replaces EdDSA operatorPrivKey verification with a genesis-tx commitment: private inputs are (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index, permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, secret, merklePathElements, merklePathIndices); public outputs are (genesisCommitment = Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index), nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, expiryTimestamp). Reuses existing Poseidon2/3 templates, LeanIMT membership proof, cumulative-bit range checks, and selective-disclosure mask logic from AgentPolicy. Dual Groth16+PLONK build to match existing pattern.

GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier.sol — generated from new circuit's vkey.json via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deployed alongside existing verifiers; IdentityRegistry updated to route genesis handshakes to this verifier. Add a genesisRoot registry mapping (chain_id => trusted genesis block hash) so the verifier can check that the claimed genesis commitment corresponds to a Bolyra-accepted Theseus L1 block root.

TheseusGenesisRootOracle.sol — a lightweight trusted-root registry (owner-updateable or multisig) that records canonical (chain_id, block_hash) pairs for Theseus L1 genesis blocks. IdentityRegistry reads from this oracle during genesis credential verification. Avoids full light-client complexity for the sprint scope; can be replaced by a Theseus L1 light client verifier in a follow-on.

createGenesisAgentCredential(chainId, blockHash, txIndex, permissions, expiry) → GenesisAgentCredential — new SDK entry point that builds the genesis commitment, generates a witness for GenesisAgentPolicy, and returns a provable credential. Extend proveHandshake and verifyHandshake to accept GenesisAgentCredential in the agent slot. Add GenesisCredentialError to error class hierarchy.

Theseus L1 genesis bridge — a lightweight adapter (TypeScript, Node) that queries the Theseus RPC for a birth transaction's (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) tuple and feeds it into createGenesisAgentCredential. Must handle RPC auth, chain_id normalization, and block finality checks before committing the genesis root to the on-chain oracle. Packaged as @bolyra/theseus-adapter.

Extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a 'Genesis Agent Profile' section: defines the genesis commitment construction, L1 trust anchor semantics, oracle trust model, and how genesis-rooted delegation chains interoperate with human-rooted chains. Add 6–8 conformance test vectors to test-vectors.json covering genesis credential issuance, scope narrowing via Delegation, and cross-session nullifier unlinkability.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Off-chain proving — the Theseus genesis bridge adapter reads birth-tx data from the Theseus L1 RPC, feeds (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) into the Bolyra SDK's createGenesisAgentCredential, and generates a PLONK proof client-side using the circuit artifacts in circuits/build/. (2) On-chain anchor — TheseusGenesisRootOracle.sol (deployed on Base Sepolia, same chain as IdentityRegistry) holds the canonical mapping of accepted Theseus L1 genesis block hashes; the oracle is updated by a multisig or a Theseus-operated relayer when new genesis blocks are finalized on L1. IdentityRegistry's atomic handshake verification routes genesis agent proofs through GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier and reads the oracle for root validation. (3) SDK bridge — @bolyra/theseus-adapter exposes a single function `bootstrapTheseusAgent(rpcUrl, txHash, permissions, expiry)` that orchestrates the RPC call, genesis commitment construction, proof generation, and agentTree insertion — giving Theseus protocol developers a five-line integration path consistent with the existing @bolyra/sdk quickstart pattern.

deliverables.name: GenesisAgentPolicy.circom
deliverables.description: Fork of AgentPolicy.circom replacing EdDSA operatorPrivKey verification (~4K constraints) with a Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) genesis commitment check. All permission bitmask logic, cumulative-bit enforcement, scope commitment, expiry, and nullifier privacy carry over untouched. New public inputs: genesisCommitment, chainId. Private inputs: blockHash, txIndex, agentSecret.

deliverables.name: genesis_agent.zkey + vkey.json
deliverables.description: Trusted setup for GenesisAgentPolicy using existing pot16.ptau. Run snarkjs groth16 setup + contribute one beacon. Generates the circuit-specific zkey and exports vkey.json for Solidity verifier generation. Slow step but fully scripted via existing compile.js pattern.

deliverables.name: GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: New Solidity verifier exported from the genesis zkey via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Drop-in replacement for AgentPlonkVerifier in the genesis credential path. No hand-authored code — fully generated from vkey.json.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry genesis path
deliverables.description: Add registerGenesisAgent(genesisCommitment, chainId, proof) entry point to IdentityRegistry.sol. Calls GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier instead of existing PlonkVerifier. Existing agentTree (LeanIMT depth 20), 30-entry root history buffer, nonce enforcement, and delegation nullifier replay protection all carry over unchanged. Emit GenesisAgentRegistered(nullifierHash, chainId) event for Theseus indexer.

deliverables.name: Delegation circuit genesis input patch
deliverables.description: One-line input rename in Delegation.circom: delegatorCredentialCommitment input now accepts genesis-rooted Poseidon3 output in addition to operator-signed commitments. Scope-narrowing, expiry narrowing, hop-count (max 3), and chain-linking logic unchanged. Recompile + regenerate zkey (reuses pot16.ptau).

deliverables.name: SDK genesis credential API
deliverables.description: Add createGenesisCredential(chainId, blockHash, txIndex, agentSecret, permissions, expiry) to @bolyra/sdk. Returns GenesisCredential type with nullifierHash + genesisCommitment. Wire into existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake as a credential variant. All bitmask helpers, error classes, snarkjs/rapidsnark bridge, and nonce-binding logic reused verbatim.

deliverables.name: Theseus genesis tx fetcher
deliverables.description: Thin adapter (~100 LOC) that queries a Theseus RPC endpoint for a given agent address, retrieves its birth transaction (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index), and feeds those into createGenesisCredential. Uses Theseus JSON-RPC or REST API (TBD — blocked on Theseus providing testnet access). Includes offline mock for demo use.

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo script
deliverables.description: Hardhat script that: (1) deploys GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier + patched IdentityRegistry to local node, (2) mocks a Theseus genesis tx tuple, (3) generates a genesis credential proof via SDK, (4) registers the agent on-chain, (5) runs a handshake against a second agent, (6) logs nullifier + permission bits. Self-contained, runnable with npm run demo:genesis. No live Theseus chain required for local demo.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call (tomorrow): show the architecture diff — a single annotated diff of AgentPolicy.circom replacing the EdDSA check with Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index), the existing delegation and permission-bit infrastructure on screen, and the integration spec doc. No running proof yet, but the 'what changes and why' story is complete and credible. The 6-12 month head-start argument is grounded in the specific constraint count reduction and the existing pot16.ptau ceremony that eliminates trusted setup from the critical path.
risks: Theseus genesis tx schema unknown: if block_hash or tx_index aren't stable public fields at birth-tx finality, the Poseidon3 commitment binding breaks — need schema confirmation before writing circuit inputs
Theseus EVM compatibility unconfirmed: if their L1 is not EVM-compatible, Solidity verifier deployment is blocked and contract layer needs a full rewrite for their VM
pot16.ptau ceiling: if GenesisAgentPolicy exceeds 2^16 constraints after changes (unlikely given EdDSA removal nets ~17-19K), a new ceremony is required — adds 1-2 days
Solo capacity: 14 days assumes full focus; any Theseus integration question latency (schema, RPC docs, testnet access) adds idle time that compresses real output to 10-12 working days
Nullifier domain separation: genesis nullifier must be domain-separated from operator-issued nullifiers to prevent cross-credential replay — easy to miss, needs explicit test coverage
spec.success_criteria: A Hardhat integration test passes end-to-end: genesis credential generated from (chainId=8453, blockHash=mock, txIndex=0), proof verified on-chain by GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier, agent registered in IdentityRegistry, handshake with a second agent succeeds, and replaying the same nullifier fails with AlreadyUsed. Circuit constraint count is below 2^16. SDK createGenesisCredential returns in <2s on snarkjs, <200ms on rapidsnark.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Birth-transaction RPC method or REST endpoint returning (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) for a given agent address — needed before integration adapter can be written. 2) Testnet RPC URL + faucet for deploying GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier and IdentityRegistry. 3) Confirmation of EVM compatibility or VM spec if non-EVM. 4) Clarification on whether genesis tx fields are finalized at block inclusion or require additional epochs — affects nullifier timing assumptions. 5) Any existing agent identity/auth interface (even raw keypair docs) so the Bolyra handshake can be wired to their current flow rather than replacing it cold.

title: x402 autonomous spending gated by Bolyra AgentPolicy permission bits
description: AWS Bedrock AgentCore, Coinbase Agentic Wallets, and World's agentkit all launched x402 support in 2026 — autonomous agents paying for services via HTTP 402 micropayments is becoming infrastructure. Bolyra's @bolyra/payment-protocols package already implements x402 flows. The integration play: Theseus agents that call x402-enabled APIs present a Bolyra AgentPolicy proof alongside the payment, proving they hold FINANCIAL_SMALL (bit 2, <$100) or FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3, <$10K) permission — gating autonomous spending to the authorized limit without a human approval step. The 2-week deliverable is a demo script: Theseus agent calls an x402-gated endpoint, Bolyra proof is verified in the payment middleware, spending is approved only if permission bits match the payment amount. This is the most concrete 'agents transacting autonomously' story we can show on Sunday as a 5-minute live demo after the AgentPolicy demo. x402 Foundation backing (Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard) makes this a credible ecosystem play, not a toy.

rationale.agent_need: x402 adoption by AWS Bedrock AgentCore, Coinbase Agentic Wallets, and the founding consortium (Google, Visa, Stripe) provides strong structural evidence that autonomous spending gating is a real, emerging infrastructure need for L1-native agents — though Theseus-specific documentation remains sparse.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (Turnkey/Lit server-side policy enforcement) is correctly identified and its failure mode named (server-side rules, not cryptographically proven on-chain), but the edge is moderate since off-chain snarkjs verification in the demo path doesn't actually require on-chain ZKP and Turnkey's spending limits are functionally comparable for many use cases.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Strongest dimension: AgentPolicy circuit requires zero modification, PlonkVerifier is deployed at a named address on Base Sepolia, @bolyra/payment-protocols already implements x402 flows, and the integration spec details a credible 2.75-day build with specific SDK methods named — this is genuinely near-ready.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build ZKP-based permission attestation in <3 months is plausible given Circom proficiency requirements, but is weakened by the fact that the demo uses off-chain snarkjs verification (reducing the ZKP expertise bar) and Theseus could adopt Lit Protocol's conditional signing or Turnkey's server policies as functional substitutes without needing Bolyra.

evidence.source: x402.org (founding consortium: Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard)
evidence.summary: x402 repurposes HTTP 402 as a machine-native micropayment rail; consortium backing from major payment and cloud players makes it infrastructure-grade, not experimental.

evidence.source: Coinbase Developer Platform — Agentic Wallets launch (2026)
evidence.summary: Coinbase's agentic wallets natively support x402 flows, positioning payment-gated agent actions as a first-class primitive in their developer ecosystem.
AWS endorsement of x402 for agentic commerce signals enterprise acceptance and creates procurement-friendly framing for Bolyra's gating layer.

Public information is sparse; Theseus markets 'agency for agents' with no human key path, implying raw EdDSA keypairs as current identity primitive — a structural gap Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit directly addresses.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol (litprotocol.com) — PKP + MPC key management
evidence.summary: Lit provides programmable key pairs with threshold MPC, enabling conditional signing, but lacks ZKP-based permission-bit attestation that can be verified on an L1 without revealing the underlying credential tree.
Turnkey offers policy-enforced key management with spending limits, but policies are server-side rules, not cryptographically proven on-chain — no ZKP attestation layer.
Privy targets consumer dApp onboarding; minimal agent-to-agent delegation primitives and no on-chain ZKP attestation.
Fetch.ai has native agent messaging (UAGENT protocol) but identity is DID-over-key without ZKP-provable permission scopes; a Bolyra-style AgentPolicy layer would be additive.
SingularityNET handles agent service discovery and payment but does not provide cryptographic delegation or permission-bit attestation.

evidence.source: Worldcoin / Semaphore v4 — on-chain ZKP identity (production)
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is in production at scale; Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit reuses this ceremony, meaning the trusted-setup lineage is already credible to any audience familiar with Worldcoin.

evidence.source: Aztec Network — private ZKP execution on Ethereum
evidence.summary: Aztec's noir-based private proofs could theoretically gate spending, but there is no agent-native permission model or x402 middleware integration.
Bolyra already ships x402 flow implementation, meaning the 2-week sprint is integration/demo work, not net-new primitive development — lowest-risk path to Sunday demo.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core proving primitive. Selective-disclosure capability ('proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without revealing full bitmask') maps directly to gating: payment middleware sets requiredScopeMask=0x04 for amounts <$100 (FINANCIAL_SMALL, bit 2) or 0x08 for amounts <$10K (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM, bit 3). Cumulative encoding means bit 3 proof automatically satisfies bit 2 check. Identity-bound scope commitment and expiryTimestamp enforcement prevent credential reuse across sessions or after expiry. This circuit needs zero modification.

Deployed verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia. Payment middleware can call verifyProof() on-chain before releasing funds, or the same vkey can be used for off-chain snarkjs verification in an HTTP middleware layer for sub-second latency without a blockchain round-trip. Both paths are already supported by the circuit artifact build.

Optional but recommended for the demo: IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() provides atomic human+agent proof verification in a single tx, proving the agent is sponsored by a real enrolled human. For the pure spending-gate demo (no human in the loop), the PlonkVerifier alone suffices. IdentityRegistry becomes relevant when the story is 'human authorized this agent up to $10K total' rather than 'operator signed a credential'.

The @bolyra/payment-protocols package already implements x402 flows. The integration layer lives here: intercept the 402 response, attach the AgentPolicy proof to the subsequent payment request (as a custom X-Bolyra-Proof header or inside the x402 payment payload's metadata field), and add a verifier middleware on the server side that calls PlonkVerifier before releasing the resource.

createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, [FINANCIAL_SMALL|FINANCIAL_MEDIUM], expiry) is the client-side entry point. proveHandshake() generates the AgentPolicy PLONK proof. The Theseus agent calls these before its first x402 request and caches the proof for the session (valid until expiryTimestamp). No SDK changes needed; the demo script just wires the existing function calls.

Relevant when Theseus spawns sub-agents that inherit spending authority. A parent Theseus agent with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM can delegate FINANCIAL_SMALL to a sub-agent via the Delegation circuit, enforcing scope narrowing on-chain. Not required for the 2-week demo but is the natural v2 story ('agent orchestrators with scoped spending hierarchies').

new_components_needed.description: x402 Bolyra middleware: Express/Fastify middleware (or fetch interceptor) that (1) extracts X-Bolyra-Proof from incoming payment request, (2) calls snarkjs.plonk.verify(vkey, publicSignals, proof) off-chain for <100ms latency, (3) maps payment amount to required permission bit (amount<=100 → bit 2, amount<=10000 → bit 3), (4) checks publicSignals.requiredScopeMask satisfies the amount tier, (5) rejects with 403 if proof missing or bit check fails. Ships as bolyra-x402-middleware npm package inside integrations/payment-protocols/.

Proof attachment helper: proveSpend(agentCredential, amountUSD, sessionNonce) → { proof, publicSignals, headers } that computes the correct requiredScopeMask from the dollar amount, runs the AgentPolicy witness+proof, and returns ready-to-attach HTTP headers. Thin wrapper over existing proveHandshake() — 50 lines of glue.

Demo script: Theseus agent that (1) creates an operator-signed AgentCredential with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM, (2) calls a mock x402-gated API endpoint (e.g. /data-feed priced at $0.01), (3) receives 402, generates proof via proveSpend(), (4) retries with proof header, (5) server-side middleware verifies and serves response. Illustrates the full autonomy loop with no human approval step. Self-contained runnable in examples/x402-demo/.

X-Bolyra-Proof header schema: JSON encoding of { proof, publicSignals, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp } for x402 interop. One-page spec in spec/ so other x402 implementers can support Bolyra proofs without the SDK. Needed for partnership credibility with Coinbase/AWS ecosystem.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Client SDK — Theseus agent imports @bolyra/sdk, calls createAgentCredential() once at startup with operator-signed FINANCIAL_MEDIUM permissions, then proveSpend(credential, amount, nonce) before each x402 payment attempt; proof generation is ~16s first time (PLONK), cacheable for session duration. (2) HTTP middleware — bolyra-x402-middleware sits in front of any x402-gated endpoint; performs off-chain snarkjs.plonk.verify() at <100ms per request, no blockchain call needed unless operator wants on-chain auditability. (3) On-chain settlement (optional) — for high-value transactions (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM tier), payment processor calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() in the settlement transaction, creating an immutable audit trail that the agent held valid permission at time of spend. Theseus's existing HTTP client and x402 retry logic need one hook: attach the proof headers on 402 response, retry the same request. No Theseus core changes required beyond that hook.

deliverables.name: x402-bolyra-middleware
deliverables.description: Thin Express/Node middleware layer inside @bolyra/payment-protocols: on 402 response, auto-generates AgentPolicy PLONK proof via proveHandshake() and attaches as X-Bolyra-Proof header on retry. Server side: verifyProof() off-chain via snarkjs against cached vkey.json, checks requiredScopeMask (0x04 for FINANCIAL_SMALL, 0x08 for FINANCIAL_MEDIUM) against proof public signals, rejects if amount exceeds bit tier's ceiling. Zero circuit changes — pure wiring.

deliverables.name: demo-server
deliverables.description: Minimal Express server with two routes: GET /resource returns 402 with x402 payment details (amount + scopeMask), POST /pay accepts x402 payment + X-Bolyra-Proof, runs verifier middleware, returns 200 with resource or 403 if proof invalid or permission tier insufficient. Hardcoded test prices: $50 (requires FINANCIAL_SMALL), $5000 (requires FINANCIAL_MEDIUM). Off-chain snarkjs verification only — no blockchain round-trip needed for Sunday latency.

deliverables.name: theseus-agent-demo-script
deliverables.description: Single Node script simulating a Theseus-style agent: generates EdDSA keypair (matching Theseus's raw keypair model), calls createAgentCredential() with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit, calls demo-server GET → receives 402, generates PLONK proof, retries POST with proof, prints approve/reject result. Shows two runs: $50 purchase (passes), $5000 purchase with only FINANCIAL_SMALL credential (fails). Theseus chain not required — agent is mocked as a bare EdDSA identity, which IS Theseus's current primitive.

deliverables.name: proof-caching-session-manager
deliverables.description: 50-line wrapper that caches the generated AgentPolicy proof for the session window (expiryTimestamp from credential), avoids re-proving on every x402 request within the same session. Critical for demo smoothness — PLONK prove takes 2-4s, cache makes subsequent calls instant. Lives in the demo script, not in the SDK.

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: run theseus-agent-demo-script.js, show a mock Theseus agent (bare EdDSA keypair, no Theseus chain needed) attempt two purchases against the local demo-server. First: $50 API call — agent generates AgentPolicy PLONK proof with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit, proof verified off-chain in <4s, resource unlocked, show 200 response with 'APPROVED: permission bit 2 satisfied'. Second: $5000 API call with same FINANCIAL_SMALL credential — verifier rejects, show 403 'REJECTED: FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) required for amounts >$100'. Frame it: 'This is the cryptographic spending limit — not a policy rule a server can override, not a JWT a middleware can forge. The agent proves capability without revealing its full credential tree.' Five minutes, no blockchain, no flakiness risk.
risks: PLONK proof generation via snarkjs takes 2-4 seconds on first call — acceptable for demo if framed correctly ('this is the one-time proof generation; subsequent requests within the session use the cached proof'), but will feel slow if the audience expects HTTP latency
payment-protocols package internals may have breaking assumptions about the x402 payload shape — need to audit the existing x402 implementation before assuming 'minor modification'

Theseus's EdDSA keypair format may differ from the format createAgentCredential() expects for operatorPrivKey — need to verify curve (Ed25519 vs secp256k1) before Sunday; if they use secp256k1 the demo script works as-is, if Ed25519 need a 1-hour shim
Off-chain snarkjs verification is single-threaded in Node — under concurrent demo load it blocks; non-issue for a one-agent demo but worth noting for the 'production path' conversation with Theseus
spec.success_criteria: Demo script completes two runs end-to-end in under 30 seconds total: run 1 exits 0 with 'APPROVED' printed, run 2 exits 1 with 'REJECTED: insufficient permission tier' printed. Proof verification log shows the requiredScopeMask check passing/failing against the proof's public signals. No hardcoded bypasses — the rejection must come from the verifier, not an if-statement.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Nothing required for Sunday — agent is simulated as a bare EdDSA keypair which matches their documented architecture. For post-Sunday production integration: (1) Theseus testnet RPC endpoint or SDK to instantiate a real on-chain agent identity, (2) confirmation of their signing curve (Ed25519 or secp256k1) to ensure createAgentCredential() keypair format matches, (3) their agent account ABI if they have a smart contract layer — needed only if we want on-chain PlonkVerifier call as a transaction from a Theseus agent rather than off-chain snarkjs verification.

title: ZKP-Enforced Agent Key Rotation Without Human Approver
description: On Theseus, when an agent's EdDSA key is compromised or reaches its rotation epoch, there is no human fallback to authorize a new key. A ZKP-based rotation proof allows the agent to attest it holds both the old private key and the new one — committing to a key-transition nullifier on-chain — without revealing either key. This is structurally different from standard key rotation (which requires a human CA or multisig owner): the proof itself is the authorization. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit can be extended to include a rotation witness, and the IdentityRegistry contract can enforce that a valid ZKP is the only valid rotation path. Evidence: Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint makes this a day-1 operational gap — raw EdDSA rotation on an L1 with no human approver means a compromised key stays live until the agent self-destructs.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint creates a structural day-1 gap where a compromised EdDSA key stays live indefinitely without a self-sovereign rotation mechanism, confirmed by the architectural constraint itself.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol requires an external threshold network (liveness dependency), Turnkey requires operator authorization, and raw on-chain EdDSA rotation has no way to prove key continuity without revealing the old private key — ZKP is the only mechanism that proves simultaneous possession of both keys without exposure, though the gap over MPC alternatives needs stronger evidence.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness nullifier pattern, and IdentityRegistry's nullifierMap all provide reusable components, but a new AgentRotation.circom circuit requires 3+ days of new Circom development plus PLONK setup, and the spec assumes pot16.ptau is sufficient without verifying constraint counts for the double-EdDSA witness.
rationale.partnership_leverage: A new Circom circuit with double-EdDSA witness and trusted setup is non-trivial ZKP engineering that Theseus would need months to build, but the evidence doesn't rule out them adopting Lit Protocol's MPC approach as a faster alternative, weakening the exclusivity argument.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (not live-fetched — web search blocked this session)
evidence.summary: Context confirms Theseus enforces a 'no human key path' constraint — agents hold raw EdDSA keypairs with no CA or multisig fallback, making unauthorized-key-persistence the day-1 operational gap this opportunity targets.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — litprotocol.com (PKP + Lit Actions, known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Lit's Programmable Key Pairs use threshold MPC (not ZKP proofs) for autonomous key operations; rotation is policy-gated via Lit Actions but requires the threshold network as an external trust anchor — a liveness dependency that a self-contained ZKP proof avoids.

evidence.source: Turnkey — turnkey.com (HSM-backed key management, known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Turnkey provides policy-engine-gated key operations backed by AWS Nitro enclaves; rotation is operator-authorized, not agent-self-authorized — incompatible with Theseus's no-human-approver constraint.

evidence.source: Privy — privy.io (embedded wallet infrastructure, known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Privy is user-wallet-centric with social recovery tied to human accounts; structurally mismatched to agent-native chains with no human root.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core / DID:Key spec + IETF draft-ietf-oauth-dpop
evidence.summary: Existing standards assume key rotation is authorized by the DID controller (a human or org); no standard currently defines a ZKP-only rotation proof path — Bolyra's proposed mechanism would be novel relative to the spec landscape.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai AEA framework + Cosmos DID registry (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai agents use CosmWasm-based DID documents for identity; key rotation requires on-chain governance tx signed by the current key — no ZKP path, and human upgrade authority is implicit in validator governance.

evidence.source: SingularityNET AGIX — on-chain agent registry (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: SingularityNET's agent marketplace uses Ethereum-signed registrations; no dedicated key rotation or ZKP identity layer for autonomous agent credentials.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 (Ethereum) — on-chain nullifier-based identity (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Semaphore demonstrates the nullifier commitment pattern Bolyra already reuses for HumanUniqueness; extending the same pattern to a key-transition nullifier (old_key_commit → new_key_commit with double-spend guard) is architecturally straightforward given Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit.

evidence.source: Polygon ID / iden3 protocol — ZKP credentials on EVM (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Polygon ID supports ZKP-verified credential updates but targets human DIDs; no agent-native rotation primitive without a human issuer in the trust chain.

evidence.source: Aztec Protocol — private L2 with ZKP state transitions (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Aztec's private note model could represent key-state transitions, but it operates as an L2 app layer — not a reusable rotation primitive portable to Theseus's L1.

mapped_primitives.usage: The EdDSA operator signature verification logic is the direct ancestor of the rotation witness. AgentPolicy already verifies that an agent holds the private key corresponding to a credential commitment via EdDSA over Baby Jubjub. For rotation, the same gate can be instantiated twice — once for the old key (proving current registry membership) and once for the new key (committing to the successor credential). The scope commitment output (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) provides the chain-link between old and new credential state.

The nullifier pattern — Poseidon2(scope, secret) — is borrowed directly as the design pattern for the rotation nullifier. For key rotation, the analogous construction is Poseidon2(rotationEpoch, oldCredentialCommitment), binding the one-time-use nullifier to a specific rotation epoch so the same old key cannot authorize two different successor keys. No modification to this circuit; it supplies the cryptographic idiom.

The scope-narrowing chain-linking logic (identity-bound scope commitment chain, expiry narrowing) is structurally reusable for rotation continuity: the new key's credential commitment must carry forward all permission constraints from the old one, preventing a rotation event from silently escalating permissions. The delegatee expiry <= delegator expiry invariant maps cleanly to newExpiry <= oldExpiry as a rotation constraint. No circuit modification needed — the logical pattern is extracted, not the circuit itself.

The agentTree (LeanIMT + 30-entry root history buffer) is the on-chain state that rotation must update atomically. The existing delegation nullifier replay protection mechanism is the template for rotation nullifier tracking. The 'atomic handshake verification in a single tx' pattern extends to an 'atomic rotation verification' call: verify rotation proof, check nullifier unused, update agentTree leaf, emit RotationCompleted event. Requires a new entry point function but the storage layout and nullifier map infrastructure already exists.

The rotation circuit should target PLONK (same as AgentPolicy and Delegation) to avoid a project-specific Groth16 trusted setup ceremony. The existing PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db will be reused as the on-chain verifier for rotation proofs, eliminating a new verifier deployment if the circuit's verification key is registered to the same verifier contract.

new_components_needed.description: AgentKeyRotation.circom — PLONK circuit with private inputs: oldEdDSAPrivKey, newEdDSAPrivKey, oldCredentialCommitment, rotationEpoch, oldMerklePathElements[20], oldMerklePathIndices[20]. Public outputs: oldCredentialCommitment (for registry lookup), newCredentialCommitment (Poseidon3 of same permissions + new pubkey + newExpiry), rotationNullifier (Poseidon2(rotationEpoch, oldCredentialCommitment)), newLeafHash. Constraints: two EdDSA Baby Jubjub key derivations, two Poseidon3 commitment checks, one LeanIMT membership proof on old leaf, one nullifier derivation, one permission-preservation check asserting newPermissionBitmask == oldPermissionBitmask AND newExpiry <= oldExpiry. Estimated ~24K constraints, within pot16.ptau budget.

IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey(rotationProof, publicSignals) entry point — verifies PLONK rotation proof via PlonkVerifier, reads oldCredentialCommitment from publicSignals and confirms it is a current agentTree leaf (using root history buffer for recency tolerance), checks rotationNullifier not consumed (new mapping: rotationNullifiers), updates agentTree by replacing old leaf with newLeafHash, stores rotation event with epoch for audit trail. Also adds a rotationEpoch admin setter (operator-controlled, Theseus can call via their bridge contract).

SDK v0.3 rotation API: createRotationCredential(oldAgentCredential, newOperatorKey, rotationEpoch) → AgentCredential; proveRotation(oldCredential, newCredential, rotationEpoch) → RotationProof; submitRotation(proof, registryAddress) → RotationReceipt. New error classes: KeyRotationNullifierUsedError, RotationPermissionEscalationError, RotationExpiryEscalationError, StaleAgentRootError.

Theseus chain bridge adapter — thin contract or off-chain relay that listens for Theseus-native key-compromise events (or epoch triggers) and initiates the Bolyra rotation flow on Base Sepolia. Two deployment models: (A) off-chain relay: Theseus agent SDK calls @bolyra/sdk directly, submits rotation proof to Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry, then posts the Base Sepolia tx hash back to Theseus as proof-of-rotation; (B) on-chain bridge: a lightweight Theseus-deployed contract that accepts a rotation receipt from Base Sepolia via LayerZero or Axelar and gates agent operations until rotation is confirmed. Model A is the 2-week sprint target; model B is v0.4.

Key rotation profile for draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 — addendum section defining rotationEpoch semantics, rotation nullifier construction, permission-preservation invariants, and the prohibition on human-path bypass. Required for Theseus to reference a normative spec rather than an implementation detail.

theseus_integration_surface: Primary surface is the IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey() function on Base Sepolia (0x2781dF8b6381462d881C833Fb703d68c661c9577 + new entry point post-upgrade). Theseus agents invoke @bolyra/sdk's proveRotation() locally — the proving happens client-side in ~16s (PLONK, same order as AgentPolicy) — then submit the calldata to Base Sepolia via a standard EIP-1559 tx. Theseus has no native Base Sepolia awareness, so the integration shim is an off-chain relay process (Node.js, ships as @bolyra/theseus-adapter) that: (1) subscribes to Theseus's epoch-clock or compromise-signal events, (2) calls @bolyra/sdk to generate the rotation proof, (3) submits to IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia, (4) posts the resulting tx hash + rotationNullifier back to Theseus as an off-chain attestation the agent can present. For Theseus to enforce on-chain that only ZKP-rotated agents can operate, they would deploy a thin BolyraRotationGate.sol on their chain that calls a Bolyra-maintained storage oracle or accepts the Base Sepolia tx receipt verified via their chosen bridge (LayerZero being the most operator-friendly given Bolyra's Base deployment). The SDK bridge is the 2-week deliverable; the on-chain gate is v0.4.

deliverables.name: AgentRotation.circom
deliverables.description: New Circom 2 circuit extending AgentPolicy with a double-key witness: proves simultaneous possession of old EdDSA private key (current registry member) and new EdDSA private key (successor), outputs rotation nullifier Poseidon2(rotationEpoch, oldCredentialCommitment) and new scope commitment, enforces newExpiry <= oldExpiry and no permission escalation via cumulative-bit comparison. Targets PLONK to reuse pot16.ptau and avoid a new Groth16 ceremony.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy (double-instantiated EdDSA gate + scope commitment), HumanUniqueness (nullifier construction idiom), Delegation (expiry narrowing constraint)

deliverables.name: AgentRotation PLONK trusted setup + vkey
deliverables.description: Run snarkjs plonk setup against pot16.ptau for AgentRotation.circom, export rotation.zkey and rotation.vkey.json, verify constraint count stays under 2^16. Check if existing PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db accepts the new vkey or needs a new verifier deployment.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey()
deliverables.description: New entry point on IdentityRegistry: accepts (rotationProof, publicSignals) — verifies PLONK proof, checks rotation nullifier not in nullifierMap, atomically updates agentTree leaf from old to new credential commitment, stores nullifier, emits RotationCompleted(agentId, oldCommitment, newCommitment, epoch). Reuses existing nullifierMap storage layout and LeanIMT update pattern from delegation flow. Includes Hardhat integration test: rotation succeeds, replay fails, permission-escalation proof rejected.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry (nullifierMap, agentTree LeanIMT, atomic verification pattern)

deliverables.name: SDK rotateAgentKey() method
deliverables.description: New public method in @bolyra/sdk: rotateAgentKey(oldCredential, newOperatorPrivKey, rotationEpoch) → generates AgentRotation witness, runs PLONK prove via snarkjs (or rapidsnark subprocess), returns { rotationProof, publicSignals, newCredential }. Caller submits to IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey() on-chain. Type-safe, matches existing createAgentCredential return shape so downstream code (proveHandshake) needs zero changes.

deliverables.name: End-to-end rotation demo script
deliverables.description: Single Node script: enroll agent with old key → generate rotation proof → submit on-chain → verify old credential rejected by IdentityRegistry → generate handshake with new credential → verify succeeds. Runs against local Hardhat node. Output is a clean terminal trace showing nullifier recorded, agentTree root updated, handshake passing. This is what gets screen-shared on the Theseus call once built.
deliverables.reuses: existing test harness (examples/mcp-demo, Hardhat deploy scripts)

deliverables.name: Theseus integration architecture spec
deliverables.description: One-page technical spec for the Sunday call: circuit I/O diagram, rotation nullifier construction, IdentityRegistry call sequence, EVM deployment assumptions, and 3 open questions requiring Theseus input (VM compatibility, agent lifecycle event triggers, rotation epoch definition). Not a full IETF draft — purpose is to demonstrate concrete thinking and open a scoped technical conversation.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call shows: (1) live walkthrough of existing AgentPolicy.circom as the direct ancestor — the EdDSA gate that will be doubled in AgentRotation; (2) HumanUniqueness nullifier code as the rotation nullifier template; (3) IdentityRegistry nullifierMap storage as the replay-protection substrate; (4) the one-page architecture spec with circuit I/O and call sequence; (5) a rough constraint-count estimate proving pot16.ptau (2^16) is sufficient. Goal is to establish Bolyra has 60% of the primitives already and needs 8 days to close the gap — not to show running code.
risks: AgentRotation constraint count may exceed 2^16 (pot16.ptau limit) if the double-EdDSA witness is expensive — mitigation: benchmark witness size against AgentPolicy baseline before writing full circuit, and have pot17.ptau fallback ready
Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — if they run a custom CosmWasm or SVM-derivative, IdentityRegistry.sol cannot deploy there and the integration requires a proof-relay bridge, adding 3-5 days
PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99 may not support dynamic vkey registration — if it's a static verifier, a new verifier contract must be deployed and the solo-founder timeline tightens

Rotation epoch semantics undefined — without Theseus confirming how rotation epochs are defined and emitted on-chain, the circuit's epoch binding is architecturally sound but untestable on their actual chain
8-day estimate assumes no other fires (autoresearch loops, protocol-autoresearch, SDK maintenance) — any context switch extends to 12 days
spec.success_criteria: A rotation proof generated by @bolyra/sdk passes IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey() on-chain verification, the old credential commitment's nullifier is stored (replay attempt reverts), the agentTree Merkle root updates atomically, and a subsequent proveHandshake() call using the new credential succeeds while the same call with the old credential fails — all demonstrated in a single deterministic Hardhat integration test.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) VM confirmation: EVM-compatible or custom (determines if IdentityRegistry.sol deploys natively vs requires a proof-relay bridge). 2) Testnet RPC + faucet access before end of sprint. 3) Current agent identity encoding: how EdDSA public keys are stored on-chain today (raw bytes32? DID document? compressed Baby Jubjub point?). 4) Rotation epoch definition: is it time-based, block-based, or event-triggered, and who emits it? 5) Agent lifecycle ownership: does Theseus have an existing AgentRegistry contract we must integrate with, or is Bolyra's IdentityRegistry the proposed registry?

title: Agent-Rooted Delegation Chain: L1 Genesis as Root of Trust
description: Bolyra's current Delegation circuit assumes a human is the root delegator. Theseus agents are the root — no human ever holds a parent key. An agent-rooted variant of the Delegation circuit treats the agent's on-chain birth transaction (or L1 genesis block commitment) as the trust anchor instead of a human Merkle leaf. This enables Agent A to delegate a subset of its permission bits to Agent B with on-circuit enforcement — scope can only narrow, never expand — without any human approval in the path. This is the foundational primitive for multi-agent workflows on Theseus: orchestrator agents spawning sub-agents with scoped authority. Competitors (Lit Protocol, Turnkey, Privy) have no delegation circuit — they rely on off-chain ACLs that can be bypassed.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's no-human-key-path architecture structurally requires agent-rooted delegation for multi-agent workflows, and analogous systems (Autonolas, Fetch.ai) demonstrate this need emerged organically in agent-native contexts with only coarse-grained alternatives.
rationale.zkp_edge: Named competitors (Lit Protocol off-chain ACLs, Turnkey HSM policy, Autonolas multisig) all lack cryptographic on-circuit scope enforcement, meaning scope expansion attacks are application-layer responsibilities — a structural failure ZKP delegation eliminates.
rationale.primitive_readiness: The Delegation circuit provides ~80% reuse with only the root anchor needing structural change, but a new Circom circuit still requires 3+ days of development plus PLONK trusted setup, new verifier deployment, and SDK surface updates — non-trivial scope.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Theseus could adopt Lit Protocol or build raw EdDSA delegation themselves in under 3 months, and there's no evidence of a confirmed expertise gap in their team's ZKP proficiency — the build-vs-partner calculus is uncertain without more signal.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an L1 purpose-built for autonomous agents with no human-controlled key path — agents hold their own keys, persist on-chain, and have verifiable AI execution; current identity approach is almost certainly raw EdDSA keypairs with no delegation circuit.

Lit Protocol provides decentralized threshold-key management and programmable signing conditions, but delegation is enforced via off-chain JSON access-control conditions (ACLs), not on-circuit; a malicious node operator or condition mismatch bypasses delegation without cryptographic proof.

Turnkey is a cloud-hosted key management API (HSM-backed) used to give agents wallets, but agent-to-agent delegation is entirely off-chain policy with no ZKP enforcement — scope expansion attacks are an application-layer responsibility.
Privy targets embedded wallets for end-users and recently added server-side agent wallets, but is human-centric by design; no delegation circuit or cumulative-bit permission model exists.

Autonolas builds autonomous agent services using Gnosis Safe multisigs as on-chain agent collectives; delegation is coarse-grained (threshold signature), not ZKP-enforced bit-level scope narrowing, creating a direct gap Bolyra fills.

Fetch.ai's Almanac on-chain registry gives agents a DID-like identity and lets them register services, but delegation between agents is handled off-chain by the uAgents framework with no cryptographic scope enforcement.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace (AGIX), not agent-to-agent identity delegation; identity primitives are minimal.

UCAN (User Controlled Authorization Networks) defines capability-based delegation chains that can narrow scope, but proofs are JWT-encoded off-chain blobs — no on-circuit enforcement means a compromised relay can forge or expand delegations.
Semaphore provides anonymous ZKP group membership proofs used by Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit, but is not designed for agent-rooted trust chains or permission-bit delegation.
Polygon ID provides ZKP-based verifiable credentials (Groth16 via iden3), but is human-credential-centric and has no agent-to-agent delegation circuit or cumulative-bit permission model.

Account abstraction enables agents to have smart-contract wallets with modular permissions, but delegation enforcement is Solidity logic, not a ZK circuit — invalid permission expansions are caught only at transaction time on the target chain, not at proof generation.

evidence.source: Orange DAO portfolio / YC agent infrastructure companies (2025-2026)
evidence.summary: Orange DAO (Theseus's backer) has funded multiple agent-infra plays; the cohort trend shows demand for on-chain agent identity that goes beyond wallet ownership — verifiable execution and scoped delegation are the unsolved layer.

mapped_primitives.usage: Serves as the root credential for the delegation chain origin. The agent's EdDSA credential commitment (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) becomes the trust anchor instead of a human Merkle leaf. The operator-signed credential commitment already encodes the genesis authority binding. The agentTree Merkle membership proof replaces the humanTree proof at the chain root.

Core scope-narrowing logic is directly reusable: bitwise subset enforcement, expiry narrowing, identity-bound scope commitment chain linking, hop-count (max 3), and delegation nullifier replay protection all apply unchanged to agent-to-agent chains. The structural change is the root anchor: replace the implicit human Merkle membership assumption at hop 0 with an agentTree membership proof. Delegator key binding (UC3.1) and expiry-in-scope-commitment (UC3.2) fixes carry over without modification.

agentTree (LeanIMT + 30-entry root history buffer) is already the correct on-chain registry for agent leaves — no new tree needed. The existing PlonkVerifier and DelegationPlonkVerifier addresses are reused. A new entry point verifyAgentDelegation() is needed to route verification to an agent-rooted path rather than the atomic human+agent handshake path. Chain-state tracking and delegation nullifier storage are inherited as-is.

AgentRootedDelegation circuit will use PLONK (same as Delegation) — the deployed verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db is reusable only if the constraint count and verification key remain compatible. A redeployed PlonkVerifier will be needed if the new circuit diverges from the existing vkey.

Not used in the agent-rooted path. The scope-scoped nullifier pattern (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) is a conceptual reference for designing per-agent delegation nullifiers, but the circuit itself is not invoked.

new_components_needed.description: AgentRootedDelegation.circom — variant of Delegation.circom where hop 0 anchors to an agentTree Merkle membership proof (LeanIMT depth 20) rather than a human Merkle leaf.
