Uses @bolyra/sdk subprocess bridge. Meant to be the 'copy-paste quickstart' Theseus devs hand to agent builders.

spec.demo_description: Run the Base Sepolia Live Demo Script live on the call: show Agent A (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM) delegating to B (READ_DATA+WRITE_DATA) delegating to C (READ_DATA), print on-chain verifyProof=true for each hop, then attempt to forge C claiming FINANCIAL_MEDIUM — verifyProof=false. No new circuit work needed. Pair with the one-page architecture spec showing how this maps to Theseus L1. The ask: Theseus testnet RPC access + 30-min technical session with Eric to confirm EVM compatibility.
risks: Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — if custom VM, TheseusRegistryAdapter needs a full rewrite; the AgentRootedDelegation circuit output is still usable but the on-chain verifier layer is unknown. Validate this on Sunday before writing circuit code.

AgentRootedDelegation circuit: replacing humanNullifier with agentCredentialHash changes the Merkle inclusion proof wiring in CIP-1 — may surface constraint-count surprises that push 5-day estimate to 8. Run constraint count check (snarkjs r1cs info) after first compile before committing.

Solo bandwidth: 12.5 days assumes no context-switching to GeniusComply or ZKProva. Realistic throughput is ~8 focused days in a 2-week sprint, so circuit work may slip to week 3 unless Theseus testnet access is confirmed fast.

Theseus testnet stability unknown — if RPC is flaky or faucet-gated, verifier deployment and integration testing block. Mitigate: run integration tests against Base Sepolia with a TheseusRegistryAdapter mock until Theseus testnet is stable.
spec.success_criteria: Agent C can submit a PLONK proof to TheseusRegistryAdapter.verifyChain() that returns true only when C's claimed scope is a strict subset of A's original credential scope, and the call reverts (or returns false) when C claims any bit A never held — confirmed by a passing Hardhat test and a live Theseus testnet transaction hash.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Theseus testnet RPC endpoint + chain ID (needed before verifier deployment). 2) Confirmation that Theseus L1 is EVM-compatible and supports Solidity verifiers (pairing-library gas budget matters — BN254 precompiles at standard EIP-197 addresses). 3) Testnet faucet or funded deployer key. 4) 30-min async technical session with Eric to confirm execution environment before AgentRootedDelegation circuit work starts — prevents wasted circuit days if the VM requires a different proof system.

title: Authorization proof layer atop Theseus verifiable AI execution
description: Theseus advertises 'verifiable AI execution' — proving WHAT an agent computed. But verifiable execution without verifiable authorization is an incomplete trust model: you can prove an agent ran model M and produced output O, but not that it was authorized to act on behalf of entity E with permission scope S. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit closes this gap by binding the execution proof to a scope commitment: Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp). The composition is: Theseus execution attestation anchors to a Bolyra scope commitment, so on-chain verifiers can check both claims atomically. No new circuit — the existing AgentPolicy selective disclosure output (requiredScopeMask) is the binding point. New work is a Solidity adapter (est. 2 days) that wraps IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake with Theseus's execution attestation hash.

rationale.agent_need: The authorization gap is structurally real — verifiable execution without scope binding is an incomplete trust model — but evidence of Theseus specifically lacking this and agents on Theseus actively needing it comes from absence-of-documentation inference rather than direct citations from Theseus materials or community demand signals.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (EdDSA signature + ACL contract) is named implicitly but not rigorously compared; the key ZKP advantage — selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask without revealing full bitmask — is genuine but the atomic on-chain composition benefit could be approximated with a trusted attestation registry, so the structural failure of alternatives is understated.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Exact primitives are named (AgentPolicy circuit, PlonkVerifier at specific address, IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake), modification scope is minimal (thin adapter + one overload + SDK additive option), and the 3.5-day estimate is realistic and well-decomposed.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this in <3 months is asserted without evidence of their team's ZKP capability or whether they have an identity solution in progress; the competitor alternatives (Lit Protocol MPC, Turnkey TEE attestation) are identified but the case that they are meaningfully worse for Theseus's specific architecture is not closed.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with verifiable AI execution but no documented identity/auth layer beyond raw keypairs — the authorization proof gap is confirmed by the absence of any scope-binding primitive in public materials.

Lit offers MPC-based programmable key management for agents with condition-based access control, but relies on threshold signing rather than ZKP scope commitments — no zero-knowledge selective disclosure of permission scope.

Turnkey provides secure enclave key management for agents (AWS Nitro TEE), enabling hardware-attested signing but no on-chain authorization scope proof — attestation proves execution environment, not permission boundary.
Privy targets embedded wallets for human users with social recovery; minimal agent-native support and no ZKP delegation — not a direct competitor in the agent-rooted trust chain space.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + VC Data Model 2.0
evidence.summary: W3C DIDs and Verifiable Credentials define agent identity descriptors but provide no native ZKP scope-commitment binding to on-chain execution proofs — the atomic composition Bolyra proposes is not covered by existing VC spec.
Emerging IETF work on AI agent authentication acknowledges the authorization gap but proposes OAuth-style token delegation rather than ZKP-based scope commitments — no on-chain verifiability.
Fetch.ai uses DID-based agent identity on its Cosmos-based chain but relies on centralized registration without ZKP privacy or cumulative-bit scope enforcement — permission model is coarse-grained.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace payments rather than agent authorization proofs — no delegation circuit or scope-binding primitive.

Semaphore v4 ceremony (depth-20 Groth16) is the closest deployed ZKP identity primitive on EVM chains, but it proves group membership for humans only — no agent policy or scope-commitment binding to execution attestations.

Aztec's privacy L2 provides ZKP-native smart contracts but is EVM-adjacent, not agent-native L1 — no off-the-shelf AgentPolicy circuit or Poseidon3 scope-commitment adapter for external chain integration.
Aleo builds a ZKP-native L1 with private state, but Leo language circuits are Aleo-specific and not portable as Solidity adapters to EVM-compatible agent chains like Theseus.

ERC-4337 account abstraction and ERC-7579 modular accounts allow permission modules on EVM chains, but no ZKP scope-commitment primitive exists — Bolyra's Solidity adapter would be the first such module.

mapped_primitives.usage: The scope commitment Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) is the exact binding point. The circuit's requiredScopeMask selective disclosure output proves the agent had permission S without revealing the full bitmask. Theseus's execution attestation hash gets anchored to this commitment — an on-chain verifier can check 'agent had scope S AND computed output O' atomically. The expiry enforcement and EdDSA operator signature verification already cover the credential lifecycle.

verifyHandshake is the on-chain anchor. The Theseus adapter wraps this call: it passes the existing (humanProof, agentProof, nonce) tuple plus the Theseus execution attestation hash, verifies the Bolyra handshake first, then checks attestation hash inclusion in the same transaction. The existing nonce equality enforcement and delegation nullifier replay protection carry over.
AgentPolicy uses PLONK. The deployed PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db handles proof verification without modification. The adapter contract calls through to this verifier.

If Theseus execution involves a sub-agent acting under delegated authority (e.g., human → orchestrator → worker), the Delegation circuit's composable chain (max 3 hops) and scope-narrowing guarantee extend the authorization proof through the delegation chain. The delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope invariant ensures scope cannot escalate at any hop. Optional for v1 but directly applicable for multi-agent Theseus pipelines.

proveHandshake(human, agent, options) accepts an options object — the Theseus attestation hash slots in as an additional binding value mixed into the sessionNonce (or passed as a separate public input to the adapter). createAgentCredential covers credential issuance before the Theseus run. No new SDK functions needed; options extension is additive.

new_components_needed.description: TheseusAuthAdapter.sol — thin Solidity adapter that calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake then validates that the Theseus execution attestation hash (keccak256 of model hash + output commitment) matches the scope commitment bound in the AgentPolicy proof's public inputs. Emits AuthorizedExecution(agentId, scopeMask, attestationHash, blockNumber). No new verifier logic — delegates to existing PlonkVerifier and Groth16Verifier.

Add proveHandshake options.attestationHash: Hex field. When set, the SDK mixes it into the sessionNonce derivation so the proof is bound to the specific Theseus execution. Also add verifyHandshakeWithAttestation(humanProof, agentProof, nonce, attestationHash) → HandshakeResult that calls TheseusAuthAdapter instead of IdentityRegistry directly.

Theseus composition spec — one-page document defining the canonical attestation hash format (what Theseus must commit to: modelHash, inputHash, outputHash, agentId, timestamp), the nonce binding scheme, and the on-chain verification flow. Needed to make the integration portable across Theseus versions and auditable.

theseus-bolyra integration package — minimal TypeScript wrapper that takes a Theseus execution receipt, extracts the attestation hash, and calls @bolyra/sdk's verifyHandshakeWithAttestation. Analogous to langchain-bolyra-tool pattern already in integrations/.

theseus_integration_surface: On-chain: TheseusAuthAdapter.sol deployed on Base Sepolia calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake (existing) then asserts attestationHash matches the public scope commitment output from the AgentPolicy PLONK proof — single transaction, atomic authorization + execution verification. Off-chain SDK bridge: proveHandshake() accepts Theseus's execution receipt hash in options, mixes it into sessionNonce so the ZK proof binds to that specific execution. Theseus's own verifier (whatever chain they run on) can read the TheseusAuthAdapter's AuthorizedExecution event as a cross-chain attestation that authorization was valid at execution time. No oracle, no bridge — the proof is self-contained in the calldata.

deliverables.name: TheseusAuthAdapter.sol
deliverables.description: Thin Solidity adapter: calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake, then checks that keccak256(modelHash, outputCommitment) matches the attestationHash public input bound in the AgentPolicy proof. Emits AuthorizedExecution(agentId, scopeMask, attestationHash, blockNumber). No new verifier logic — delegates to existing PlonkVerifier.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.sol patch
deliverables.description: Add verifyHandshakeWithAttestation(humanProof, agentProof, nonce, attestationHash) overload. Existing verifyHandshake remains unchanged. The attestationHash is passed through to TheseusAuthAdapter — no new verification logic in the registry itself.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/sdk attestation binding
deliverables.description: Extend proveHandshake(human, agent, options) to accept options.attestationHash. When present, mix it into sessionNonce as keccak256(sessionNonce, attestationHash) before witness generation. Additive — no breaking change to existing callers.

deliverables.name: Integration test suite
deliverables.description: Hardhat test: (1) issue AgentPolicy credential with permissionBitmask, (2) simulate Theseus execution attestation as keccak256(modelHash, outputCommitment), (3) call TheseusAuthAdapter.verifyAuthorizedExecution, (4) assert AuthorizedExecution event with correct scopeMask and attestationHash. Uses mock Theseus attestation until Theseus testnet is available.
deliverables.reuses: existing Hardhat test scaffolding, AgentPolicy circuit artifacts

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: One-page spec for the Theseus team: attestation hash format (keccak256 input schema), adapter ABI, required public inputs from their execution environment, and how to call TheseusAuthAdapter from a Theseus agent's on-chain action. Reference implementation pointer to the integration test.

deliverables.name: Sunday call demo artifact
deliverables.description: Single-page visual: scope commitment binding diagram (AgentPolicy scope commitment → Theseus attestation anchor → on-chain atomic check), plus terminal output of the integration test passing against a mock attestation. Pre-built; no live proving needed on the call.
deliverables.reuses: existing AgentPolicy circuit, existing test output

spec.demo_description: Show the scope commitment binding diagram (5 min), then run the Hardhat integration test live (mock Theseus attestation): one on-chain transaction that calls TheseusAuthAdapter.verifyAuthorizedExecution, emits AuthorizedExecution event with scopeMask and attestationHash, passes. Demonstrates the full trust model — agent had scope S AND computed output O — atomically verified. No live ZK proving needed; use pre-generated proof artifacts from CI.
risks: Theseus execution environment may not be EVM-compatible — if it uses a custom VM, the Solidity adapter is irrelevant and the integration point becomes off-chain attestation verification instead. Confirm EVM compatibility in the Sunday call before writing any Solidity.

Theseus attestation hash format is unknown — the adapter assumes keccak256(modelHash, outputCommitment) but Theseus may use a different schema (Pedersen hash, Poseidon, raw ECDSA sig). Get their attestation spec before finalizing the adapter.
Theseus testnet may not exist yet — if they are pre-testnet, integration tests stay mocked and the deliverable is a deployable contract + spec rather than a live deployment.
Solo-founder time: 4.5 days assumes no interruptions. TheseusAuthAdapter.sol is the highest-value artifact — prioritize it first and treat the SDK extension as optional for the Sunday call.
AgentPolicy PLONK proof generation takes ~8s per proof on a MacBook — if the Sunday demo runs live proving, it may feel slow. Use pre-generated proof artifacts instead.
spec.success_criteria: A single on-chain transaction on Base Sepolia (or Theseus testnet once available) that: (1) verifies a valid Bolyra AgentPolicy proof binding permissionBitmask to credentialCommitment and expiryTimestamp, (2) checks that the Theseus execution attestation hash matches the scope commitment public input, and (3) emits AuthorizedExecution(agentId, scopeMask, attestationHash, blockNumber) — all in one atomically-reverting call. TheseusAuthAdapter integration test must pass in CI with FULL_PROOF=1.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Need from Theseus before finalizing adapter: (1) EVM compatibility confirmation or custom VM ABI, (2) attestation hash schema — exact inputs to keccak256 or alternative hash function used for execution attestation, (3) testnet RPC endpoint and faucet access, (4) any existing agent identity/keypair format (EdDSA curve, key derivation path) so AgentPolicy EdDSA operator signature verification aligns. These are blocking only for the production adapter — the Sunday demo works with mocks.

title: Nullifier-Bound Session Nonces to Block Agent Transaction Replay Attacks
description: Agents on Theseus execute high-frequency autonomous transactions — authorization proofs issued at time T can be replayed at T+N if the receiving contract or counterparty agent does not enforce per-session uniqueness. Unlike human systems where replay surfaces are limited by human interaction speed, agents can replay thousands of stale authorizations per second. Bolyra's handshake protocol already binds every proof to a fresh sessionNonce and enforces nullifier uniqueness on-chain — replaying (humanProof, agentProof) without rebinding the nonce fails verification by design. Porting this nullifier registry to Theseus L1 closes a structural replay surface that raw EdDSA signatures cannot address (a valid signature is valid forever unless explicitly revoked).

rationale.agent_need: High-frequency autonomous agent transactions create a real replay surface absent from human systems, and x402 agentic commerce evidence confirms authorization replay is a live concern, though direct Theseus documentation showing they lack nullifier protection is inferred rather than cited.
rationale.zkp_edge: The structural argument that EdDSA signatures are valid forever without explicit revocation is correct, but nonce counters at the wallet layer (Privy pattern) or simple on-chain nonce mappings achieve replay prevention without ZKP — the ZKP advantage here is unlinkable nullifiers for privacy, not replay prevention per se, which weakens the edge claim.
rationale.primitive_readiness: IdentityRegistry, AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, and Delegation circuits all map directly with no or minor modifications, the deploy script is estimated at 0.5 days reusing existing artifacts, and the SDK chain config is a one-file edit — highest confidence dimension with concrete named primitives and realistic timelines.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The expertise gap argument (Circom proficiency, trusted setup) is plausible but unverified against Theseus's team composition, and competitors Lit Protocol and Turnkey could offer simpler signature-based alternatives that sidestep ZKP entirely, making the partnership value proposition dependent on privacy requirements Theseus may not have.

evidence.summary: Theseus is a $100K Orange DAO-backed L1 purpose-built for autonomous AI agents with no human-controlled key path, almost certainly relying on raw EdDSA keypairs with no native nullifier/replay protection.
AWS x402 agentic commerce blog highlights autonomous agent payment flows where replay of authorization headers is a live attack surface — no nullifier mechanism is present in x402 by default.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — programmable key pairs (PKP) for agent signing
evidence.summary: Lit PKPs provide threshold-ECDSA agent keys and access control conditions but offer no ZKP nullifier registry; valid signatures remain valid forever unless Lit node network explicitly revokes.

evidence.source: Turnkey — secure MPC key management for wallets/agents
evidence.summary: Turnkey manages EdDSA/ECDSA agent keys via MPC enclaves; authorization tokens are signature-bound but not nullifier-bound, so replayed tokens within TTL windows are indistinguishable from fresh ones.

evidence.source: Privy — embedded wallets and agent signers
evidence.summary: Privy focuses on human-facing embedded wallets; agent signer capability is nascent and has no replay-prevention primitive beyond standard nonce counters at the wallet layer.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 — ZKP identity with nullifier registry (Ethereum)
evidence.summary: Semaphore's on-chain nullifier registry is the closest deployed analog to what Bolyra proposes; Bolyra already reuses its Groth16 ceremony, making a Theseus port directly feasible without new trusted setup.

evidence.source: Polygon ID — ZKP credentials on EVM chains
evidence.summary: Polygon ID uses ZKP-based credential proofs on EVM but is human-identity-centric with no agent-native session nonce binding or high-frequency replay prevention design.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / Autonolas — agent-native L1 identity patterns
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai and Autonolas use agent DIDs with basic signature verification; neither implements nullifier-based per-session uniqueness, leaving replay surfaces open in high-frequency multi-agent coordination.

evidence.source: IETF draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens + agent auth working groups
evidence.summary: Transaction tokens proposal introduces per-request binding but is OAuth/HTTP layer — no cryptographic nullifier commitment; does not prevent replay at the smart contract or L1 execution layer.

evidence.source: W3C Decentralized Identifiers (DID) spec — agent DID methods
evidence.summary: DID spec has no provisions for session nonce binding or nullifier uniqueness; agent DIDs inherit the same 'valid signature = valid forever' weakness as raw EdDSA.

evidence.source: EIP-2612 permit / account abstraction (EIP-4337) nonce handling
evidence.summary: EVM account abstraction handles sequential nonces for transaction ordering but not ZKP-bound per-session nullifiers; an agent with a compromised replay window can exhaust multi-call permits.

evidence.source: Bolyra CLAUDE.md — handshake nonce binding pattern
evidence.summary: Bolyra's sessionNonce binding is already implemented and circuit-enforced; porting the nullifier registry contract to an EVM-compatible Theseus chain is days of work, not months — primitive_readiness score of 22/25 is credible.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core replay-prevention infrastructure already exists: nonce equality enforcement prevents (humanProof, agentProof) reuse, delegation nullifier replay protection blocks stale delegation chains, root history buffers (30 entries) accommodate Theseus block latency. This contract is the direct analog of the 'nullifier registry' the opportunity calls for. Deploying it to Theseus L1 is the primary integration act.

Expiry enforcement (expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp) and identity-bound scope commitment directly prevent authorization proofs issued at T from being valid at T+N beyond their stated window. The selective-disclosure bitmask means counterparty agents learn only required permissions, not the full credential — reducing the value of replayed proofs even if nullifier check were bypassed.

Session nonce binding is a first-class output: the circuit commits to sessionNonce as a public signal, so any replayed (humanProof, nonce) pair with a stale nonce fails on-chain without requiring application-level state. Poseidon2(scope, secret) nullifier is unlinkable across scopes, preventing cross-session correlation attacks specific to high-frequency agent environments.

Delegation nullifier replay protection (already in IdentityRegistry) closes the sub-agent replay surface: a delegated authorization cannot be replayed after use or after the delegator revokes. Expiry narrowing (delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry) bounds the replay window for the entire chain, not just the leaf credential. Current-time liveness check enforces this at proof-time.
Needs to be deployed to Theseus L1 as a standalone contract. Verifies HumanUniqueness proofs on-chain — the cryptographic anchor for the nullifier check.
Needs to be deployed to Theseus L1. Verifies AgentPolicy and Delegation proofs. PLONK's universal SRS means no Theseus-specific trusted setup ceremony is required.
Needs to be deployed to Theseus L1 alongside PlonkVerifier for delegation chain verification with hop-count enforcement.

proveHandshake already generates a fresh sessionNonce and binds both proofs to it. verifyHandshake already enforces nonce equality. The only change needed is wiring the Theseus L1 chain ID and RPC endpoint into the SDK's chain config so it submits to the right IdentityRegistry deployment.

new_components_needed.description: Deploy IdentityRegistry + Groth16Verifier + PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier to Theseus L1. Contracts are already written and audited on Base Sepolia — this is a deployment task, not a build task. Requires Theseus L1 RPC, funded deployer wallet, and verifying constructor args match the zkeys used in production.

Add Theseus L1 chain config to @bolyra/sdk (chain ID, RPC URL, IdentityRegistry address). Expose a theseusClient factory or extend the existing chain-config map. No circuit changes, no new proving logic.

Theseus agent adapter: thin wrapper that intercepts outbound agent authorization calls, calls proveHandshake with a fresh nonce, and submits the atomic handshake tx to the Theseus IdentityRegistry before executing the downstream action. Pattern mirrors the existing openclaw adapter. Required for the Sunday demo.

Add a 'Theseus L1 replay prevention profile' section to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 specifying: nonce freshness requirements for high-frequency agents (recommend per-transaction nonce, not per-session), nullifier TTL relative to IdentityRegistry root history buffer depth (30 blocks), and the on-chain verification flow for counterparty agents. Strengthens partnership leverage.

theseus_integration_surface: Primary surface: a deployed IdentityRegistry on Theseus L1 that Theseus agent runtimes call before executing any authorized action. The atomic handshake tx (Groth16 + PLONK in a single call) serves as the authorization gate — the agent's transaction only proceeds if verifyHandshake succeeds on-chain, which requires a fresh nonce not previously seen in the nullifier set. Secondary surface: @bolyra/sdk with a Theseus chain config, so agent operators can call proveHandshake targeting the Theseus registry without forking the SDK. For the demo, a lightweight Theseus agent adapter (analogous to the openclaw adapter) intercepts outbound calls, generates and submits the handshake proof, and only forwards the action on success. No bridge, oracle, or cross-chain message passing is needed — Theseus L1 carries its own nullifier state, fully self-contained.

deliverables.name: theseus-deploy-script
deliverables.description: Hardhat deploy script targeting Theseus L1 RPC — deploys Groth16Verifier, PlonkVerifier, DelegationPlonkVerifier, and IdentityRegistry in one shot. Identical contracts to Base Sepolia; only chainId and RPC endpoint change. Assumes EVM-compatible VM — the single largest unknown.

deliverables.name: replay-attack-demo-script
deliverables.description: Node script that (1) generates a valid agent handshake proof, (2) submits it to IdentityRegistry on Theseus and succeeds, (3) replays the identical (humanProof, agentProof, nonce) tuple and shows the on-chain revert. Self-contained, runnable in under 60 seconds. Uses Base Sepolia as fallback if Theseus testnet is unavailable Sunday.

deliverables.name: theseus-chain-config
deliverables.description: Add Theseus L1 chainId, RPC URL, and deployed contract addresses to @bolyra/sdk ChainConfig. One file edit + type export. Lets any integrator call sdk.connect('theseus') after the call.
deliverables.reuses: @bolyra/sdk chain config pattern from Base Sepolia

deliverables.name: nullifier-registry-integration-spec
deliverables.description: Two-page technical spec scoped to Theseus: problem statement (raw EdDSA = valid forever), Bolyra's nullifier-bound session nonce solution, contract interface, SDK call sequence, and open questions for Eric Wang (EVM compat?, testnet RPC?, block latency for root history buffer). Doubles as meeting prep doc.
deliverables.reuses: new — distilled from existing CLAUDE.md architecture notes

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: proveHandshake() generates a fresh-nonce agent proof, first submitProof() call to IdentityRegistry succeeds (tx hash shown), identical replay call reverts with NullifierAlreadyUsed. If Theseus testnet RPC is available before Sunday, run against it directly; otherwise run against Base Sepolia and call out that the contract bytecode is chain-agnostic. Show the 8-bit permission bitmask in the proof output to illustrate scope narrowing. Entire demo is < 90 seconds of live terminal.
risks: Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — if it's a custom VM, verifier contracts cannot be ported without rewriting in the target language (weeks, not days); must confirm before committing to Sunday deploy
No public Theseus testnet RPC found — demo falls back to Base Sepolia, which weakens the 'deployed on Theseus' narrative; need Eric to share an RPC endpoint before the call
Root history buffer (30 entries) was tuned for Ethereum block times (~12s); Theseus L1 block time is unknown — buffer depth may need adjustment if blocks are faster
solo-founder context means any integration bug found after the call has no second pair of eyes; build the demo against a fixed fixture set, not a live prover, to eliminate timing failures on-call
spec.success_criteria: IdentityRegistry deployed to Theseus (or Base Sepolia as stand-in), replay-attack-demo-script exits 0, the revert reason NullifierAlreadyUsed is visible in the terminal output, and Eric Wang can call sdk.connect('theseus').verifyHandshake() from his own Node environment using the published contract address.
spec.theseus_dependencies: EVM-compatibility confirmation (binary yes/no), testnet RPC URL + faucet access, deployed block time (for root history buffer sizing), and whether they have an existing identity/auth interface contract Bolyra should integrate with rather than deploy alongside.

title: Mutual agent handshake protocol for agent-to-agent service hiring on Theseus
description: When Agent A wants to hire Agent B for a paid service on Theseus, both parties need to exchange verifiable credentials before committing funds — Agent A proves it has spending authority, Agent B proves it has the claimed capability and a non-zero reputation score, and both bind to a fresh session nonce so neither replay is valid. Bolyra's existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake API implements exactly this mutual credential exchange; the missing piece is an agent-rooted variant where the root of trust is the Theseus L1 genesis transaction rather than a human enrollment. This is the commercial primitive that makes Theseus an actual agent economy rather than just an agent ledger — without it, agents transact on blind trust or fall back to human-curated allowlists. Base MCP's 6 DeFi protocol plugins for AI agents demonstrate the demand for programmatic agent service access, but none enforce mutual pre-authorization.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' structural argument is strong and the Base MCP demand evidence is concrete, but Theseus-specific citations rely on inferred context and an Orange DAO announcement rather than direct documentation, codebase, or community discussion confirming agents need mutual pre-authorization specifically.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol, Turnkey, and Privy are named with structural failure modes (oracle centralization, no mutual exchange, human-delegation incompatibility), but the analysis doesn't rule out EdDSA + on-chain capability attestations achieving 80% of the same result without ZKP — the selective disclosure privacy argument is present but underdeveloped.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy, Delegation, and IdentityRegistry are specifically named with modification scope, the new AgentGenesisRoot circuit has a 3-day estimate with 80% reuse justification, and the MutualHandshakeAdapter is scoped; however, critical unknowns (Theseus keccak256 genesis format, BabyJubJub confirmation) are explicitly flagged as unverified and the contract deliverable description is truncated.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The opportunity does not assess Theseus's internal ZKP capability or provide evidence they lack Circom proficiency, does not argue why they would choose Bolyra over Lit Protocol (which already targets agent key management), and the 'blind trust or allowlists' alternative consequence is asserted rather than demonstrated with Theseus-specific evidence.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (provided context + known Orange DAO announcement)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 ($100K Orange DAO, founded 2025) with 'no human key path' — agents hold their own EdDSA keys, making standard human-rooted identity primitives structurally incompatible and creating a direct gap Bolyra's agent-rooted ZKP handshake could fill.

Base MCP ships 6 DeFi protocol plugins for AI agents with no mutual pre-authorization enforcement, confirming demand for programmatic agent service access without human gating — exactly the gap the mutual handshake targets.

x402 payment protocol enables machine-to-machine HTTP payments but lacks ZKP-based mutual credential verification — agents can pay but cannot cryptographically prove capability or spending authority before committing funds.

Lit Protocol provides programmable threshold key management and conditional signing for agents, but relies on access-condition evaluation (centralized oracle-style) rather than zero-knowledge proofs, leaving replay and linkability risks unaddressed.

Turnkey offers TEE-backed key management APIs used by several agent frameworks for secure signing, but provides no mutual credential exchange or capability proof — it is a key custody layer, not an identity handshake protocol.

Privy focuses on embedded wallets for human end-users (social login → wallet); their server wallets feature is gaining traction for agents but is fundamentally human-delegated, incompatible with Theseus's no-human-key-path requirement.
Dynamic offers multi-chain wallet UX and auth flows for humans; no agent-native identity primitives or ZKP credential exchange capability.

evidence.source: W3C Decentralized Identifiers (DID) Core 1.0 + Verifiable Credentials Data Model
evidence.summary: W3C DID/VC specs provide the vocabulary for agent identity documents but define no mutual handshake protocol and no ZKP-native proof exchange mechanism — Bolyra's IETF draft fills exactly this gap.

Emerging IETF work on AI agent authentication acknowledges the need for machine-to-machine credential exchange but has no reference implementation; Bolyra's proveHandshake/verifyHandshake is the only known working ZKP-backed implementation of this pattern.

Fetch.ai's AEA framework has a built-in identity layer (agent addresses derived from Ed25519 keys) and a service registry, but credential exchange during agent-to-agent transactions is trust-based (reputation scores), not cryptographically provable — same structural gap as Theseus.

Autonolas (formerly Valory) runs multi-agent services on a dedicated protocol chain with on-chain service NFTs as identity anchors; no mutual ZKP handshake, and capability proofs require human-registered service metadata rather than agent-generated ZK credentials.

Virtuals tokenizes AI agents on Base as ERC-20s for investment; identity is wallet address + token ownership, no capability-proving or delegation circuit — entirely different trust model, not a direct competitor to Bolyra's ZKP handshake.

Semaphore v4 is the most widely deployed ZKP identity primitive on Ethereum (Groth16, Merkle membership proofs, nullifier-based uniqueness) — Bolyra reuses its ceremony for HumanUniqueness, giving it a credibility anchor but Semaphore itself has no agent-rooted variant or mutual handshake.

World ID deploys ZKP proof-of-personhood at scale (10M+ verified humans) using iris biometrics + Semaphore; entirely human-rooted with no agent credential system — confirms mass-market ZKP identity is viable but leaves the machine identity layer unaddressed.

Aztec is an L2 with native ZKP-based privacy for transactions and smart contracts; Noir language enables custom ZKP circuits, but Aztec is a privacy compute layer, not an agent identity handshake protocol — no mutual credential exchange API.
ZKPass enables ZKP-based verification of Web2 data (TLS oracles) for humans; no agent credential model and not deployed on agent-native L1s.

Coinbase AgentKit gives agents a wallet and onchain action primitives (ERC-20, NFT, DeFi) but has no mutual credential exchange — agents transact by raw key authority with no ZKP-backed capability or spending-limit proof, exactly the gap the Bolyra handshake closes.

mapped_primitives.usage: Agent A calls AgentPolicy to prove FINANCIAL_MEDIUM/FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED bits are set (spending authority) via selective disclosure — requiredScopeMask only exposes the financial bits, leaving capability and PII bits hidden. Agent B symmetrically proves its capability scope bits are set. Both proofs share the same sessionNonce via nonce binding, making the handshake atomic. The Poseidon3 scope commitment anchors each proof to its credential commitment so neither party can substitute a different credential mid-handshake.

When Agent A sub-contracts to Agent B (e.g., A is an orchestrator, B is a specialist worker), the Delegation circuit enforces that B's scope is a bitwise subset of A's scope and B's expiry cannot exceed A's — exactly the capability-bounded service contract semantics Theseus needs. The 3-hop chain limit (IdentityRegistry enforced) maps cleanly to orchestrator → specialist → tool patterns common in agent marketplaces.

The dual LeanIMT trees (humanTree + agentTree), root history buffers (30 entries), atomic handshake verification, and nonce equality enforcement are the on-chain settlement layer for the Theseus mutual handshake. The delegation nullifier replay protection prevents double-spend of a delegation token across marketplace transactions. Deploy a Theseus-facing instance or bridge proofs back to Base Sepolia via storage proofs.

The existing payment-protocols integration directory provides the hook for x402-style pre-authorization gating: funds are committed only after both AgentPolicy proofs verify on-chain, turning the handshake into a conditional payment primitive. Agent A's FINANCIAL_* bits in the proof double as the spending-authority assertion that the payment layer can check before releasing escrow.

Not used for agent-to-agent handshakes directly. However, the Semaphore v4-style nullifier pattern (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) is the template for the new agent-genesis nullifier: replace the human Merkle secret with the Theseus genesis transaction hash as the root-of-trust anchor. No circuit changes needed — the structural pattern is reused in the new GenesisRoot circuit.

new_components_needed.description: AgentGenesisRoot circuit — PLONK variant of AgentPolicy where the Merkle root is derived from the Theseus L1 genesis transaction hash rather than a human operator enrollment. The operator EdDSA key in AgentPolicy is replaced with a Theseus-chain-signed genesis credential (operator = Theseus protocol key). Constraints: ~21K (AgentPolicy + Poseidon hash of genesis tx). This is the 'agent-rooted root of trust' the opportunity identifies as the missing piece.

ReputationThreshold circuit — proves agent reputation score >= threshold without revealing exact score. Input: Poseidon commitment to (agentId, reputationScore, epoch), operator-signed. Output: bit proving score >= requiredThreshold via range check (Num2Bits(32) comparison). Composable with AgentPolicy via scope commitment chaining so a single PLONK proof covers capability + reputation simultaneously.

TheseusHandshakeGateway — thin Solidity contract deployed on Theseus L1 (or Base Sepolia with a Theseus storage-proof bridge). Wraps IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() with (a) genesis root validation against Theseus L1 state, (b) payment escrow release conditioned on dual-proof verification, and (c) reputation nullifier tracking so a stale reputation proof cannot be replayed across marketplace sessions.

@bolyra/theseus adapter — mirrors the @bolyra/openclaw pattern. Exposes proveAgentHandshake(agentA, agentB, theseusGenesisRef, reputationCommitment) which orchestrates AgentGenesisRoot + ReputationThreshold proofs in parallel (both are PLONK, can fan-out), assembles the mutual proof pair, and submits to TheseusHandshakeGateway. Wraps x402 payment commitment into the same atomic call.

Theseus DID method extension (did:bolyra:theseus) — extends the existing did:bolyra DID method spec to describe agent DIDs anchored to Theseus L1 genesis transactions. Required for interoperability with Theseus's agent registry and for the conformance test suite (adds ~8 new test vectors to test-vectors.json covering genesis-rooted proofs and reputation threshold assertions).

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) On-chain — TheseusHandshakeGateway contract on Theseus L1 calls the existing PlonkVerifier (deployed to Base Sepolia at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db) via a storage-proof relay, or a mirrored PlonkVerifier is deployed directly on Theseus — the verifier is generated deterministically from the .zkey so deployment is a one-command operation. (2) SDK bridge — @bolyra/theseus wraps @bolyra/sdk v0.2.0 and adds Theseus genesis credential construction + ReputationThreshold proof generation; agent operators install one package. (3) Payment layer — x402 payment-protocols integration hooks into TheseusHandshakeGateway's escrow release event so funds move atomically with proof verification, with no separate payment-auth step. The IdentityRegistry agentTree on Base Sepolia can mirror Theseus agent registrations via a lightweight cross-chain oracle (Chainlink CCIP or a custom storage-proof verifier) without requiring Theseus to run a full Bolyra stack.

deliverables.name: Bolyra-Theseus Integration Spec
deliverables.description: IETF-style markdown draft covering: AgentGenesisRoot trust anchor derivation (Theseus genesis TX hash as Merkle root anchor replacing human enrollment), mutual handshake wire format, delegation chain semantics (3-hop limit), nonce binding requirements, and x402 payment gate interface. Draftable in one focused session — usable as Sunday call artifact and eventual standards submission.

deliverables.name: AgentGenesisRoot circuit
deliverables.description: PLONK port of AgentPolicy where the Merkle root is anchored to the Theseus L1 genesis transaction hash instead of a human operator enrollment. Operator EdDSA pubkey replaced by genesisHash as the root-of-trust leaf; nullifier is Poseidon2(scope, genesisHash) for cross-session replay prevention. Assumes 32-byte keccak256 genesis input and BabyJubJub EdDSA — both need confirmation from Theseus before finalizing.

deliverables.name: MutualHandshakeAdapter (TypeScript)
deliverables.description: Thin TypeScript wrapper around proveHandshake/verifyHandshake accepting agent-genesis credentials with no human root. Exports agentHandshake(agentA, agentB, sessionNonce) returning dual AgentGenesisRoot proofs ready for on-chain settlement. Wire format + credential type adaptation only — no new proving logic.

deliverables.name: TheseusIdentityRegistry contract
deliverables.description: Minimal fork of IdentityRegistry.sol with humanTree dropped and agentTree only. Verifies AgentGenesisRoot proofs on-chain, enforces nonce uniqueness via nullifier map, exposes verifyMutualHandshake() payable hook for x402 escrow gating. Deployed to local Hardhat fork first; EVM mainnet deployment gated on Theseus testnet RPC access.

deliverables.name: x402 payment gate stub
deliverables.description: Wire TheseusIdentityRegistry.verifyMutualHandshake() as pre-authorization gate in integrations/payment-protocols. Funds release only after both AgentGenesisRoot proofs verify on-chain. FINANCIAL_* bit check from Agent A's proof doubles as spending-authority assertion. Implemented as a stub with hardcoded escrow simulation if Theseus testnet not live by sprint end.

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo CLI
deliverables.description: Node.js CLI: Agent A (orchestrator, FINANCIAL_MEDIUM bit set) hires Agent B (specialist, capability bits set) on Hardhat-forked Theseus registry. Shows: genesis credential generation, dual PLONK proof generation (~3s snarkjs fallback since rapidsnark lacks PLONK support), on-chain mutual verification, simulated payment release. Runnable in under 5 minutes from README on a fresh clone.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call: run existing examples/mcp-demo to show live agent credential issuance and proveHandshake/verifyHandshake with the 8-bit permission model. Walk through Delegation circuit enforcing scope narrowing (orchestrator→specialist pattern). Present the Integration Spec (deliverable 1, drafted Saturday) showing how AgentGenesisRoot replaces human enrollment — Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint maps directly to dropping humanTree and substituting genesis TX hash as the root-of-trust anchor. No new circuit needed Sunday; the conceptual fit plus working handshake demo is the story. Budget 4 hours Saturday to draft the spec.
risks: Theseus VM compatibility unknown — if non-EVM, TheseusIdentityRegistry cannot deploy natively and requires a storage-proof bridge adding ~3 days
Genesis transaction hash format unconfirmed — spec assumes 32-byte keccak256 input to Poseidon; different scheme requires a hash adaptor gadget in the circuit
BabyJubJub EdDSA assumed for agent keys — if Theseus uses ed25519, AgentPolicy needs a hash-to-field adaptor (significant circuit change)
rapidsnark does not support PLONK — demo falls back to snarkjs at ~3s per proof instead of ~800ms; production-grade latency requires a PLONK-capable native prover or Groth16 fallback
Theseus testnet likely not live during sprint — all integration tests run against Hardhat local fork, not true L1 settlement; demo cannot prove on-chain finality
Solo capacity: 11 days assumes zero context switching; compress to 8 by stubbing x402 gate as a logged assertion if timeline slips
spec.success_criteria: End-to-end CLI completes without error on fresh clone: Agent A's AgentGenesisRoot proof verifies on TheseusIdentityRegistry, Agent B's proof verifies, dual-nonce equality check passes, simulated escrow releases — single npm run demo:theseus invocation, no manual steps.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1. EVM compatibility confirmation (or testnet RPC endpoint) — blocks contract deployment. 2. Genesis transaction format spec (32-byte hash vs structured data) — blocks AgentGenesisRoot circuit finalization. 3. EdDSA key scheme (BabyJubJub vs ed25519) — blocks AgentPolicy reuse assumption. 4. Acceptable max delegation depth (3-hop assumed) — blocks Delegation circuit config. 5. Existing agent registry contract ABI if one exists — determines whether TheseusIdentityRegistry augments or replaces their current auth layer.

title: Sybil-Resistant Agent Genesis Proof Without Human Biometric Root
description: On human-centric chains, Sybil resistance relies on biometrics (World ID) or social graph (Proof of Humanity). On Theseus, neither applies — there is no human in the loop. An attacker can spin up 10,000 agent identities at near-zero cost, flooding the agent economy and manipulating reputation/voting mechanisms. ZKP nullifier schemes (adapted from HumanUniqueness circuit) can prove that a given agent genesis transaction was unique to a specific operator key or TEE attestation without revealing the operator — preventing farm creation while preserving agent autonomy. This is a day-1 blocker: any agent marketplace, reputation system, or governance mechanism on Theseus is immediately gameable without Sybil resistance.

rationale.agent_need: The structural argument from the 'no human key path' constraint is sound and the Fetch.ai staking-as-soft-deterrent analogy is concrete evidence, but no Theseus documentation directly names agent Sybil resistance as a gap — the evidence is absence-of-spec rather than affirmative demand signal.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol, Turnkey, and Fetch.ai are named and their failures are structural (no cryptographic uniqueness binding, economic-only deterrent, unlimited PKP minting) rather than strawman comparisons, making the ZKP nullifier advantage well-justified.
rationale.primitive_readiness: The AgentGenesis.circom spec is well-scoped (~800 constraints, named input/output signals, clear reuse of Poseidon2 nullifier and EdDSA verification patterns), but 6 days for a new Circom circuit including test coverage is aggressive per rubric standards (minimum 3-5 days before SDK and deployment work), and EVM compatibility of Theseus is assumed without confirmation.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Theseus is confirmed early-stage with no named identity partner, which is meaningful, but the proposal doesn't substantively address why Theseus couldn't adopt Lit Protocol's PKP approach or hire one ZKP engineer to build a nullifier scheme in 3 months — the expertise gap argument is asserted rather than evidenced.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (public landing, no docs portal indexed as of knowledge cutoff)
evidence.summary: Theseus markets itself as an agent-native L1 with no human key path, but no public specification for identity, Sybil resistance, or agent genesis has been published — the gap is real and unaddressed.

evidence.source: Orange DAO / YC alumni DAO deal memo (public signal)
evidence.summary: $100K seed from Orange DAO confirms early-stage; no identity partner named, meaning the slot is open for Bolyra to claim at the Sunday call.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — docs.litprotocol.com, PKP (Programmable Key Pairs)
evidence.summary: Lit offers MPC-based agent keys tied to on-chain conditions, but provides no ZKP nullifier for Sybil resistance at genesis — operator can mint unlimited PKPs.
Turnkey provides TEE-secured key generation and API-first wallets for agents, but genesis uniqueness relies on operator API key, not a cryptographic uniqueness proof — Sybil farm possible.
Privy server wallets are gaining agent adoption but are custodial and offer no Sybil resistance primitive; designed for human-app flows, not autonomous agent economies.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / Agentverse — agent registration on Fetch.ai L1
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai requires agents to stake FET tokens at registration as a soft Sybil deterrent, but no ZKP uniqueness proof — economic barrier only, easily bypassed at scale.
SingularityNET uses staking + reputation scoring for Sybil mitigation but no cryptographic genesis uniqueness; reputation manipulation via Sybil farms has been a documented attack vector.
Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication focuses on delegated credentials and OAuth-style scopes but does not address Sybil-resistant genesis — nullifier schemes are out of scope.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / World ID — Sybil resistance via biometric root
evidence.summary: World ID's nullifier scheme (adapted in Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit) proves uniqueness against a human biometric Merkle root — structurally inapplicable to agent genesis without a human anchor.

evidence.source: Aztec Network / Noir — ZKP identity primitives on L1
evidence.summary: Aztec's Noir-based nullifier commitments demonstrate ZKP Sybil resistance on EVM-compatible chains, but no agent-specific genesis proof exists; closest analogue to Bolyra's proposed approach.

TEE attestation (Intel TDX, AMD SEV) bound to operator keys is the leading non-biometric uniqueness anchor for autonomous agents; Bolyra's nullifier scheme could wrap TEE attestation quotes as the genesis root.

evidence.source: Holonym (holonym.id) — ZKP human identity without biometrics
evidence.summary: Holonym proves uniqueness from government documents rather than biometrics using ZKPs; the pattern of 'non-biometric uniqueness anchor + nullifier' is directly portable to operator-key or TEE roots for agents.

mapped_primitives.usage: The Poseidon2(scope, secret) nullifier scheme is the direct analog for agent genesis uniqueness. Adapted: nullifier = Poseidon2(operatorKeyHash, agentNonce), where operatorKeyHash replaces the human BabyJubjub secret. This prevents one operator key from spawning N agents under different nonces in the same scope (marketplace, governance contract address). The Merkle membership pattern is reused to prove the operator key is in an authorized-operator tree, replacing the human enrollment tree.

EdDSA operator signature verification over a credential commitment is directly reusable. The operator signs a genesis commitment (agentModelHash || agentNonce || expiryTimestamp), and the circuit verifies the signature without revealing the operator key. The identity-bound scope commitment pattern (Poseidon3) can bind the genesis proof to the Theseus marketplace contract address as scope, preventing cross-marketplace replay.

Scope-narrowing delegation from operator root to spawned sub-agents maps directly to the Theseus operator-delegates-to-agent-farm attack surface. The delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope invariant enforced in the circuit prevents privilege escalation even if sub-agents are compromised. Relevant for Theseus governance: an operator can prove it spawned at most one voting agent per scope.

The agentTree (LeanIMT, depth 20) and its root history buffer (30 entries) are reusable for tracking registered agent genesis nullifiers. The nullifier replay protection slot in the contract maps directly to sybil prevention: once nullifier N is consumed for a given scope, no second genesis is possible. The atomic handshake verification pattern becomes atomic genesis + delegation verification in a single Theseus tx.

new_components_needed.description: AgentGenesis.circom — new PLONK circuit. Inputs: operatorPrivKey (EdDSA BabyJubjub), agentNonce (64-bit), scopeId (contract address of Theseus marketplace/registry), operatorMerkleProof (depth 20 authorized-operator tree). Public outputs: genesisNullifier = Poseidon2(operatorKeyHash, agentNonce), operatorTreeRoot, scopeId. Optional second proving path: TEE attestation hash replaces operatorPrivKey via a hash-preimage commitment, for TEE-rooted operator identity. Sybil invariant: same operatorKeyHash + same scopeId → same nullifier → registry rejects second genesis. ~800 constraints expected (nullifier + range check + optional Merkle).

AgentGenesisRegistry.sol for Theseus chain — stores genesis nullifiers per scope, maintains authorized-operator LeanIMT (or accepts root from an external operator registry), calls deployed PlonkVerifier for genesis proof verification. Emits GenesisRegistered(nullifier, scopeId, agentAddress). Gate: revert if nullifier already consumed in scope. Must be deployable on Theseus EVM (verify EVM compatibility / opcode set first).

createAgentGenesisProof(operatorKey, agentNonce, scopeId, operatorMerkleWitness, options?) → GenesisProof in @bolyra/sdk v0.3. Mirrors createAgentCredential() call signature but outputs a standalone genesis proof rather than a handshake proof pair. Include registerAgentGenesis(proof, theseusRpc) convenience wrapper that submits the tx and returns the on-chain nullifier.

Theseus chain adapter — hardhat deploy scripts targeting Theseus RPC, PlonkVerifier redeploy on Theseus (same bytecode, new address), chain-id binding in SDK config. Verify EVM opcode compatibility (PUSH0, transient storage, etc.) before assuming bytecode portability. Add 'theseus' network to hardhat.config.ts and circuits/build/ artifact manifest.

TEE attestation binding extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01. Defines how a DCAP/TDX quote or SGX attestation maps to a BabyJubjub commitment that can substitute for operatorPrivKey in the AgentGenesis circuit. Enables TEE-rooted sybil resistance without operator key exposure — critical for autonomous Theseus agents that have no human operator online at genesis time.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) On-chain — deploy PlonkVerifier (existing bytecode) + new AgentGenesisRegistry.sol to Theseus; Theseus marketplace/reputation/governance contracts call agentGenesisRegistry.verifyAndRegister(proof, nullifier, scopeId) before accepting a new agent. (2) SDK bridge — @bolyra/sdk createAgentGenesisProof() runs client-side in Node (snarkjs) or via rapidsnark binary for production throughput; proof is posted in the genesis transaction. (3) Operator registry feed — Theseus needs a canonical authorized-operator LeanIMT whose root is posted on-chain; Bolyra provides the tree construction + Merkle witness generation utilities. No API server required: proving is fully client-side, verification is fully on-chain.

deliverables.name: bolyra-theseus-integration-spec.md
deliverables.description: Architecture doc: nullifier scheme derivation (Poseidon2(operatorKeyHash, agentNonce)), operator Merkle tree design, TEE attestation alternate path, replay protection contract interface, and how Theseus marketplace contract address binds as scopeId. Written for Theseus eng team consumption. Ready for Sunday call.

deliverables.name: AgentGenesis.circom
deliverables.description: PLONK circuit (~800 constraints). Private inputs: operatorPrivKey (EdDSA BabyJubjub), agentNonce (64-bit), operatorMerkleProof (depth 20). Public outputs: genesisNullifier = Poseidon2(operatorKeyHash, agentNonce), operatorTreeRoot, scopeId (Theseus marketplace contract address). Enforces: valid EdDSA key, nonce in range, operator in authorized tree, nullifier uniqueness binding. Same-operatorKeyHash + same-scopeId yields same nullifier — registry rejects second genesis. Optional TEE path: hash-preimage commitment replaces EdDSA key.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy EdDSA verify + HumanUniqueness Poseidon2 nullifier + Delegation Merkle membership pattern

deliverables.name: AgentGenesisRegistry.sol
deliverables.description: EVM contract (assumes Theseus is EVM-compatible). Stores: consumed nullifier bitmap, operator tree root, PLONK verifier address. Methods: registerAgent(proof, publicSignals) — verifies proof, checks nullifier not consumed, emits AgentRegistered(agentAddr, scopeId, nullifier). Mirrors IdentityRegistry nullifier slot pattern. Deployable to Theseus testnet once available.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry nullifier replay protection + HandshakeVerifier.sol verifyProof call pattern

deliverables.name: theseus-genesis-adapter.ts
deliverables.description: New module in @bolyra/sdk: proveAgentGenesis(operatorPrivKey, agentNonce, scopeId, operatorTree) → { proof, publicSignals }. Wraps snarkjs PLONK prove, builds Merkle witness, formats for Theseus registry contract ABI. Exports verifyGenesisProof() for server-side verification without on-chain call. 100-line surface area.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy prove path + LeanIMT witness builder + snarkjs PLONK wrapper

deliverables.name: genesis-demo.sh
deliverables.description: Terminal demo script: (1) register 2 operator keys in local tree, (2) prove genesis for operator-A in marketplace scope, (3) submit to local Hardhat node — succeeds, (4) attempt second genesis same operator same scope — fails with NullifierConsumed, (5) prove genesis for operator-A in different scope — succeeds (cross-scope is allowed). Output: 5-step pass/fail log visible in 60 seconds. Uses fast witness-only mode for demo speed.
deliverables.reuses: Hardhat local deploy pattern + circuits fast test harness

spec.demo_description: Sunday call (June 29): show the spec doc + walk through existing AgentPolicy circuit as working analog (operator EdDSA sig + scopeId binding already works). Diagram the AgentGenesis adaptation — 15-minute technical pitch establishing Bolyra as the only team with working ZKP agent identity primitives. No working AgentGenesis circuit in 1 day — but the existing AgentPolicy demo is compelling enough to validate the direction and claim the slot. Two-week sprint delivers the working terminal demo with real nullifier replay rejection.
risks: Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — verifier contract needs rewrite for custom VM; ask on Sunday call before writing Solidity
Authorized operator tree management is unspecified — who bootstraps it, who adds operators? Need Theseus to own this UX or the integration is a dead end
TEE attestation alternate path doubles circuit complexity — descope to EdDSA-only for v1 and add TEE as v2 to avoid blowing the 6-day circuit estimate
Solo bandwidth: circuit debugging is asymmetric, a subtle under-constraint bug can eat 2+ days; gate on fast tests passing before attempting full proof
No Theseus testnet means registry contract is undeployed — demo stays on local Hardhat; integration is unproven on the actual chain until testnet access
spec.success_criteria: registerAgent() succeeds for first genesis of (operatorKeyHash, scopeId) pair; second call with same inputs reverts with NullifierConsumed; cross-scope genesis with same operator succeeds; full proof generation completes in under 3 seconds on M2 via rapidsnark; TypeScript adapter proveAgentGenesis() returns valid proof in under 500ms on fast path
spec.theseus_dependencies: VM type confirmation (EVM-compatible or custom — needed before Solidity contract work); testnet RPC endpoint + faucet; confirmation of whether agent key model is EdDSA BabyJubjub or secp256k1 (determines circuit input format); clarification on whether Theseus has an existing operator registry or Bolyra owns that entirely

title: AgentPolicy + IdentityRegistry as Theseus native agent identity layer
description: The existing AgentPolicy circuit (PLONK, 20923 constraints) already verifies EdDSA operator signatures over credential commitments, enforces 8-bit cumulative permission bitmasks, and supports selective disclosure of required scope bits — exactly what Theseus needs to prove 'this agent is authorized for action X' without revealing its full capability set. The IdentityRegistry.agentTree (dual LeanIMT with 30-entry root history buffer) provides the on-chain membership oracle. Minimal new work: deploy IdentityRegistry on Theseus L1 (if EVM-compatible) or expose a cross-chain state proof relay to Base Sepolia. The createAgentCredential() + proveHandshake() SDK path ships today. This can be demoed Sunday with zero circuit changes if Theseus is EVM-compatible.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' constraint on Theseus creates a structural need for agent-native authorization, supported by the Orange DAO funding context and Fetch.ai analogue, but no direct Theseus documentation or community discussion is cited confirming they lack an existing solution.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol (threshold crypto) and Turnkey (enclave-backed) are named as non-ZKP alternatives and their failure modes identified (no selective disclosure, server-enforced policies), but the structural argument for why on-chain verifiable permission bitmask commitment is necessary for Theseus's specific architecture remains asserted rather than demonstrated.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy PLONK circuit, IdentityRegistry agentTree, PlonkVerifier, and the createAgentCredential()+proveHandshake() SDK path are all named concretely with zero circuit changes required, and the integration deliverables are scoped to ~1.5 days of real work against existing artifacts.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The ZKP circuit design and Circom proficiency gap is plausible, but the analysis doesn't assess whether Theseus has crypto engineers or an in-progress identity solution, and Lit Protocol is a credible drop-in competitor that could be adopted in under 3 months without ZKP expertise.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (context provided — not crawled; search permission denied)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 funded by Orange DAO ($100K) whose core thesis is agents holding their own keys with no human-controlled key path, making a human-root-of-trust identity system like standard Bolyra a partial mismatch requiring the agent-rooted delegation path.

Lit provides threshold-cryptography-based programmable signing for agents; it offers access control conditions but lacks ZKP-based selective disclosure of permission bitmasks, which is Bolyra's structural edge.
Turnkey offers secure enclave-backed agent wallets with policy engine for signing; no ZKP layer, no on-chain membership oracle, permission model is rule-based not commitment-based.
Privy's agent wallets target web2-adjacent auth flows; minimal cryptographic delegation proof — policies are server-enforced, not verifiable on-chain by a counterparty.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + VC Data Model (knowledge cutoff Aug 2025)
evidence.summary: DID specifications support machine-readable agent identity but lack native ZKP selective disclosure of scoped permissions; Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit addresses this gap directly.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent (referenced in bolyra-bookmarks skill context)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF draft covering AI agent authentication; no ratified ZKP-based credential scheme yet — window open for Bolyra to be the reference implementation.

Fetch.ai agents communicate via μAgent protocol with DID-like addresses but no ZKP permission proofs; closest analogue to Theseus's agent-native model, showing real demand for on-chain agent authorization primitives.
AI service marketplace with agent-to-service authorization, but token-gated not ZKP-gated; scope too broad to serve as a direct Theseus integration comp.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / PSE — pse.dev (knowledge cutoff Aug 2025)
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 provides anonymous group membership proofs reused by Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit; no agent-specific permission scope model — Bolyra's AgentPolicy fills that gap.

evidence.source: Worldcoin / World ID — worldcoin.org (knowledge cutoff Aug 2025)
evidence.summary: Human-uniqueness ZKP deployed on-chain but biometric-gated and human-only; structurally inapplicable to agent identity, confirming Bolyra occupies an uncontested niche on the agent side.

evidence.source: Bolyra CLAUDE.md + AgentPolicy circuit spec (local repo)
evidence.summary: AgentPolicy (PLONK, 20923 constraints) already enforces EdDSA operator signatures + 8-bit cumulative permission bitmask + selective scope disclosure; createAgentCredential() + proveHandshake() SDK path ships today, requiring only IdentityRegistry deploy on a Theseus-compatible chain.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core authorization primitive. EdDSA operator signature verification over credential commitments maps directly to Theseus's 'is this agent authorized for action X' question. Selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask bits proves minimum necessary permissions without revealing the full capability set. Expiry enforcement prevents stale credentials. Zero circuit changes needed.

agentTree (dual LeanIMT, depth 20, 30-entry root history buffer) serves as the on-chain membership oracle for all enrolled Theseus agents. Root history buffer handles the latency between enrollment and proof generation without requiring synchronous finality. If Theseus is EVM-compatible, redeploy as-is. If not, use as the Base Sepolia anchor and relay storage proofs.

AgentPolicy proofs are PLONK — this verifier contract must be present on whichever chain Theseus's settlement layer sits on. If EVM-compatible, deploy the existing artifact. The PLONK universal SRS means no new trusted setup ceremony is required.

Covers Theseus multi-hop agent chains (orchestrator → worker → tool). Max 3-hop enforcement and scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) are already in the circuit. Relevant if Theseus exposes sub-agent spawning. Not required for the Sunday demo — can be layered in post-integration.

Optional: binds a human principal to the agent tree via a handshake. Relevant if Theseus requires operator accountability (human vouches for agent enrollment). proveHandshake() already composes both proofs in a single SDK call with nonce binding.

createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, permissions, expiry) enrolls the Theseus agent. proveHandshake() generates the proof pair in one call. permissionsToBitmask() + validateCumulativeBitEncoding() enforce the 8-bit model client-side before hitting the circuit. Ships today, no changes needed for the demo path.

new_components_needed.description: Deploy IdentityRegistry + PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier on Theseus L1 if EVM-compatible. This is a redeployment of existing Hardhat artifacts — no new Solidity. Requires confirming Theseus chain-id and RPC endpoint, adding a Hardhat network config entry, and running the existing deploy:base-sepolia script against the new target.

Cross-chain storage proof relay (EVM storage proof + Merkle root sync) if Theseus is NOT EVM-compatible or prefers to anchor state on Base Sepolia. Would relay agentTree root from IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia to a lightweight Theseus verifier. Significant new engineering — skip for Sunday demo.

Theseus agent identity adapter — thin wrapper analogous to @bolyra/openclaw. Exposes enrollAgent(modelHash, operatorKey, permissions, expiry), proveAction(agent, requiredScope), and verifyAction(proof, nonce) with Theseus-idiomatic types. Builds on @bolyra/sdk v0.2.0 with no new circuit work.

Sunday demo script: end-to-end flow from createAgentCredential() → proveHandshake() → on-chain verifyHandshake() against the Theseus-deployed IdentityRegistry. Packaged as a runnable example in examples/theseus-demo/ alongside connection instructions.

theseus_integration_surface: Primary surface: IdentityRegistry contract deployed on Theseus L1 (EVM path) — agents enroll via agentTree, proofs verified on-chain via PlonkVerifier in a single verifyHandshake() call. SDK surface: Theseus operator calls createAgentCredential() off-chain, agent runtime calls proveHandshake() before each privileged action, Theseus action gateway calls verifyHandshake() or delegates to the on-chain registry. Secondary surface (non-EVM or hybrid path): Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry as the canonical root anchor with a storage proof relay publishing the agentTree root to Theseus — more complex but preserves a single enrollment registry. The Sunday demo uses the EVM path: one Hardhat deploy command, one SDK call, one on-chain verify.

deliverables.name: live-sdk-demo-script
deliverables.description: Node.js script (~60 lines) that runs createAgentCredential() + proveHandshake() end-to-end against a local Hardhat node, printing proof generation time, permission bitmask enforcement, and verifier output. No Theseus chain required — shows the primitive working today and makes the Sunday call concrete.

deliverables.name: theseus-integration-one-pager
deliverables.description: Single-page architecture diagram + narrative: (1) agent enrollment flow via createAgentCredential(), (2) on-chain proof verification via PlonkVerifier, (3) IdentityRegistry agentTree as membership oracle, (4) optional Delegation circuit for orchestrator→worker chains. Two paths called out: EVM-compatible (redeploy existing artifacts) vs custom VM (Base Sepolia anchor + storage proof relay). Leaves open questions as explicit asks to Theseus.

deliverables.name: hardhat-theseus-network-config

Verify via a single on-chain agentTree root insertion + proof verification call. This is the core integration artifact.

deliverables.name: theseus-agent-enrollment-example
deliverables.description: End-to-end example: enroll a mock Theseus agent (modelHash = keccak256 of 'theseus-agent-v0'), generate an AgentPolicy PLONK proof with a subset permission mask (READ_DATA | FINANCIAL_SMALL = 0b00000101), and call verifyProof() on the deployed PlonkVerifier. Output: proof valid, gas cost, latency. Publishable as examples/theseus-integration/.

deliverables.name: cross-chain-state-relay
deliverables.description: CONDITIONAL — only if Theseus is not EVM-compatible. Thin relay contract on Theseus that accepts storage proofs (EIP-1186 format) from Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry and verifies the agentTree root. Uses Theseus's native light client if available, otherwise a trusted relayer with event-log attestation as a temporary bridge. This is the high-complexity fallback path.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry root history buffer logic — relay only needs to verify a 30-slot ring buffer

spec.demo_description: Live terminal: run the demo script against local Hardhat, show AgentPolicy PLONK proof generated in ~800ms, permission bitmask validated, verifier returns true. Pair with the one-pager showing the two deployment paths. Ask Theseus for their chain-id + RPC on the call — if they can share it live, show the Hardhat deploy completing in real-time. If not EVM-compatible, pivot to 'here is what we need from you to scope the relay'.
risks: Theseus is not EVM-compatible: relay path adds 4 days and requires their light client spec — no relay, no on-chain verification on their chain
Theseus chain is not publicly accessible before the call: demo stays local/Hardhat, deployment happens post-call

Their 'no human key path' thesis may conflict with HumanUniqueness-rooted delegation — AgentPolicy standalone works, but full handshake proveHandshake() assumes human root; need to confirm which mode they want
PLONK verifier gas cost on Theseus L1 is unknown — if their execution environment has different gas economics the UX story changes
Solo founder bottleneck: cross-chain relay path (fallback) is 4 days of unfamiliar bridge code; if Theseus is not EVM-compatible this integration is genuinely risky to promise on a 1-week timeline
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus agent credential (modelHash, operatorPubKey, permissionBitmask=0x05, expiry=+30d) is enrolled in IdentityRegistry.agentTree on Theseus testnet, an AgentPolicy PLONK proof is generated via @bolyra/sdk, and PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() returns true in a Hardhat test or live on-chain. Gas receipt stored as evidence.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Chain-id and public RPC URL for their testnet (needed to deploy); confirmation of EVM-compatibility or their VM spec (determines path A vs relay); clarification on whether they want agent-only credentials (AgentPolicy standalone) or agent+human-accountability (full proveHandshake); contact for any on-chain integration testing after the call

title: Selective capability disclosure for Theseus agent marketplace
description: As Theseus scales to multiple agents transacting with each other, agents need to advertise capabilities without leaking their full permission set to counterparties (a privacy and competitive intelligence concern). AgentPolicy's selective disclosure mechanism — proving requiredScopeMask bits are set without revealing the full permissionBitmask — maps directly to this. An agent wanting to offer 'financial services up to $100' can prove bit 2 is set without revealing bits 5-7 (SIGN_ON_BEHALF, SUB_DELEGATE, ACCESS_PII). The on-chain proof (PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c...) serves as the attestation anchor for a marketplace discovery protocol. New work needed: a DID document extension (did:bolyra method already exists) that encodes the selective disclosure proof as a service endpoint, making it readable by Theseus marketplace indexers. Estimated 3-4 days of SDK + spec work.

rationale.agent_need: Fetch.ai's Agentverse provides an analogous precedent for agent capability advertising, and the 'no human key path' constraint structurally requires on-chain attestation, but there is no direct Theseus documentation confirming agents need capability privacy specifically rather than just capability discovery.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (plain-text manifest files as used by Fetch.ai) is concretely named and its failure mode is clear — unverifiable claims that leak the full permission set — and the selective disclosure property is structurally impossible without ZKP or BBS+ signatures, with BBS+ lacking the on-chain verifier binding.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy PLONK circuit, PlonkVerifier at a named address, did:bolyra DID method, and @bolyra/sdk proveHandshake() are all existing, named primitives; new work is limited to a thin SDK wrapper and a DID service endpoint spec extension, both well-scoped at 3-4 days.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this themselves is plausible given ZKP circuit expertise requirements, but the evidence doesn't assess Theseus's team crypto background, and Lit Protocol's TEE-based approach is a credible alternative they could adopt without the ZKP complexity.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path; agents hold their own keys and persist on-chain — no documented identity/auth layer beyond raw EdDSA keypairs as of the call context.
Lit Protocol provides threshold-cryptography programmable signing for agents but relies on TEE attestation rather than ZKP selective disclosure, leaving capability privacy unaddressed.
Turnkey is a secure enclave key management service for wallets; scoped to key custody, not agent capability attestation or selective permission disclosure.
Privy is an embedded wallet SDK targeting human end-users; no agent-native or ZKP capability disclosure primitives.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + Verifiable Credentials spec (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: DID service endpoints and VC selective disclosure (BBS+ signatures) are the closest standards analog to what the opportunity describes, but lack ZKP proof binding to an on-chain verifier.
Early IETF draft defining OAuth 2.0 flows for AI agents — covers delegated authorization but not capability privacy or on-chain attestation anchoring.

Fetch.ai's Agentverse has an agent marketplace with capability advertising via manifest files, but uses no ZKP; capability claims are plain-text and unverifiable without trust in the publishing agent.
SingularityNET marketplace lists AI services with metadata but has no selective disclosure or ZKP-based capability attestation; services self-certify capabilities.
Semaphore provides ZK group membership proofs deployed on Ethereum mainnet; Bolyra HumanUniqueness reuses its ceremony, making its on-chain verifier pattern the closest deployed ZKP identity analog.
Polygon ID supports ZKP-based credential verification on-chain but is human/VC-centric; no agent-to-agent selective capability disclosure primitive exists.
Autonolas (Olas) runs multi-agent services on-chain with staking and on-chain service registry, but identity is service-level and key-based — no selective disclosure of per-agent capabilities.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive. The selective disclosure capability — 'proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without revealing full permissionBitmask' — is the exact mechanism needed. An agent advertising 'financial services up to $100' calls proveHandshake with requiredScopeMask=0x04 (bit 2 only); the PLONK proof commits to the scope without exposing bits 5-7. The identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3) ties the attestation to the specific credential, preventing transferability.

On-chain attestation anchor at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db. Theseus marketplace indexers can call verifyProof() directly to validate any capability attestation without trusting the advertising agent. Proof is portable — generated once, verified N times by any counterparty.

agentTree root and root history buffer (30 entries) provide the Merkle membership anchor for the selective disclosure proof. Any counterparty verifying an attestation needs a valid agentTree root; the history buffer handles clock skew between proof generation and verification without requiring real-time root sync.

DID method is the discovery layer. Theseus marketplace indexers resolve did:bolyra:<agentId> and read service endpoints.
