Needs a new service endpoint type (CapabilityAttestation) that carries the selective disclosure proof, the requiredScopeMask it asserts, and a PlonkVerifier contract reference. The DID document becomes the marketplace listing card.

createAgentCredential() issues the underlying credential. proveHandshake() drives the PLONK proof generation path. Need a thin SDK wrapper that extracts just the selective disclosure proof leg (AgentPolicy proof with a caller-supplied requiredScopeMask) without requiring a paired HumanUniqueness proof — marketplace attestation is agent-only, not a mutual handshake.

IETF draft's proof envelope format and content-type headers can be extended to cover single-leg capability attestations (not just mutual handshakes). Theseus indexers consuming the attestation endpoint benefit from a standardized wire format they can validate without Bolyra-specific tooling.

new_components_needed.description: did:bolyra DID document extension: define 'CapabilityAttestation' service endpoint type. Fields: proofHex (AgentPolicy PLONK proof), requiredScopeMask (uint8, the bits being asserted), agentTreeRoot (the Merkle root used), proofTimestamp, verifierContract (PlonkVerifier address + chainId). Schema goes into spec/did-method-bolyra.md and test-vectors.json (add ~5 vectors covering single-bit, multi-bit, and boundary cases).

New SDK function: proveCapabilityDisclosure(credential: AgentCredential, requiredScopeMask: bigint, agentTreeRoot: bigint) → CapabilityAttestation. Wraps the AgentPolicy PLONK proof path without the handshake nonce pairing. Returns a JSON-serializable object matching the DID service endpoint schema. Also add verifyCapabilityAttestation(attestation, agentTreeRoot) → boolean for counterparty verification without the full SDK credential context.

DID document publication workflow: a CLI command or SDK call that (1) generates the CapabilityAttestation proof, (2) serializes it into a DID document service endpoint, (3) publishes it to a resolvable endpoint (IPFS CID anchored in a registry mapping, or a lightweight HTTP DID resolver). Theseus indexers need a deterministic resolution path. Define the resolution algorithm as an addendum to spec/did-method-bolyra.md.

Theseus marketplace adapter: a TypeScript module (integrations/theseus/) that implements the indexer read side — resolves a did:bolyra DID document, finds CapabilityAttestation service endpoints, calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Base Sepolia, and returns a typed CapabilityReport. This is the bridge Theseus calls when an agent lists itself or a counterparty queries capabilities before transacting.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) On-chain verifier — Theseus marketplace contracts or indexers call PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia to validate any CapabilityAttestation without trusting the advertising agent or Bolyra infrastructure. (2) DID resolution — Theseus indexers resolve did:bolyra:<agentId> via the did:bolyra resolution algorithm (to be published as addendum to spec/did-method-bolyra.md), read CapabilityAttestation service endpoints, and build their capability index. (3) SDK bridge — agent operators building on Theseus call proveCapabilityDisclosure() from @bolyra/sdk to generate and refresh their marketplace listing proofs; the integrations/theseus/ adapter handles the Theseus-side read path. The IdentityRegistry agentTree root (with its 30-entry history buffer) serves as the shared state anchor that both sides trust without requiring a live Bolyra API call.

deliverables.name: CapabilityAttestation DID service endpoint spec
deliverables.description: Extend did:bolyra DID document with a CapabilityAttestation service endpoint type. Fields: proofHex (AgentPolicy PLONK proof bytes), requiredScopeMask (uint8 bitmask being asserted), verifierContract (PlonkVerifier address), agentTreeRoot (Merkle root at proof time), expiresAt (unix timestamp). Defines JSON-LD context fragment and resolution semantics for Theseus indexers.

deliverables.name: proveCapability() SDK wrapper
deliverables.description: Single-leg selective disclosure path in @bolyra/sdk. Takes (agentCredential, requiredScopeMask) and returns an AgentPolicy PLONK proof + structured attestation object without requiring a paired HumanUniqueness proof. Strips the mutual-handshake coupling from proveHandshake() and exposes the AgentPolicy proof leg directly. Outputs attestation object ready for embedding in DID document service endpoint.

deliverables.name: Marketplace attestation wire format
deliverables.description: Extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 proof envelope to cover single-leg capability attestations. New content-type: application/bolyra-capability-attestation+json. Defines HTTP GET response schema for the DID service endpoint URL so Theseus indexers can validate without Bolyra SDK. Includes verification pseudocode for an EVM-native indexer calling verifyProof() directly.

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: Single Node.js script: (1) createAgentCredential with bits 0-4 set, (2) call proveCapability(credential, 0x04) to produce PLONK proof asserting only FINANCIAL_SMALL without leaking SIGN_ON_BEHALF/SUB_DELEGATE/ACCESS_PII, (3) print JSON attestation object shaped like the new DID service endpoint, (4) call PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Base Sepolia and log tx hash. Runnable in 60 seconds from cold start. Uses existing deployed verifier — no new contract deploy needed.

deliverables.name: Theseus indexer integration guide
deliverables.description: One-page reference for Theseus marketplace indexers: how to resolve did:bolyra:<agentId>, parse CapabilityAttestation service endpoints, call verifyProof() against the anchored PlonkVerifier, and handle root history buffer skew (up to 30 stale roots accepted). Includes curl examples and Solidity snippet for on-chain counterparty verification.

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: generate an AgentPolicy PLONK proof for scope mask 0x04 (FINANCIAL_SMALL only), show the proof does NOT reveal bits 5-7 (SIGN_ON_BEHALF, SUB_DELEGATE, ACCESS_PII), call verifyProof() on Base Sepolia (existing deployed verifier, no new deploy), print the DID document JSON with the CapabilityAttestation service endpoint populated. Takes 60-90 seconds end-to-end. Theseus team sees the full flow — agent advertises, counterparty verifies on-chain — without any dependency on Theseus infrastructure.
risks: Theseus may not be EVM-compatible — PlonkVerifier is Solidity; if they run a custom VM the on-chain anchor needs a port or they must call Base Sepolia as an external verification oracle
proveHandshake() in current SDK tightly couples both proof legs — extracting the AgentPolicy-only path may surface undocumented snarkjs witness generation assumptions that add a day
Theseus marketplace indexer protocol is undocumented; the DID service endpoint design may need rework once their actual indexer query format is known

PLONK proof size (~800 bytes) + DID document resolution adds latency to agent discovery; acceptable for marketplace listing but not for per-transaction authorization — scope this clearly to avoid over-promising
Solo founder bandwidth: 3.5 days assumes zero interruptions; realistic calendar is 5-6 days given other product work
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus marketplace indexer (or a script simulating one) can resolve did:bolyra:<agentId>, fetch the CapabilityAttestation service endpoint, call PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() with the provided proof and public signals, receive true, and confirm the asserted scopeMask matches the advertised capability — without the agent revealing its full permissionBitmask at any point in the flow.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Need from Theseus before full integration: (1) confirmation of EVM compatibility or their VM's foreign-call mechanism for external contract verification, (2) testnet RPC endpoint + faucet so a bolyra-registered PlonkVerifier can optionally be deployed there instead of Base Sepolia, (3) their marketplace indexer's DID resolution protocol or service endpoint query format (REST? GraphQL? on-chain registry?), (4) any existing agent identity schema they are already using so CapabilityAttestation service type does not collide. Sunday call goal: get answers to (1) and (3) — (2) and (4) can follow async.

title: ZKP delegation chain proof appended to x402 payment receipts for audit and dispute
description: Base's Builder Codes (ERC-8021) establish on-chain attribution for x402 payments, but attribution is not authorization — knowing which agent paid tells you nothing about whether it was permitted to pay, on whose behalf, or under what delegation chain. Bolyra can generate a compact ZKP that attaches to an x402 payment receipt and proves 'this payment was authorized by a valid, unrevoked delegation chain from root identity X with permission bit FINANCIAL_MEDIUM active at time T' without revealing X or the intermediate delegators. This is the audit and dispute primitive that enterprise operators and DeFi protocols will require before letting autonomous agents access high-value services. The x402 Foundation's Linux Foundation backing (Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard) signals regulatory scrutiny is coming; authorization proofs on payment receipts position Bolyra as the compliance layer for autonomous agent commerce.

rationale.agent_need: The attribution-vs-authorization distinction for x402 receipts is a structurally sound argument, and x402's Linux Foundation backing signals enterprise scrutiny is coming, but Theseus-specific evidence is absent (web search unavailable) so the claim that Theseus agents specifically need this remains inferred rather than cited.
rationale.zkp_edge: The ZKP edge is genuine and well-argued: a non-ZKP approach (EdDSA + on-chain ACL) either leaks the full delegation chain or requires O(n) on-chain lookups per hop, while the ZKP proves 'authorized by valid chain from root X with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM active at time T' without revealing X or intermediaries — a structural privacy and scalability win.
rationale.primitive_readiness: The Delegation + AgentPolicy circuits cover the core predicate, but the gap (no payment-context binding inputs) requires a major Delegation circuit modification, new zkeys from pot16.ptau, a regenerated Solidity verifier, and a new SDK export — realistically 10+ days of work with non-trivial ceremony overhead, which is correctly scoped but makes this a build rather than a near-term deploy.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The Circom/ZKP expertise gap is a real barrier for most chains, and the x402 compliance angle creates bilateral value, but the assessment that Theseus cannot build this in <3 months is speculative without confirming their team's crypto background, and the existence of MPC alternatives (Lit, Turnkey) that could partially satisfy enterprise audit requirements weakens the exclusivity argument.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path; agents hold their own keys and are first-class citizens, implying raw EdDSA keypairs with no delegation primitive as of the call date.
Could not retrieve Theseus GitHub repos, docs, or community discussions to confirm current identity/auth implementation details.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol (litprotocol.com) — known from training data
evidence.summary: Lit provides programmable key management and MPC-based signing for agents but does not offer ZKP delegation chains or on-chain permission-bit enforcement.
Turnkey offers sub-organization wallet scoping for agents but is custodial MPC, not ZKP-based, and has no delegation circuit with scope narrowing.
Privy targets human-rooted embedded wallets and has no agent-native delegation or ZKP authorization primitive.
Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication is active; no finalized spec for agent-to-agent delegation yet, leaving the standards gap Bolyra can anchor.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / SingularityNET — known from training data
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai uses DID-based agent identifiers with on-chain registration but no ZKP delegation or cumulative permission encoding; SingularityNET relies on reputation oracles, not proofs.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / Worldcoin — known from training data
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is the closest deployed ZKP identity primitive on Ethereum mainnet, but it is human-uniqueness only — no agent policy or delegation circuit exists in that stack.

evidence.source: x402 Foundation / Linux Foundation backing (provided context)
evidence.summary: x402 has Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard backing; regulatory scrutiny on agent payment authorization is imminent, creating pull for audit-grade delegation proofs on receipts.
ERC-8021 provides on-chain attribution (who paid) but explicitly not authorization (was the agent permitted to pay), confirming the gap Bolyra's delegation proof fills.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core cryptographic engine for the payment authorization proof. Already proves an unrevoked chain (human→agent→sub-agent, max 3 hops) with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM bit (bit 3, which implies bit 2 by cumulative encoding) active at a committed timestamp, without revealing the root identity or intermediate delegators. The identity-bound scope commitment chain and delegator key binding (UC3.1 fix) directly satisfy the 'authorized by valid delegation chain from root identity X' predicate. The liveness check (currentTimestamp < delegateeExpiry) anchors the proof to time T. Gap: circuit currently has no public input that commits to payment-specific parameters (amount, recipient address, tx/receipt hash), so a detached proof could be replayed against a different payment event.

Proves the paying agent holds FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) with selective disclosure—only the required scope mask is revealed, not the full bitmask. The identity-bound scope commitment ties permission to the specific credential commitment, preventing credential substitution. Expiry enforcement and EdDSA operator signature verification are already in-circuit. This circuit runs in parallel with Delegation to prove both 'agent is credentialed' and 'agent is delegated' simultaneously, matching the handshake pattern.

Root history buffers (30 entries each for humanTree and agentTree) are the critical on-chain primitive here: a payment receipt must reference the Merkle root that was valid at payment time T, not the current root. The 30-entry buffer gives a sliding window to validate historical proofs. Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents the same authorization proof from being submitted twice for two different payments. The existing delegation chain-state tracking on-chain maps cleanly to audit trail requirements.

Already deployed at 0x285D2E315AdD9c82337d8a4BDF567AB3a9215B93 on Base Sepolia. x402 receipt verifiers can call this contract directly to check the PLONK proof attached to a payment receipt. PLONK's universal SRS means no per-payment trusted setup. This is the on-chain verification endpoint that x402 facilitators or Theseus infrastructure would invoke.

integrations/payment-protocols/src/spend-policy.ts already exists and implements budget/spend-policy enforcement logic. This is the SDK-level integration surface where payment authorization proofs get wired: SpendPolicy can be extended to call provePaymentAuthorization() before releasing an x402 HTTP 402 challenge response, attaching the Bolyra delegation proof to the receipt. The conformance runner structure there can host x402-specific test vectors.

new_components_needed.description: PaymentCommitmentBinding: extend Delegation.circom with a new public input `paymentCommitment = Poseidon4(paymentAmount, recipientAddress, receiptHash, paymentTimestamp)`. This binds the delegation proof to one specific payment event—a detached proof is cryptographically unusable on a different receipt. Implemented as a thin wrapper template around the existing Delegation circuit rather than a full rewrite. Requires new .zkey ceremony (pot16.ptau is sufficient at 22k constraints + ~2k new constraints).

X402AuthorizationVerifier: a Solidity contract that x402 facilitators and Theseus call. Accepts (receiptHash, delegationProof, agentProof, merkleRoot, permissionMask) and (1) calls DelegationPlonkVerifier.verifyProof(), (2) checks merkleRoot is in IdentityRegistry's root history buffer, (3) verifies paymentCommitment = Poseidon4(receiptHash params), (4) emits PaymentAuthorized(nullifier, scopeMask, expiryTimestamp) for the on-chain audit log. Wraps existing verifier contracts—no new cryptography.

provePaymentAuthorization(delegationChain, paymentParams): new SDK function that (1) resolves the active Merkle root at payment time from IdentityRegistry history, (2) computes paymentCommitment from payment params, (3) generates the extended Delegation PLONK proof with payment binding, (4) serializes to a compact base64url blob for x402 header attachment. Requires completing the delegate() v0.3 stub first—that's the primary dependency. Also needs a deserializePaymentProof() counterpart for verifiers.

X-Bolyra-Payment-Authorization HTTP header spec: defines the wire format for attaching a Bolyra proof to an x402 payment receipt. Fields: proof (base64url PLONK proof), publicSignals (paymentCommitment, nullifier, scopeMask, expiryTimestamp, merkleRoot), bolyraVersion. Extends draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a new PaymentAuthorization proof type. Needed so x402 facilitators know what to parse and forward to X402AuthorizationVerifier.

ERC-8021 (Builder Codes) bridge: on-chain mapping from Bolyra nullifier to Base Builder Code attribution. When X402AuthorizationVerifier emits PaymentAuthorized, a bridge contract reads the Builder Code from the tx and records (builderCode → bolyraRootNullifier) for attribution-plus-authorization correlation. Enables compliance queries like 'show all payments by root identity X within delegation scope FINANCIAL_MEDIUM in Q3'. Complexity is high because ERC-8021 attribution events and Bolyra nullifiers have different privacy models that need reconciliation.

theseus_integration_surface: Theseus (theseuschain.com) sits between x402 payment facilitators and autonomous agents. Bolyra plugs in at two surfaces. First, the agent-side SDK surface: before an agent submits an x402 402-response, it calls provePaymentAuthorization(delegationChain, {amount, recipient, receiptHash, timestamp}) via @bolyra/sdk, receives a serialized PLONK proof, and attaches it as the X-Bolyra-Payment-Authorization header. Theseus forwards this header alongside the payment. Second, the facilitator-side verifier surface: the x402 facilitator (or Theseus's settlement layer) calls X402AuthorizationVerifier.verifyPaymentAuthorization(receiptHash, proof, publicSignals) on Base Sepolia before accepting the payment as authorized. The verifier contract calls DelegationPlonkVerifier (already deployed), checks IdentityRegistry root history for the claimed Merkle root at payment time T, and emits the PaymentAuthorized event for the audit log. The ERC-8021 bridge optionally correlates the emitted nullifier to Builder Code attribution. Theseus never learns the root human identity or intermediate delegator keys—only the scope mask, nullifier, and expiry timestamp appear as public signals.

deliverables.name: PaymentContextBinding.circom patch
deliverables.description: Add three public inputs to Delegation circuit: paymentAmount (u64), recipientAddress (field), receiptHash (field). Commitment H(amount||recipient||receiptHash||nonce) becomes a 4th public output, binding the authorization proof to a specific payment event and preventing replay across different receipts. Requires recompile + new groth16/plonk zkeys from existing pot16.ptau.

deliverables.name: Updated DelegationVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: Regenerate Solidity verifier from new vkey.json after circuit recompile. Add verifyPaymentAuthorization(proof, publicSignals, receiptHash) entrypoint that checks the paymentContextCommitment signal matches the provided receipt. Deploy to Base Sepolia and — if Theseus is EVM-compatible — to Theseus testnet. If Theseus uses a custom VM, this becomes a verification adapter spec only.
deliverables.reuses: DelegationPlonkVerifier at 0x285D2E315AdD9c82337d8a4BDF567AB3a9215B93 — redeploy with new vkey

deliverables.name: provePaymentAuthorization() SDK function
deliverables.description: New export in @bolyra/sdk that takes (delegationChain, paymentContext: {amount, recipient, receiptHash}, sessionNonce) and returns a PaymentAuthProof struct. Wires through the modified Delegation circuit via snarkjs (dev) or rapidsnark (prod). Includes proof serialization to a compact bytes format suitable for appending to x402 receipt headers.
deliverables.reuses: sdk/src/delegation.ts + existing proveHandshake pattern — moderate extension

deliverables.name: SpendPolicy.proveBeforeRelease() extension
deliverables.description: Extend integrations/payment-protocols/src/spend-policy.ts to call provePaymentAuthorization() before releasing an x402 HTTP 402→200 payment. Attach the compact proof bytes to the x402 Receipt header as X-Bolyra-Auth-Proof. Add verifyPaymentAuthProof() on the receiver side. Covers the FINANCIAL_MEDIUM bit enforcement path.

deliverables.name: Theseus Agent Key Bridge spec + adapter stub
deliverables.description: Theseus agents hold raw EdDSA keypairs with no delegation primitive. This spec defines how a Theseus agent key (Ed25519 pubkey) maps to a Bolyra AgentCredential: operator signs the agent's pubkey hash as the modelHash field, issuing a credential with the desired permission bitmask. The adapter stub in sdk/ reads a Theseus-format agent identity JSON and emits a Bolyra credential. BLOCKED on Theseus providing their agent identity schema.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy circuit — none needed, existing circuit handles EdDSA signature verification already

deliverables.name: x402 Payment Auth Proof — spec doc
deliverables.description: One-page IETF-style spec defining the X-Bolyra-Auth-Proof header format: CBOR-encoded {proof_type, public_signals, pi_a, pi_b, pi_c, payment_context_commitment, chain_id, registry_address}. Covers Base Sepolia and Theseus testnet variants. This is the artifact to share with Theseus team on the call and propose as a joint standard.
deliverables.reuses: spec/draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md — new section, minimal new work

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo script
deliverables.description: Node.js script: (1) create a Theseus-style agent credential via bridge adapter, (2) call provePaymentAuthorization() for a $500 simulated payment (within FINANCIAL_MEDIUM), (3) attach proof to x402 receipt header, (4) call DelegationVerifier.verifyPaymentAuthorization() on Base Sepolia and log the boolean result. Runnable in < 60s with mock proofs, ~3min with full Groth16. This is the Sunday call demo.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call (tomorrow): show existing Delegation + AgentPolicy circuits running in fast mode (witness-only, no full proof). Walk through the architecture diagram: Theseus agent key → Bolyra credential → payment auth proof → x402 receipt header → on-chain verifier. Show the deployed DelegationPlonkVerifier on Base Sepolia via Basescan. Present the X-Bolyra-Auth-Proof header spec as a draft and propose co-authoring. The circuit modification and full end-to-end demo are 2 weeks out — frame Sunday as a technical alignment call, not a live integration demo.
risks: Theseus VM is not EVM-compatible: DelegationVerifier.sol cannot deploy directly; need to ship proof bytes off-chain and verify on Base Sepolia instead, adding a cross-chain round-trip that hurts UX and is a harder sell.

Circuit recompile with new public inputs requires regenerating zkeys from pot16.ptau (~20min local, non-parallelizable); if pot16.ptau has wrong constraint capacity the build fails and needs a new ceremony — check constraint count before starting.

Theseus agent key schema is unknown: if agents use something other than Ed25519 (e.g. secp256k1 or a custom VM key type), the AgentPolicy circuit's EdDSA verifier doesn't apply and a new constraint subcircuit is needed (~3 extra days).

Solo founder context-switching cost: Delegation circuit modification requires uninterrupted deep work (Circom constraint debugging is non-linear). Interleaving with ZKProva/GeniusComply tasks will double the real-calendar time.
x402 is not yet stable: the Linux Foundation spec is still in draft; the receipt format may change before Bolyra's header extension ships, requiring a spec update pass.
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus agent submits an x402 payment; the receiving service calls DelegationVerifier.verifyPaymentAuthorization(proof, receiptHash) on-chain and gets true; a replayed proof against a different receiptHash returns false. The proof generation adds < 5s latency for snarkjs (dev) and < 300ms for rapidsnark (prod). Theseus team can reproduce the demo using the bridge adapter with their own agent keys.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Agent identity schema: exact format of their EdDSA keypairs and any on-chain agent registration struct. 2) Testnet RPC + faucet access if they want the verifier deployed on their chain. 3) Confirmation of VM type (EVM-compatible or custom) — this is the single highest-leverage question for Sunday. 4) x402 facilitator they're planning to integrate with (Base x402, custom) so the receipt format is known. 5) Willingness to co-author the X-Bolyra-Auth-Proof spec as a joint Bolyra+Theseus proposal to the x402 Foundation.

title: Agent-rooted Delegation circuit: L1 genesis as root of trust
description: Bolyra's existing Delegation circuit hard-assumes a human delegator — the delegator key is bound to a HumanUniqueness credential commitment. Theseus agents have no human key path; the root of trust is the agent's on-chain genesis transaction. A new AgentDelegation circuit variant is needed where the root delegator is validated against the Theseus L1 genesis block commitment rather than a Semaphore-style human tree root. The existing scope-narrowing logic (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope, expiry monotonicity, max-3-hop chain) carries over with ~3k additional constraints to verify the L1 genesis commitment. This is the single most important architectural gap: without it, Bolyra cannot support any agent-to-agent delegation on Theseus at all.

rationale.agent_need: The structural argument is compelling — if Theseus is truly agent-native L1 with no human key path, the current Delegation circuit literally cannot function as a delegator root — but the evidence relies on training-knowledge characterizations of Theseus rather than cited documentation or codebase, weakening the required evidence standard.
rationale.zkp_edge: Correctly names Lit Protocol (no circuit-level scope narrowing), Turnkey (custodial trusted third party), and Fetch.ai (replay/impersonation gaps) as structural failures, but does not address whether a simple on-chain ACL contract with Merkle inclusion proofs could provide 80% of the value without ZKP overhead.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Names specific circuits (Delegation.circom fork, AgentPolicy), contracts (IdentityRegistry new enrollment path, new verifiers), and a plausible 2-week timeline, but the entire work is gated on a critical external dependency — Theseus must share their genesis block commitment format before a single constraint can be written, making the timeline speculative.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Correctly identifies ZKP circuit design and Circom proficiency as the expertise gap and notes competitor alternatives (Lit, Turnkey) Theseus could adopt instead, but does not assess Theseus's actual crypto team composition or whether they have an identity solution already in progress, leaving the 'they can't build this themselves' claim unsubstantiated.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human-controlled key path; agents hold their own keys and persist on-chain, creating a structural gap with all human-rooted identity systems including Bolyra's current Delegation circuit.

Lit Protocol provides programmable key management and access control for agents using threshold cryptography, but does not use ZKP delegation chains or cumulative-bit permission encoding — no circuit-level scope narrowing.
Turnkey offers API-driven key management with policy enforcement for agents, but is custodial and trust-anchored to Turnkey's infrastructure, not to an L1 genesis commitment.
Privy targets human wallet UX (embedded wallets, social login) and has limited agent-native primitives; not designed for agent-rooted trust chains.

Fetch.ai runs an agent-native network where agents register on-chain and communicate via the Agent Communication Protocol (ACP); delegation is handled via agent-signed messages rather than ZKP circuits, creating exploitable replay/impersonation gaps.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace mechanics; identity is service-level API-key gating, not cryptographic agent delegation.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent / OAuth 2.0 for AI agents (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF drafts on AI agent authorization propose OAuth-style token delegation but assume a human principal at the root — no standard yet covers agent-genesis-rooted delegation chains, which is exactly the gap a Theseus-native AgentDelegation circuit would fill.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / Worldcoin ZKP identity (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is the most deployed ZKP identity primitive on L1s (Ethereum mainnet), but its Merkle tree root is human-biometric-anchored; no variant exists that substitutes an L1 genesis block commitment as the root of trust.

evidence.source: Aztec / Noir ZKP on L1 (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Aztec's Noir language enables privacy-preserving circuits on Ethereum, but no published circuit addresses agent-genesis delegation chains; the tooling is available for Bolyra to build this without competition.
Polygon ID supports W3C Verifiable Credentials with ZKP, but is human-credential-centric and does not model agent autonomy or genesis-block trust anchors.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core scope-narrowing logic (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope, expiry monotonicity, max-3-hop chain, delegation nullifier replay protection, EdDSA delegation token signature) carries over verbatim. The circuit template is the starting point for AgentDelegation — only the root-delegator identity check changes.

The root delegator in AgentDelegation is an agent credential, not a human. AgentPolicy's identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) and EdDSA operator signature verification become the delegator identity anchor, replacing the HumanUniqueness Merkle root check that the existing Delegation circuit uses.

Not applicable as delegator root. Its Semaphore v4 Merkle tree and nullifier construction are human-specific. However, mixed human→agent delegation chains that terminate at a Theseus agent still need HumanUniqueness for any human-origin hop, so the circuit interface must remain compatible with the existing handshake tuple.

The agentTree (dual LeanIMT, 30-entry root history buffer) already tracks enrolled agent commitments on Base Sepolia. For agent-rooted delegation, the genesis commitment from Theseus L1 must either (a) be mirrored into agentTree via a bridge-attested enrollment, or (b) verified out-of-band before the delegation proof is accepted. The existing delegationVerify path and hop-count enforcement reuse with a new entry point that accepts an agent-root proof instead of a human-root proof.

Cannot reuse directly — the new AgentDelegation circuit will generate a different R1CS/constraint system. A new PLONK verifier contract must be generated from the new zkey. The existing PlonkVerifier address is circuit-specific.

new_components_needed.description: AgentDelegation.circom: fork of Delegation.circom replacing the delegator HumanUniqueness Merkle check with (1) AgentPolicy-style EdDSA operator signature over the root credential commitment, and (2) a Theseus L1 genesis block commitment verifier (~3k additional constraints, likely Poseidon-hashed block header fields or a compact Merkle path into the genesis state trie). Scope-narrowing, expiry, hop-count, and nullifier replay logic copy verbatim. Dual Groth16+PLONK build required for ceremony flexibility.

Theseus Genesis Commitment Interface Spec: define the exact fields from the Theseus L1 genesis block that serve as the root-of-trust anchor (block hash, state root, agent registry commitment), their encoding as ZK-friendly field elements (Baby Jubjub / BN254 scalar field), and the on-chain attestation mechanism (oracle, storage proof, or bridge message). Without this spec the circuit cannot be implemented — this is the critical unknown.
AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol: generated from the new AgentDelegation zkey via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deploy alongside existing verifiers on Base Sepolia.

IdentityRegistry extension (agentDelegationVerify): new method or overloaded verify path that accepts (agentRootProof, delegationChainProofs[], nonce) instead of (humanProof, agentProof, nonce). Must validate Theseus genesis commitment is in the trusted genesis anchor set (owner-controlled mapping), check hop count ≤ 3, enforce delegation nullifiers, and emit the same events as existing verifyHandshake for indexer compatibility.

Implement delegate() stub for agent-rooted path: createAgentDelegation(rootCredential, delegateeCredential, scopeMask, expiry) → AgentDelegationResult. Wire to AgentDelegation circuit artifacts, add ThesusBridgeAdapter that fetches and caches the genesis commitment from Theseus RPC, expose verifyAgentDelegationChain(). Add AgentDelegationError and GenesisMismatchError to error class hierarchy.

Theseus bridge adapter: lightweight module that reads the Theseus L1 genesis block commitment via Theseus RPC/REST, converts it to BN254 field elements, and caches it with a configurable TTL. Used by the SDK proving path and optionally by the IdentityRegistry oracle updater.

theseus_integration_surface: Two viable surfaces: (A) Verifier-on-Base-Sepolia with oracle bridge — Bolyra's IdentityRegistry stays on Base Sepolia; a trusted oracle (or Theseus→Base bridge message) writes the Theseus L1 genesis commitment into a GenesisAnchorRegistry contract; the AgentDelegation circuit proves membership against this anchored value; no cross-chain proving required. (B) Native Theseus deployment — deploy AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier and a minimal IdentityRegistry fork directly on Theseus L1 so the genesis commitment is a local constant (block.chainid + genesis hash available natively); agents prove delegation entirely on-chain without bridging. Surface A is faster (reuses existing Base Sepolia infra, 2-week sprint feasible) but introduces oracle trust. Surface B is cleaner (genesis commitment is a chain constant, zero oracle risk) but requires Theseus EVM compatibility verification and a new deployment pipeline.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegation spec + Theseus genesis commitment format
deliverables.description: Document the wire format for Theseus L1 genesis block commitment (block hash fields, Poseidon-hashed header, or compact Merkle path). Defines the public signal interface AgentDelegation.circom will consume. Requires Theseus to share genesis block structure. This is the critical dependency that gates all circuit work.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegation.circom
deliverables.description: Fork of Delegation.circom. Replaces the HumanUniqueness Merkle root check with: (1) AgentPolicy-style EdDSA operator signature over the root credential commitment, and (2) Poseidon-hash verification of the Theseus L1 genesis block commitment (~3k additional constraints). Scope-narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope), expiry monotonicity, max-3-hop chain, and nullifier replay protection copy verbatim. Dual Groth16+PLONK build. Will use pot16.ptau for Groth16 trusted setup — no new ceremony needed if constraint count stays under 2^16.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation.circom (scope-narrowing, expiry, hop-count, nullifier logic — ~70% of constraints)

deliverables.name: AgentDelegationGroth16Verifier.sol + AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: Regenerated Solidity verifier contracts from the new AgentDelegation zkey. Cannot reuse existing DelegationPlonkVerifier — different R1CS. Generated via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier from the new vkey.json. Hardhat integration test confirms verifyProof accepts valid witness and rejects tampered scope.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry: agent-root enrollment path
deliverables.description: New entry point on IdentityRegistry that accepts a bridge-attested Theseus genesis commitment and inserts it into the agentTree (existing dual LeanIMT, 30-entry root history buffer). Adds an enrollAgentGenesis(bytes32 genesisCommitment, bytes calldata bridgeAttestation) method. Bridge attestation is initially a multisig (2-of-3 Theseus team keys) pending a trustless bridge. Existing delegationVerify path reused with a flag selecting agent-root vs human-root entry.

deliverables.name: SDK: agentDelegation() API methods
deliverables.description: Two new TypeScript methods on @bolyra/sdk: createAgentDelegation(rootCredential, delegateeCredential, scopeBitmask, expiry, genesisCommitment) and verifyAgentDelegation(proof, nonce). Thin wrappers over snarkjs prover using the new AgentDelegation zkey. Existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake session-nonce binding reused for the handshake layer.
deliverables.reuses: createAgentCredential, proveHandshake patterns (proving pipeline, nonce binding)

deliverables.name: Theseus integration demo
deliverables.description: Node script demonstrating a 2-hop agent delegation on Base Sepolia testnet: root agent (Theseus genesis commitment enrolled via multisig) → delegatee agent (narrowed scope bitmask) → proof verification on-chain. Shows the score from 76 (CONSIDER) is actionable: scope-narrowing works, replay fails, expired delegation rejected. Requires Theseus testnet genesis commitment or a mock commitment for demo purposes.
deliverables.reuses: examples/mcp-demo pattern + existing Hardhat testnet scripts

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday June 29 call: (1) live demo of the existing Delegation circuit showing scope-narrowing and max-3-hop enforcement — proves the core primitive works; (2) AgentDelegation.circom scaffold open in editor showing the exact lines being changed (the HumanUniqueness Merkle check replaced by EdDSA + genesis hash verify); (3) the spec doc showing the wire format Theseus needs to expose. Nothing deployed yet — but the gap is precisely scoped and the delta from existing code is visibly small. The ask of Theseus: share genesis block Poseidon-hash format so spec can be finalized on the call.
risks: Theseus genesis commitment format is unknown — if they use a non-Poseidon hash (e.g. keccak256 of raw RLP), adding a keccak gadget in Circom adds ~5k constraints and complexity; resolve on the call
pot16.ptau caps at 2^16 constraints — if AgentDelegation exceeds this (unlikely but possible with complex genesis Merkle path), a new Powers of Tau ceremony is needed, adding 2-3 days
Bridge attestation is initially a multisig trust assumption — trustless bridge from Theseus L1 to Base Sepolia is a separate multi-month engineering effort; demo must be honest about this
Solo capacity: circuit work is sequential and debugging Circom witness errors is unpredictable — 8-day estimate for AgentDelegation.circom has ±3 day variance
Theseus testnet may not exist yet — demo may need a mock genesis commitment if their chain is not live
spec.success_criteria: A 2-hop agent delegation proof verifies on-chain (Base Sepolia) with: (a) delegateeScope strictly ⊆ delegatorScope enforced in-circuit, (b) replay of the same proof with a fresh nonce fails, (c) a tampered genesis commitment causes proof verification to reject, (d) SDK round-trip (createAgentDelegation → verifyAgentDelegation) completes in under 30 seconds using snarkjs on a laptop
spec.theseus_dependencies: Critical path item 1: genesis block commitment format — specifically which fields are hashed, what hash function (Poseidon preferred; keccak adds circuit complexity), and byte ordering. Item 2: a testnet or staging environment where agents have on-chain genesis transactions we can use to generate test commitment values. Item 3: a 2-of-3 multisig signing authority for bridge attestations during the demo phase (their team keys). Nice-to-have: their agent credential schema (public key format, on-chain registration record structure) so AgentPolicy's EdDSA signature check binds to real Theseus keypairs.

title: Chain-scoped nullifier binding for Theseus↔Base cross-chain agent auth
description: Bolyra's existing nullifier scheme hashes (credentialCommitment, sessionNonce) but is chain-blind — a Theseus agent credential proof replayed on Base Sepolia would pass verification today. Adding chain_id as a public input to AgentPolicy's nullifier hash (nullifier = H(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chain_id)) creates cryptographically-enforced domain separation at the circuit level. This means a Theseus agent can present the same underlying credential on multiple chains without the ZKP verifier accepting cross-chain replays. The fix is a single-line Circom change to AgentPolicy and a matching chain_id public signal in the Solidity verifier — but the protocol guarantee is fundamental to safe multi-chain operation. Without this, any cross-chain Bolyra deployment is vulnerable to replay across chains sharing the same BN254 curve.

rationale.agent_need: The Theseus 'no human key path' structural constraint is cited as motivation, but the cross-chain replay vulnerability is inherent to any multi-chain ZKP deployment — not specific to Theseus agents — and no Theseus documentation or community discussion directly evidences demand for chain-scoped nullifiers.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (wallet-level key scoping as used by Coinbase AgentKit) is named and its structural failure mode identified — it cannot enforce domain separation at proof-verification time without trusting the wallet layer — making the ZKP circuit-level guarantee genuinely superior.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Exact circuits (AgentPolicy.circom, Delegation.circom), contract functions (PlonkVerifier, DelegationPlonkVerifier), and SDK surface are named with realistic per-deliverable day estimates; PLONK's universal SRS avoids ceremony overhead, making the 2-day total credible.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this in 3 months relies on assumed ZKP inexperience with no assessment of their team's crypto background, and the availability of Lit Protocol and Coinbase AgentKit as drop-in alternatives weakens the exclusivity claim.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (fetch blocked — context provided by caller)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 funded by Orange DAO; its 'no human key path' constraint means agents hold raw EdDSA keypairs with no human root — creating a structural identity gap Bolyra can fill.

Lit offers MPC-based programmable key pairs with cross-chain support for agents, but relies on threshold signatures rather than ZKP — no nullifier-level domain separation or permission-bit delegation circuits.

Coinbase AgentKit/CDP agentic wallets provide key custody for AI agents on Base, but offer no ZKP layer — cross-chain replay is addressed only by wallet-level key scoping, not cryptographic circuit enforcement.
Turnkey provides secure HSM-backed key management for agents and enterprises but is human-policy-centric with no ZKP delegation model or chain-scoped nullifier scheme.

Privy's server wallets support agent use cases with policy enforcement, but identity is wallet-address-scoped with no ZKP proof layer — agents can't prove bounded permissions without exposing credentials.

Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication covers delegation chains and scope attestation but is OAuth/JWT-based with no cryptographic nullifier or chain-ID binding — ZKP approach is complementary, not redundant.
did:key and DID methods are the de-facto agent identity layer but are chain-agnostic with no replay protection across EVM chains — nullifier domain separation is an open gap in DID specs.

EIP-712 includes chain_id in domain separator for signature replay protection — the exact same principle Bolyra would encode in its ZKP nullifier, giving Bolyra a well-understood analogue to reference in demos.

Fetch.ai has an agent-native Cosmos SDK chain with key-based identity; cross-chain agent auth is handled via IBC relayers, not ZKP — no nullifier scheme, replay protection is at the IBC packet level only.
Autonolas runs multi-agent services across multiple EVM chains using multi-sig safe accounts; no ZKP identity layer — cross-chain replay is not addressed at the identity primitive level.
Virtuals tokenizes AI agents on Base; identity is token-ownership-based with no cryptographic delegation or ZKP nullifier scheme — consumer-facing, not infrastructure-grade identity.

Semaphore uses nullifiers for double-spend prevention in anonymous signaling groups on Ethereum — does not bind nullifier to chain_id, meaning the same gap Bolyra's fix addresses exists in Semaphore deployments.

World ID uses ZKP nullifiers for Sybil-resistant human identity across chains, but the nullifier includes an action/app scope rather than chain_id — cross-chain replay is handled by app-scoped external nullifiers, not circuit-level chain binding.
Aztec implements privacy via ZKP at the L2 computation level; agent identity is not a first-class primitive — focused on private DeFi, not agent authorization.

evidence.source: BN254 curve cross-chain replay — known EVM vulnerability class
evidence.summary: All EVM-compatible chains share the BN254 precompiles, making ZKP proofs verified via ecPairing replayable across chains unless the verifying contract or circuit binds chain_id — this is a documented class of vulnerability with no off-the-shelf ZKP-agent solution.

mapped_primitives.usage: Primary circuit requiring modification. The nullifier currently computes Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) for the scope commitment, but the replay vulnerability is in the proof's public outputs — adding chain_id as a fourth Poseidon input to the identity-bound scope commitment (or as a standalone public signal committed into the nullifier hash) cryptographically domain-separates proofs per chain. The PLONK build is preferred here since it avoids a per-circuit Groth16 ceremony when public inputs expand.

Delegation chains inherit the AgentPolicy credential commitment as their root. If AgentPolicy adds chain_id binding, the Delegation circuit's delegator-credential-commitment link (UC3.1 fix) must also incorporate chain_id in its scope commitment chain, otherwise a cross-chain attacker can strip the delegation envelope and resubmit the base AgentPolicy proof on a different chain. The scope commitment chain linking already exists — chain_id just needs to thread through it.

Adding chain_id as a public input to AgentPolicy changes the number of public signals, which invalidates the existing vkey.json and thus the deployed PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48. A new verifier must be snarkjs-exported from the updated .zkey and redeployed. PLONK's universal SRS (pot16.ptau) means no new trusted setup ceremony — just re-export and redeploy.

Same regeneration requirement as PlonkVerifier: if Delegation circuit adds chain_id to its public signal set, the DelegationPlonkVerifier at 0x285D2E must be regenerated from the updated vkey and redeployed.

The atomic handshake verification function must pass chain_id as a public signal when calling PlonkVerifier.verifyProof(). The root history buffer and nonce equality logic are unaffected. The contract needs a chain_id immutable set at deploy time (or read from block.chainid) so the verifier call is self-consistent — each chain's IdentityRegistry only accepts proofs bound to its own chain_id.

proveHandshake(human, agent, options) and the internal AgentPolicy witness builder must accept and forward chain_id into the circuit's public input vector. verifyHandshake must validate that the chain_id in the proof matches the expected deployment chain. The options bag already exists on proveHandshake, so chain_id threads in without a breaking API change.

new_components_needed.description: AgentPolicy.circom patch: add chain_id as a public input signal and include it in the Poseidon hash that produces the identity-bound scope commitment (or as a direct input to the nullifier hash). Constraint delta is negligible (~1-2 constraints for the additional Poseidon input). Requires re-running the PLONK trusted setup (snarkjs plonk setup using existing pot16.ptau — no new ceremony) and exporting a new vkey.

Delegation.circom patch: thread chain_id through the delegator-scope-commitment chain so the scope commitment chain linking remains consistent end-to-end across hops. Requires re-running PLONK setup and exporting new DelegationPlonkVerifier.

Regenerated PlonkVerifier and DelegationPlonkVerifier Solidity contracts (snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier from updated vkeys). Deploy to Base Sepolia and Theseus. Update IdentityRegistry to read block.chainid as the chain_id immutable and pass it in the verifyProof public signals array.

Add chain_id parameter to proveHandshake options (defaults to current chain from provider, overridable for cross-chain pre-computation). Update AgentPolicy witness builder, verifyHandshake chain_id assertion, and TypeScript types. Bump to @bolyra/sdk v0.2.1 (non-breaking addition).

Update draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 section on nullifier computation to formally specify chain_id domain separation. Add conformance test vectors covering cross-chain replay rejection (extend test-vectors.json with ~4 new vectors: valid same-chain, rejected cross-chain Base→Theseus, rejected cross-chain Theseus→Base, valid independent proof on each chain). Update did:bolyra method spec to note chain_id scoping of agent DIDs.

theseus_integration_surface: Theseus deploys a fresh IdentityRegistry (with chain_id = Theseus chain_id immutable) plus the regenerated PlonkVerifier and DelegationPlonkVerifier to their chain. Bolyra agents targeting Theseus call proveHandshake(..., { chainId: theseusChainId }) via @bolyra/sdk — the circuit binds the proof to Theseus's chain_id at witness-generation time. The IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia independently holds its own chain_id binding. An agent operating across both chains generates two independent proofs (same underlying credential, different chain_id inputs) — the ZKP verifier on each chain rejects the other chain's proof by construction. The integration surface for Theseus is therefore: (a) the IdentityRegistry deployment on Theseus's chain, (b) the SDK's chainId option in proveHandshake, and (c) an optional thin RPC bridge if Theseus agents need to present Base Sepolia root history proofs (cross-chain Merkle root sync, which is a separate tracked opportunity). No API layer or off-chain relay is required for the core domain separation fix.

deliverables.name: AgentPolicy.circom chain_id binding
deliverables.description: Add chain_id as a 4th public input to the Poseidon nullifier hash: nullifier = Poseidon4(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chain_id, expiryTimestamp). Single-line change + new public signal declaration. PLONK build preferred — no new trusted setup since pot16.ptau covers expanded public input vector.

deliverables.name: Delegation.circom chain_id threading
deliverables.description: Thread chain_id through the delegator-credential-commitment scope chain so a stripped delegation envelope can't be replayed on a different chain with the base AgentPolicy proof. chain_id added to the delegatorScopeCommitment Poseidon hash — mirrors the AgentPolicy change structurally.

deliverables.name: Circuit recompile + vkey regeneration
deliverables.description: Run npm run compile:circuits to rebuild .r1cs and .zkey for both AgentPolicy and Delegation (Groth16 + PLONK). Export updated vkey.json files via snarkjs. No new ceremony — pot16.ptau is the universal SRS. Validate witness generation with updated test vectors.

deliverables.name: PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier redeployment
deliverables.description: Export new Solidity verifier contracts from updated vkey.json files via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deploy both to Base Sepolia. Record new contract addresses. Groth16 verifiers get same treatment — two deploys total per circuit = 4 contract deployments.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry chain_id immutable
deliverables.description: Add `uint256 public immutable CHAIN_ID = block.chainid` set in constructor. Pass CHAIN_ID as public signal in the verifyProof call inside the atomic handshake function. Each chain's IdentityRegistry self-enforces its own chain_id — no cross-chain proof accepted. Root history buffer and nonce logic untouched.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/sdk chain_id option
deliverables.description: Thread chainId into proveHandshake(human, agent, { chainId }) options bag — no breaking API change. Internal AgentPolicy witness builder forwards chainId to the public input vector. verifyHandshake validates proof.publicSignals[chainId_index] matches expected chain. Update TypeScript types + JSDoc. Python SDK subprocess bridge picks this up automatically via the JS call.

deliverables.name: Cross-chain replay integration tests
deliverables.description: Test suite verifying: (1) proof generated with chainId=84532 (Base Sepolia) fails on a verifier expecting chainId=1; (2) same credential works on the correct chain; (3) delegation chain with chain_id threading rejects cross-chain stripped proof; (4) SDK throws descriptive error on chain_id mismatch. Uses existing Mocha + Hardhat test harness.

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec (call prep)
deliverables.description: Two-page technical spec covering: Bolyra's chain-scoped nullifier model, how it fills Theseus's no-human-key-path identity gap, agent-rooted trust chain via AgentPolicy + Delegation circuits, proposed Theseus chain_id registration (they need to assign a canonical chain_id), and the 3-call integration API (proveHandshake, verifyHandshake, onchain IdentityRegistry.atomicVerify). Includes a sequence diagram. Shareable before the Sunday call.

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday June 29 call: show the Theseus integration spec doc + the 3-line Circom diff that adds chain_id binding + a live terminal demo of proveHandshake failing with the wrong chainId (witness generation error). Enough to establish technical credibility and drive the 'what chain_id will Theseus register?' conversation. A deployed end-to-end proof on Base Sepolia is 5 days out — not Sunday.
risks: Theseus VM compatibility unknown: if Theseus is not EVM-compatible the Solidity PlonkVerifier approach doesn't translate directly — need to know their execution environment before investing in contract deployment
Theseus mainnet timeline unclear: if they're 6+ months from launch the integration is premature and the sprint is better spent on Base Sepolia proof-of-concept that Theseus can observe

PLONK constraint count change: adding a public signal changes the constraint fingerprint — if existing .zkey files have hardcoded constraint counts, compile will fail silently and produce a mismatched verifier; must diff r1cs stats before/after
Solo capacity: 5-day estimate assumes no interruptions; circuit compile + vkey export alone can surface snarkjs version mismatches that eat a half-day
Theseus chain_id registration: without a canonical chain_id from Theseus the nullifier domain separation is unanchored — this is a blocker for production but not for the Sunday demo
spec.success_criteria: A proof generated by an agent on Theseus's chain_id is accepted by the Theseus IdentityRegistry and rejected by the Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry (and vice versa), verified by the integration test suite with zero false acceptances across 100 cross-chain replay attempts. SDK API remains non-breaking: existing proveHandshake callers without chainId option default to current chain via block.chainid.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Three asks before implementation is production-ready: (1) canonical chain_id they will register — needed to anchor nullifier domain separation; (2) EVM compatibility confirmation — determines whether Solidity PlonkVerifier deploys directly or needs a Theseus-native verifier adapter; (3) testnet RPC endpoint + faucet access so the IdentityRegistry can be deployed and end-to-end tested on Theseus before mainnet. For Sunday, only ask #2 is blocking.

title: EIP-1186 storage proof relay to sync Bolyra IdentityRegistry root onto Theseus L1
description: Bolyra's IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia maintains a Merkle root of enrolled agent credentials. For Theseus agents to be verifiable on Base without a trusted bridge, Theseus L1 needs to pin that Merkle root. Using EIP-1186 Ethereum storage proofs, a light-client relay can submit the IdentityRegistry slot proof to a Theseus L1 contract, updating the pinned root trustlessly. A Bolyra ZKP generated on Base is then verifiable on Theseus by checking inclusion against the locally-synced root — no bridge operator required. This is the key architectural piece that makes Bolyra the canonical cross-chain identity layer rather than a chain-specific system; Theseus cannot easily replicate this without Bolyra's IdentityRegistry being the source of truth on Base.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' structural constraint on Theseus creates an inherent need for trustless cross-chain credential verification, but evidence relies on inferred architecture rather than direct Theseus documentation citing this need.
rationale.zkp_edge: Named alternatives (trusted bridge, multisig, ACL contracts) all require a trusted third party operator, which is a structural failure ZKP+EIP-1186 eliminates, though the storage proof relay is EIP-1186 mechanics rather than ZKP itself, making the ZKP edge specifically about proof portability once the root is pinned.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific primitives named (PlonkVerifier, Groth16Verifier, IdentityRegistry root history buffer) are reusable verbatim with zero circuit changes; new work is scoped realistically to BolyraRootPinner.sol admin stub (2 days) with the full trustless relay left as follow-on, which is an honest gap.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The ZKP circuit design and Circom expertise gap is real and well-identified, and IdentityRegistry as source of truth creates bilateral value, but the argument that Theseus cannot build this themselves in 3 months is underdeveloped given no assessment of their team's crypto background or whether competitor solutions (Lit PKP) could serve the same role.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with 'no human-controlled key path' constraint, meaning agents hold keys natively — raw EdDSA keypairs are the assumed current identity primitive, with no ZKP delegation layer described.

Lit offers distributed threshold-signature key management for agent wallets (PKP — Programmable Key Pairs) with Lit Actions for conditional signing, but no ZKP-based cross-chain identity or delegation circuit — operates on access control conditions, not cryptographic proofs of scope.

Turnkey provides non-custodial server-side wallets increasingly used for agent key management, but identity is simply ECDSA/EdDSA key custody with no on-chain registry, Merkle roots, or delegation proofs.

Privy offers embedded + server wallets for agents but is strictly a key-management layer with no cross-chain identity registry, ZKP proofs, or permission encoding — targets Web2 developers, not L1-native agent architectures.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + Verifiable Credentials spec — training knowledge
evidence.summary: W3C DIDs support agent DIDs and VCs support delegation via capability invocation, but the VC model relies on JWT/JSON-LD signatures — not ZKP proofs — and has no native cross-chain Merkle root sync mechanism.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent (referenced in Bolyra bookmarks context)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication is actively evolving; no settled standard exists for agent-to-agent delegation with cryptographic scope narrowing — this is a spec gap Bolyra can position into.

EIP-1186 (eth_getProof) is a mature Ethereum standard for generating Merkle-Patricia storage proofs; it is production-ready and widely used by light clients (Helios, Nimbus) — the relay mechanism proposed for Bolyra has strong technical precedent.

Fetch.ai has agent identity via its uAgents framework with DID-like addressing, but uses a proprietary ledger and has no ZKP-based credential or permission proof system — cross-chain identity is handled via bridges, not storage proofs.

World ID deploys Semaphore-based ZKP identity on-chain (human-only), proving group membership via nullifiers — structurally analogous to Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit, but entirely human-scoped with no agent credential or delegation layer.

Polygon ID offers ZKP-based verifiable credentials on-chain with on-chain state (identity state tree) and cross-chain state sync via state relay contracts — the closest live analog to Bolyra's proposed IdentityRegistry root sync, but human/VC-centric with no agent permission bit encoding.

Semaphore is deployed on Ethereum mainnet/L2s and proves group membership anonymously — Bolyra reuses its depth-20 ceremony for HumanUniqueness, giving Bolyra ceremony legitimacy; no agent-identity equivalent exists in the Semaphore ecosystem.

Helios demonstrates production-grade EIP-1186 storage proof verification in Rust for trustless Ethereum state reading — confirms the relay architecture is buildable without novel cryptography, only integration work.

mapped_primitives.usage: The source of truth for the relay. The 30-entry root history buffer on agentTree and humanTree provides a natural sync window — a relay submitting EIP-1186 proofs can pin any of the 30 most-recent roots without requiring the relay to be atomic with each Base Sepolia enrollment. The contract's deterministic storage layout (LeanIMT root slot) is the exact slot proven via eth_getProof.

Circuit is chain-agnostic by construction: the public output agentMerkleRoot is a field element that Theseus's pinned root can be checked against directly. A proof generated on Base is valid on Theseus if the verifier there checks the same root. Zero circuit changes needed.

Same chain-agnostic property as AgentPolicy. The scope commitment chain links credential commitments, not chain IDs. Delegation proofs generated on Base are portable to any chain that has the pinned root and the PlonkVerifier deployed.

Existing PlonkVerifier bytecode (0x28b99c48…) can be deployed verbatim on Theseus. AgentPolicy and Delegation proofs from Base are verifiable against the locally-synced root with no code change — only a redeployment.
Existing Groth16Verifier bytecode (0x5467FCbe…) deployed verbatim on Theseus for HumanUniqueness proofs. Enables full handshake verification (human + agent) on Theseus once the roots are pinned.
Redeploy verbatim on Theseus to support on-chain delegation chain verification against the pinned root.

new_components_needed.description: BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol on Theseus L1. Accepts an EIP-1186 proof bundle (account proof + storage proof for the IdentityRegistry agentTree.root slot on Base Sepolia), verifies MPT inclusion against a trusted Base Sepolia block header hash, and writes the proven root to a local pinnedRoot mapping keyed by block number. Emits RootPinned(blockNumber, agentRoot, humanRoot). This is the trust anchor — all Theseus-side ZKP verification gates against this contract.

TheseusBlockHeaderOracle interface / adapter. BolyraStorageProofRelay needs a trusted Base Sepolia block header to verify the storage proof against. If Theseus already runs an Ethereum light client (e.g. Helios-style), this is a thin adapter (~2 days). If not, a committee-signed header feed must be built as an interim measure until a canonical light client exists, which is significantly heavier. Estimate assumes Theseus has a usable block header source.

BolyraVerifierHub.sol on Theseus. Mirrors IdentityRegistry's verifyHandshake and verifyDelegation surface but reads from pinnedRoot (via BolyraStorageProofRelay) rather than a local LeanIMT. Wraps the redeployed Groth16Verifier + PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier. This gives Theseus dApps a single integration point identical to the Base API.

Root relay bot (TypeScript). Watches IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia for RootUpdated events (or polls every N blocks), calls eth_getProof for the agentTree.root and humanTree.root storage slots, and submits the proof bundle to BolyraStorageProofRelay on Theseus. Includes freshness logic to skip redundant submissions when pinnedRoot already reflects the current root.

Cross-chain verification support in @bolyra/sdk. Add optional rootOverride and targetChainId to verifyHandshake options so callers on Theseus can pass the pinnedRoot fetched from BolyraStorageProofRelay. Add verifyHandshakeOnChain(proof, { chainId, relayAddress }) as a typed wrapper. Backward-compatible — existing Base callers unaffected.

EIP-1186 storage slot manifest for IdentityRegistry. Documents the exact storage layout (slot derivation for agentTree.root and humanTree.root in the LeanIMT struct), the ABI encoding of the proof bundle, and the relay API surface. Required for Theseus to implement independent relayers and for conformance testing.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) On-chain — BolyraStorageProofRelay + BolyraVerifierHub deployed on Theseus L1; dApps call verifyHandshake on BolyraVerifierHub with proofs generated on Base, which validates against the locally-pinned root. (2) Off-chain relay — a stateless relay bot (can be run by Bolyra, Theseus, or any permissionless party) that submits EIP-1186 proof bundles to BolyraStorageProofRelay whenever IdentityRegistry root changes; relay submissions are trustless since the contract self-verifies the storage proof. (3) SDK bridge — @bolyra/sdk v0.3 exposes verifyHandshakeOnChain({ chainId: theseusChainId, relayAddress }) so Theseus-native agent frameworks (including Theseus's own MCP tooling) can verify Bolyra credentials with one SDK call, making Bolyra's Base Sepolia registry the canonical cross-chain identity source without requiring any bridge operator.

deliverables.name: Bolyra × Theseus Architecture Spec
deliverables.description: One-page doc + sequence diagram: EIP-1186 relay flow, trust anchors, and proof portability model. Covers BolyraStorageProofRelay contract interface, TheseusBlockHeaderOracle dependency surface, and the 3-step verification path (pin root → submit proof → gate action). Used directly in Sunday call.

deliverables.name: Verifier redeploy on Theseus testnet
deliverables.description: Deploy PlonkVerifier (0x28b99c48), Groth16Verifier (0x5467FCbe), and DelegationPlonkVerifier verbatim onto Theseus testnet using existing Hardhat deploy scripts with a new network config entry. Zero code changes — pure redeployment. Proves Bolyra proofs are chain-portable today.

deliverables.name: BolyraRootPinner.sol (admin stub)
deliverables.description: Minimal Theseus-side contract: owner-settable agentRoot and humanRoot mapping keyed by blockNumber. Emits RootPinned events. No MPT logic — root is set by admin call. Enables end-to-end proof verification demo before the trustless relay is built. Will be replaced by BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol but is production-usable with a trusted relayer as an interim.

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: Hardhat task that: (1) generates an AgentPolicy proof on Base Sepolia fork, (2) calls BolyraRootPinner.sol on Theseus testnet with the current IdentityRegistry root, (3) calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Theseus with the Base-generated proof, (4) asserts true. Single terminal session, no live bridge needed. Shows the key claim: Bolyra proofs generated on Base are valid on Theseus.

deliverables.name: eth_getProof relay script (off-chain)
deliverables.description: Node.js script that calls eth_getProof on Base Sepolia for the IdentityRegistry agentTree.root storage slot, encodes the resulting account + storage proof RLP, and submits it to BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol on Theseus. Uses viem's getProof. Runs as a cron or triggered by IdentityRegistry RootUpdated events via a Base Sepolia websocket subscription.

deliverables.name: BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol
deliverables.description: Core trustless relay contract on Theseus. Accepts EIP-1186 proof bundle (RLP-encoded account proof + storage proof), verifies Merkle Patricia Trie inclusion against a trusted Base Sepolia block header hash sourced from TheseusBlockHeaderOracle, and writes the proven agentTree.root to pinnedRoot[blockNumber]. Uses an existing Solidity MPT library (ethereum-optimism/Solidity-RLP or succinctlabs/telepathy-contracts MerklePatricia verifier) to avoid building MPT from scratch. High complexity — storage slot must be computed deterministically from IdentityRegistry's LeanIMT layout.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry storage layout, existing Solidity MPT library

deliverables.name: TheseusBlockHeaderOracle adapter
deliverables.description: Adapter between BolyraStorageProofRelay and Theseus's block header source. Three possible implementations depending on what Theseus provides: (A) if Theseus runs Helios-style light client, wrap its getBlockHash interface; (B) if Theseus has a block hash registry, read from it; (C) if neither, deploy a minimal trusted oracle (multisig-gated) as a placeholder. Complexity and days depend heavily on Theseus testnet access and docs — this is the critical external dependency.
deliverables.reuses: new — shape determined by Theseus architecture

deliverables.name: SDK relay helper in @bolyra/sdk
deliverables.description: Add relayRootToTheseus(theseusRpcUrl, blockTag?) to the TS SDK. Fetches the storage proof, encodes it, and submits to BolyraStorageProofRelay on Theseus. Companion verifyOnTheseus(proof, theseusRpcUrl) checks pinnedRoot and calls the deployed verifier. Lets integrators do the full cross-chain flow in 2 SDK calls.

deliverables.name: Integration test suite
deliverables.description: Hardhat fork-based tests: fork Base Sepolia + simulate Theseus with local Hardhat. Generate real AgentPolicy proof, run relay script, verify against pinned root. Tests the full happy path and three failure cases: stale root (outside 30-block buffer), tampered storage proof, wrong slot. CI-runnable without live testnets.
deliverables.reuses: existing circuit test harness, Hardhat fork config

spec.demo_description: Terminal demo: generate an AgentPolicy PLONK proof on a Base Sepolia fork, manually pin the IdentityRegistry agentTree.root to BolyraRootPinner.sol on Theseus testnet (or local Hardhat), call PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Theseus with the Base-generated proof, get true. No bridge, no operator. The ZKP is the bridge. Slides show the trustless relay architecture (EIP-1186 path) as the 3-month roadmap. The claim: circuits are chain-agnostic today, the relay is the 6-week build.
risks: TheseusBlockHeaderOracle: if Theseus has no Ethereum light client or block hash oracle, the trustless relay requires Theseus to build infrastructure first — solo founder cannot unblock this. Interim: trusted admin relay with multisig, delivered in week 2.

MPT proof verification gas cost on Theseus: EIP-1186 Merkle Patricia proofs are 200-400KB RLP and expensive to verify on-chain. If Theseus gas limits are low or the VM is not EVM-equivalent, the relay contract may not be feasible — fallback is an optimistic relay with fraud proofs.
Theseus testnet availability: if testnet is not publicly accessible before the call, the Sunday demo runs on local Hardhat only — sufficient to prove the concept but not live cross-chain.
IdentityRegistry storage slot stability: LeanIMT internal storage layout must be pinned — any contract upgrade that shifts the root slot breaks the relay. Add a slot-stability CI check.
Solo founder sequencing: BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol is 18 days of focused Solidity work. Any parallel SDK or other product obligations push total wall time past 8 weeks.

Theseus EVM compatibility: if Theseus uses a custom VM (not EVM-equivalent), PlonkVerifier bytecode redeploy fails and the verifier must be reimplemented — changes reuse percentage from 65% to under 30%.
spec.success_criteria: A proof generated by @bolyra/sdk on Base Sepolia (or its fork) passes PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Theseus L1 against a root that was pinned by BolyraStorageProofRelay without any trusted intermediary signing the root — only the EIP-1186 storage proof and the Theseus block header oracle as trust anchor. Measured by: relay script submits proof → RootPinned event emitted → verifyProof returns true → integration test green in CI.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Testnet RPC endpoint + faucet for contract deployment and gas. 2) Confirmation that Theseus is EVM-compatible (precompiles, opcode parity) — PlonkVerifier uses bn254 pairing precompile (EIP-197). 3) Block header oracle surface: does Theseus expose Base Sepolia block hashes on-chain via a light client, a block hash registry, or neither? This determines relay architecture. 4) VM gas limits: EIP-1186 proof verification is ~2-4M gas; need confirmation Theseus block gas limit accommodates it. 5) If Theseus has existing agent identity contracts, their ABIs — to wire Bolyra root pinning as a gating condition on existing agent registration flows.

title: Authorization proof layer completing Theseus's verifiable execution story
description: Theseus advertises 'verifiable AI execution on-chain' — but verifiable execution without verifiable authorization is an incomplete security story. You can prove WHAT a model computed; you cannot prove WHETHER that agent was authorized to act. Bolyra provides the missing half: a Groth16/PLONK proof that this agent held permission bits 0b00000111, delegated by entity X, not expired, not replayed. The partnership pitch to Theseus's investors (Orange DAO / future Series A) is: 'Theseus proves the computation; Bolyra proves the mandate.' This is a 3-month roadmap item requiring a joint spec that defines how Theseus's execution attestation format (unknown — TEE? ZKP? optimistic?) composes with Bolyra's authorization proof in a single on-chain handshake. It's worth raising on Sunday to plant the narrative seed, but the concrete deliverable is a joint whitepaper / IETF draft amendment (extending draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with an agent-execution attestation binding) that both teams co-author. This differentiates Theseus from every other agent L1 in the market.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' architectural constraint creates a structural need for authorization proofs, but the evidence is largely inferred from Theseus's design rather than citing documented pain points or community discussion.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol (MPC, no scope proof) and Turnkey (TEE attestation, no self-contained proof) are named and their structural failures articulated, but the gap analysis assumes Theseus needs a verifiable on-chain proof artifact rather than just trusting their own execution layer.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits map cleanly to the need with only minor modifications (Poseidon3→Poseidon4), but the IdentityRegistry requires major work for cross-chain deployment on Theseus's L1, and Theseus's attestation format is explicitly unknown, making the integration spec speculative.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The complementary narrative ('Theseus proves computation; Bolyra proves mandate') is credible and differentiated, and Bolyra's ZKP circuit expertise (Circom, trusted setup) represents a genuine build-vs-buy moat, but Theseus's identity roadmap and whether they've already engaged Lit/Turnkey is unknown.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path — agents hold their own keys and persist on-chain, making raw EdDSA keypairs the presumed identity primitive with no delegation or permission layer documented.

x402 is an HTTP-native micropayment protocol for AI agents; its presence in Theseus's orbit suggests Theseus is building toward agent economic autonomy, where authorization scope (spending limits, permission bits) is a natural adjacent need.

Lit offers Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) for agent signing with condition-based access control, but uses threshold MPC not ZKP — no cryptographic proof of permission scope is emitted, making it opaque to on-chain verifiers.

Turnkey provides secure key management via TEE-backed signing with policy rules, but produces no standardized proof artifact — verification requires trusting Turnkey's attestation infrastructure rather than a self-contained ZK proof.
Privy is human-centric embedded wallet auth; no agent-to-agent delegation primitive and no ZKP layer — not a direct competitor for the Theseus use case.

evidence.source: IETF draft-ietf-oauth-agentic-auth (in-progress as of training cutoff)
evidence.summary: IETF OAuth WG has active work on agentic authorization patterns extending OAuth 2.0; the draft addresses delegated agent identity but relies on bearer tokens, not ZK proofs — Bolyra's IETF draft amendment to add ZKP binding would be genuinely novel.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + DID-linked Verifiable Credentials
evidence.summary: W3C VC/DID infrastructure is the nearest standards-track analog to what Bolyra proposes, but VC presentation requires revealing the credential — ZK selective disclosure is a known gap explicitly called out in the VC-DI spec.
Fetch.ai agents use DID-rooted identity within their Agentverse but rely on centralized ACL-style registration, not cryptographic delegation proofs; no on-chain ZK authorization primitive exists.
Autonolas defines autonomous services with on-chain service NFTs encoding operator multi-sig, but no ZKP scope-narrowing delegation — closest architectural analog to Theseus among live agent L1s.
World ID is the most widely deployed ZK identity proof on L1s (Ethereum, OP), but is human-uniqueness only — no agent credential primitive and no permission-bit delegation circuit.

Semaphore v4 (the ceremony Bolyra reuses for HumanUniqueness) is deployed on Ethereum mainnet for anonymous group membership proofs; Bolyra's AgentPolicy + Delegation circuits extend this pattern into machine-credential space with no prior art.

Polygon ID ships ZK-native verifiable credentials on EVM chains, but targets human identity with a centralized issuer model — no agent-to-agent delegation circuit and no 8-bit cumulative permission encoding.
Aztec's privacy L2 uses ZK for transaction confidentiality but not for identity delegation — different problem space with no agent authorization primitive.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core authorization proof primitive. The selective-disclosure requiredScopeMask already proves 'this agent held permission bits 0b00000111 at time T, not expired.' For Theseus, the binding point is adding Theseus's execution attestation hash as an additional public input committed into the identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3 → Poseidon4).
