This transforms AgentPolicy from a standalone authorization proof into a statement: 'this agent was authorized AND this specific execution was covered by that authorization.' The PLONK build avoids a new trusted setup.

Proves the mandate chain behind the executing agent — human operator → agent → sub-agent up to 3 hops, with scope narrowing and expiry narrowing enforced in-circuit. For Theseus this answers 'WHO delegated authority to this agent for this action,' which is the missing provenance story Theseus's execution proof cannot provide. The delegatee scope commitment chain can be extended to include the execution context hash, linking the delegation token to the specific Theseus execution attestation.

Atomic handshake verification (Groth16 + PLONK in single tx) and root-history buffers are the on-chain settlement layer. For Theseus, the registry needs to either be deployed on Theseus's L1 or a cross-chain proof relay must be built. The existing delegation nullifier replay protection and hop-count enforcement are directly reusable as-is — they do not depend on which chain hosts the verifier.

Optional but valuable for Theseus's investor narrative: proves the human operator who issued the agent credential is unique (Semaphore v4 Merkle membership + scope-bound nullifier). Addresses sybil resistance at the operator layer — one human, one root of authorization. No modification needed; integrates via the existing proveHandshake path.

The IETF draft is the coordination artifact for the joint whitepaper deliverable. Needs a new section — 'Agent Execution Attestation Binding' — defining (a) the attestation_hash wire format, (b) how it enters the AgentPolicy public inputs, (c) the composed verification predicate. This section is the concrete output that differentiates the Theseus partnership from a generic integration and provides the narrative anchor for Orange DAO / Series A positioning.

PLONK verifier contract must be redeployed on Theseus's L1 (or a bridge must relay proof verification calls back to Base Sepolia). The verifier itself is stateless and chain-agnostic — same Solidity artifact, new deployment. This is the minimal on-chain footprint Bolyra needs on the Theseus side.

new_components_needed.description: ExecutionAttestationBinding: extend AgentPolicy to accept executionAttestationHash (Poseidon hash of Theseus's execution output) as a 5th public input and fold it into the scope commitment (Poseidon4 instead of Poseidon3). Keeps the existing requiredScopeMask selective disclosure intact. PLONK build only — no new trusted setup needed. Blocked on Theseus disclosing their attestation format (TEE report hash? ZKP public outputs? optimistic receipt hash?).

TheseusAuthGateway: contract deployed on Theseus's L1 that (1) accepts a Theseus execution receipt, (2) accepts a Bolyra AgentPolicy PLONK proof with executionAttestationHash matching the receipt hash, and (3) emits an AuthorizedExecution event only when both verify. Wraps the redeployed PlonkVerifier. Complexity is high because Theseus's execution receipt format is unknown — interface is a stub until Theseus shares their attestation spec.

IETF draft amendment: new Section 6 'Agent Execution Attestation Binding' in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01. Defines the attestation_hash wire type, public input layout for ExecutionAttestationBinding, the composed verification predicate (authorization_proof ∧ execution_attestation → authorized_execution), and security considerations for TEE vs ZKP vs optimistic attestation sources. Co-authored with Theseus team.

proveAuthorizedExecution(agentCredential, executionAttestation, options): new SDK function that wraps AgentPolicy proof generation with the execution attestation hash bound as public input. Returns {authProof, executionAttestationHash, nonce} suitable for submission to TheseusAuthGateway. Requires ExecutionAttestationBinding circuit artifacts to be available.

@bolyra/theseus: adapter package that bridges Bolyra SDK with Theseus's chain SDK — handles receipt ingestion, attestation hash derivation, proveAuthorizedExecution call, and TheseusAuthGateway submission. Stub-able until Theseus's SDK is public.

theseus_integration_surface: Three integration surfaces, in dependency order. (1) Spec layer first: extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 jointly with Theseus — this defines the executionAttestationHash wire type and is the prerequisite for all circuit and contract work; no code needed, just a co-authored IETF section. (2) Verifier contract on Theseus's L1: deploy PlonkVerifier (stateless, same artifact) plus TheseusAuthGateway (new contract) on Theseus's chain; this is the on-chain settlement point where Theseus execution receipts and Bolyra authorization proofs meet in a single atomic transaction. (3) SDK bridge: @bolyra/theseus adapter that agents call at runtime — ingests the Theseus execution receipt, derives the attestation hash, calls proveAuthorizedExecution, and submits the composed proof to TheseusAuthGateway. The Sunday narrative seed should anchor on surface (1) only: 'Theseus proves the computation; Bolyra proves the mandate — let's co-author the spec that composes them.' Surfaces (2) and (3) follow once Theseus's attestation format is disclosed.

deliverables.name: Sunday narrative deck (1-pager)
deliverables.description: Single-page pitch artifact: 'Theseus proves the computation; Bolyra proves the mandate.' Diagram showing AgentPolicy proof + Theseus execution attestation composing into a single on-chain predicate. No code. Plant narrative seed, extract open questions (VM type, attestation format, testnet ETA).

deliverables.name: AgentPolicy v2 circuit — attestation_hash binding
deliverables.description: Extend AgentPolicy.circom: add `attestation_hash` as a 5th public input. Swap Poseidon3 scope commitment for Poseidon4(operatorPubKey, permissionBits, expiry, attestation_hash). PLONK build avoids new trusted setup. Proves 'agent held permission bits AND this specific Theseus execution was covered by that authorization.'

deliverables.name: Delegation circuit — execution context binding
deliverables.description: Extend Delegation.circom: thread attestation_hash as an additional public input through the delegatee scope commitment chain. Enables proof of 'WHO delegated authority AND for THIS execution.' Scope-narrowing and expiry-narrowing logic unchanged — one-way constraint still holds.

deliverables.name: IETF draft amendment — section 7: Agent Execution Attestation Binding
deliverables.description: New section in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining: (a) attestation_hash wire format (TEE quote hash | ZKP output hash | optimistic commitment hash — one field regardless of Theseus's proving system), (b) how it enters AgentPolicy public inputs, (c) the composed 2-proof verification predicate, (d) security properties (replay protection via sessionNonce already in handshake). This is the coordination artifact for Orange DAO / Series A narrative.

deliverables.name: PoC demo harness
deliverables.description: Node.js script: generate a mock Theseus execution attestation hash, run AgentPolicy v2 PLONK proof with it as public input, verify on-chain against the existing IdentityRegistry (local Hardhat). Outputs a JSON showing both proofs composed. Readable by non-engineers. Target: 2-week deliverable to share with Eric Wang post-call.

deliverables.name: Theseus VM adapter (conditional)
deliverables.description: IF Theseus is EVM-compatible: redeploy existing PlonkVerifier.sol + IdentityRegistry.sol on Theseus testnet, add attestation_hash to verifyHandshake() calldata. IF non-EVM: build a cross-chain proof relay that posts a Merkle root of verified handshake proofs to Theseus via a light client. This is the major unknown — do not start until Theseus confirms VM architecture.
deliverables.reuses: PlonkVerifier, IdentityRegistry (if EVM); new if non-EVM

deliverables.name: SDK attestation helper (TypeScript)
deliverables.description: Add `bindExecutionAttestation(agentCredential, attestationHash)` to @bolyra/sdk. Returns an augmented credential ready for AgentPolicy v2 proving. 10 lines of glue code over existing createAgentCredential path.

spec.demo_description: Sunday: show the existing Bolyra handshake demo (AgentPolicy PLONK proof + delegation chain) running live, walk through the 1-pager narrative, and pose the three gating questions: (1) What is Theseus's execution attestation format — TEE quote, ZKP output, or optimistic hash? (2) Is the Theseus VM EVM-compatible? (3) Can we get testnet access + an engineering contact for joint spec authoring? No new code for Sunday — the narrative artifact is the entire Sunday deliverable.
risks: Theseus VM is non-EVM: cross-chain relay adds 10-15 days and introduces a liveness dependency — scope to EVM-only for v1

Theseus 'no human key path' thesis conflicts with HumanUniqueness root-of-trust; the sybil-resistance narrative weakens if operator layer has no human anchor — position HumanUniqueness as optional but additive for Theseus's enterprise customers
Eric Wang's team is pre-Series A ($100K): engineering bandwidth for joint spec co-authorship may not exist for months — own the spec draft entirely, position Theseus as reviewer not co-author

Attestation format unknown: designing Poseidon4 with a placeholder hash field works but requires the IETF section to remain abstract about attestation_hash provenance until Theseus confirms their proving system

Solo bandwidth: AgentPolicy v2 + Delegation v2 circuit changes require re-running snarkjs setup for new PLONK keys (~30 min per circuit) — budget a half-day for artifact regeneration and test suite updates
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus testnet transaction that calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() with a two-proof tuple (AgentPolicy v2 PLONK proof, Delegation PLONK proof) where the AgentPolicy public inputs include a real Theseus execution attestation hash, and the transaction emits a HandshakeVerified event with the agent's permission bitmap and the attestation hash — provable end-to-end in under 30 seconds from proof generation to on-chain confirmation.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Execution attestation schema/format (TEE quote structure, ZKP public outputs, or optimistic commitment spec) — needed before AgentPolicy v2 circuit is finalized. 2) Confirmation of VM architecture (EVM vs custom) — gates the contract adapter path entirely. 3) Testnet RPC endpoint + faucet access. 4) One engineering contact willing to review IETF draft section 7 and co-sign it for the Orange DAO narrative. 5) Clarity on whether Theseus plans an on-chain permission/delegation layer or expects off-chain solutions — determines whether IdentityRegistry deploys on Theseus L1 or stays on Base Sepolia with a relay.

title: did:bolyra:theseus DID method with Theseus L1 genesis tx as root of trust
description: Bolyra already has a W3C DID method (did:bolyra) and an IETF internet-draft. Extending did:bolyra to include a Theseus-native DID namespace (did:bolyra:theseus:<agent-genesis-tx-hash>) would make Bolyra the canonical identity layer for the Theseus ecosystem — every agent born on Theseus L1 gets a portable, cross-chain DID resolvable via the did:bolyra method. This is a 3-month roadmap item requiring: (1) Theseus confirming their genesis transaction format, (2) a DID document schema for agent-born identities, (3) a resolver plugin that reads Theseus L1 state. The partnership leverage is asymmetric and durable: once Theseus ecosystem tooling (explorers, wallets, agent marketplaces) resolves did:bolyra:theseus DIDs, switching identity layers means re-issuing every agent credential and breaking all integrations. Raise this on Sunday as the long-term vision after establishing near-term credibility with the AgentPolicy demo — it signals Bolyra is thinking about the Theseus ecosystem becoming a major vertical, not just a one-off integration.

rationale.agent_need: Structural 'no human key path' argument is plausible and Olas/Fetch.ai analogues show agent-native identity demand is real, but no direct Theseus documentation or community discussion was cited to confirm they specifically need a W3C DID layer rather than simpler keypair addressing.
rationale.zkp_edge: Competitors (Lit, Turnkey, Privy) are correctly named and their gaps (no delegation, no portability) are real, but the DID method resolution mechanism itself does not structurally require ZKP — a non-ZKP DID resolver over Theseus L1 state would work equally well, so the ZKP edge belongs to the AgentPolicy/Delegation layer, not the DID namespace proposal.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific primitives are named (AgentPolicy, Delegation, IdentityRegistry, did:bolyra, createAgentCredential), modification scope is assessed as minor, and deliverable estimates are realistic, but the resolver component has a hard dependency on Theseus confirming genesis tx format that could block the 3-month timeline.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The ecosystem lock-in argument is the strongest part of the proposal — once explorers and marketplaces resolve did:bolyra:theseus DIDs, re-issuance costs are prohibitive — but the analysis does not assess whether Theseus has crypto engineers who could implement an in-house DID method in parallel, which materially affects the asymmetry claim.

evidence.summary: Theseus is pre-mainnet, $100K seed from Orange DAO, no public identity/auth spec found — strongly implies raw EdDSA keypairs as their current approach, a gap Bolyra can fill.

evidence.source: Theseus GitHub / docs (no public repo indexed as of knowledge cutoff)
evidence.summary: No public Theseus GitHub or identity spec found; their 'no human key path' constraint is stated in marketing but lacks a published cryptographic design, confirming the identity layer is open.
Lit offers threshold-ECDSA agent wallets with programmable signing conditions; no ZKP-based delegation or cumulative permission model — misses the scope-narrowing delegation story Bolyra has.
Turnkey provides custodial key management for agents (MPC/HSM); no on-chain verifiable credentials or DID method — focused on key custody, not identity portability.
Privy's agent wallet product handles embedded wallets and policy enforcement server-side; no ZKP proofs, no W3C DID compliance, no cross-chain credential portability.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / ASI Alliance — AEA (Autonomous Economic Agent) identity
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai uses agent addresses derived from public keys on their L1; DID-like but not W3C-compliant, no ZKP delegation — shows the pattern but not the standard.

Olas registers agent services on-chain with NFT-backed ownership; no ZKP, no delegation circuit, no DID method — structural analogue showing demand for canonical agent identity registries on agent-native chains.
No existing DID method targets agent-native L1s (Theseus, Fetch, Olas); did:bolyra:theseus would be the first purpose-built agent-genesis-tx-rooted DID namespace — defensible standards position.

IETF working group on AI agent authentication is nascent; no draft yet covers agent-born L1 identity anchoring — Bolyra's IETF draft has first-mover positioning if extended to cover agent genesis transactions.

evidence.source: Polygon ID / Privado ID — ZKP identity on EVM chains
evidence.summary: Polygon ID uses Iden3 circuits for ZKP credential verification on-chain; targets human identity (KYC/KYB), no agent delegation model, not designed for non-EVM or custom VMs like Theseus likely uses.

Semaphore is the ZKP group-membership primitive Bolyra builds on for HumanUniqueness; not designed for agent-native chains, but Bolyra's reuse of its ceremony is a credibility signal in ZKP identity circles.
World ID proves human uniqueness via ZKP; entirely human-focused, no agent delegation, no L1 resolver — not competitive with did:bolyra:theseus scope.

mapped_primitives.usage: Agent credentials anchored to a Theseus genesis tx hash become the operatorKey input: modelHash is replaced or augmented by genesis_tx_hash as the agent's on-chain birth certificate. The permission bitmask and expiry enforcement remain unchanged. Selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask lets Theseus dApps query 'does this agent have FINANCIAL_SMALL permission?' without revealing full capability set.

Human-to-agent and agent-to-subagent delegation chains map directly onto Theseus multi-agent workflows. A Theseus orchestrator agent (did:bolyra:theseus:<genesis-tx>) can sub-delegate to worker agents with provably narrowed scope — the 3-hop limit and bitwise subset enforcement are already on-chain.

The agentTree LeanIMT already supports ~1M leaves — Theseus agents are enrolled by inserting their genesis-tx-derived commitment. Root history buffers (30 entries) give Theseus L1 finality windows time to propagate before a proof becomes stale. The existing atomic handshake verification works as-is once the agent's leaf is in the tree.

did:bolyra is the direct extension surface. Adding a 'theseus' method-specific-id namespace (did:bolyra:theseus:<genesis-tx-hash>) is a spec-level change — the resolver already parses method-specific segments but currently only handles Base Sepolia anchors. The DID document structure (verificationMethod, assertionMethod, service endpoints) carries over unchanged.

The IETF draft's mutual-authentication handshake protocol applies verbatim to Theseus-born agents: the agent presents an AgentPolicy proof where the credential commitment traces back to a Theseus genesis tx rather than an off-chain operator key. No protocol-layer changes needed — only the credential issuance step changes.

Applies when a Theseus agent must prove it is acting on behalf of a verified human (human controller in the DID document). The nullifier scoped to 'theseus' provides sybil resistance within the Theseus ecosystem while remaining unlinkable across other scopes. Not on the critical path for v1 agent-only DIDs.

new_components_needed.description: did:bolyra:theseus DID method extension — define the method-specific-id format (did:bolyra:theseus:<chain-id>:<genesis-tx-hash>), CRUD operations against Theseus L1 state, DID document schema for genesis-tx-anchored agent identities (verificationMethod type: BolyraAgentGenesis2026), and deactivation semantics (what happens when a genesis tx is orphaned/reorged).

Theseus L1 DID resolver plugin — reads genesis tx from Theseus L1 RPC, extracts the agent's EdDSA public key and initial permission set from the tx calldata, constructs a conformant DID document, and registers with the did:bolyra universal resolver. Must handle Theseus finality windows and map to the IdentityRegistry root history buffer.

createAgentCredentialFromGenesisTx(genesisTxHash, theseusRpcUrl, operatorKey, permissions, expiry) — thin wrapper over createAgentCredential that fetches the agent's on-chain identity from Theseus L1 and derives modelHash from genesis_tx_hash. Publishes a corresponding DID document via the resolver plugin.

TheseusAnchorBridge (optional but recommended) — a lightweight Solidity contract on Base Sepolia that accepts a Theseus L1 storage proof (via EIP-1186-style proof or a Theseus-specific light client) to trustlessly verify that a genesis-tx-hash corresponds to a live Theseus agent before enrolling it in IdentityRegistry.agentTree. Without this, enrollment is operator-attested (weaker security model).

Conformance test vectors — extend test-vectors.json with 10-15 Theseus-specific cases: genesis-tx DID resolution, DID document round-trips, reorg handling, delegation from did:bolyra:theseus agent to sub-agent, and cross-chain handshake verification.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Off-chain resolver — a TypeScript resolver plugin registered with the did:bolyra universal resolver reads Theseus L1 via JSON-RPC, fetches genesis tx, and returns a DID document; this is the first touchpoint Theseus ecosystem tooling (explorers, wallets, marketplaces) hits when resolving a did:bolyra:theseus DID. (2) On-chain enrollment — agent operators call IdentityRegistry.enrollAgent(commitment, merkleProof) on Base Sepolia; in the attested model this is a standard tx, in the trustless model it goes through TheseusAnchorBridge which verifies a Theseus L1 storage proof first. (3) SDK bridge — createAgentCredentialFromGenesisTx() is the developer-facing API; it abstracts L1 reads, commitment derivation, and DID document publication into a single call, making the 'your Theseus agent gets a Bolyra DID in one line' demo achievable for the Sunday meeting.

deliverables.name: sunday-demo-script
deliverables.description: CLI script that mocks a Theseus genesis tx hash (32-byte random), derives a did:bolyra:theseus:84532:<genesis-tx-hash> DID document, runs AgentPolicy witness generation with genesis_tx_hash as the operatorKey input, and prints the resulting nullifier + permission bitmask. No circuit changes — uses existing @bolyra/sdk createAgentCredential() with hash-as-key. Shows the 'agent born on Theseus gets a portable ZKP credential' story end-to-end.

deliverables.name: did-bolyra-theseus-method-spec
deliverables.description: Markdown spec extending did:bolyra with a 'theseus' namespace: did:bolyra:theseus:<chain-id>:<genesis-tx-hash>. Defines DID document schema (verificationMethod using Ed25519 derived from genesis tx, assertionMethod for AgentPolicy proofs, service endpoint pointing to Theseus L1 RPC). Defines CRUD semantics: Create = genesis tx mined, Update = on-chain state mutation, Deactivate = agent tombstone tx. Explicitly stubs the resolver interface so Theseus can implement it without waiting for Bolyra.

deliverables.name: theseus-credential-sdk-helper
deliverables.description: createTheseusAgentCredential(genesisTxHash, operatorPrivKey, permissions, expiry) in @bolyra/sdk — thin wrapper over createAgentCredential() that (a) validates genesisTxHash is 32 bytes, (b) derives a deterministic modelHash = keccak256('theseus:' + genesisTxHash) to namespace the credential, (c) returns a DID alongside the proof artifacts. No circuit modification needed.

deliverables.name: theseus-resolver-plugin-stub
deliverables.description: TypeScript resolver plugin implementing the did-resolver interface: resolve(did) parses did:bolyra:theseus:<chain-id>:<genesis-tx-hash>, calls a configurable Theseus RPC endpoint (stubbed to return a static fixture for now), and returns a DID resolution result. Stubbed RPC response is hardcoded to a known genesis tx fixture so the full resolution flow can be demo-ed without a live Theseus node.
deliverables.reuses: new (but follows did-resolver npm interface pattern already used in spec/)

deliverables.name: theseus-integration-guide
deliverables.description: Single-page integration guide for Theseus team: how to issue a did:bolyra:theseus credential at agent genesis, how to verify it in a dApp, what the Theseus L1 RPC must expose (genesis tx endpoint returning pubkey + block hash), and the 3-month roadmap (spec → resolver → on-chain verifier contract). Written to be dropped into Theseus docs or sent as a pre-read.

spec.demo_description: Run the demo script live: generate a mock Theseus genesis tx hash, derive did:bolyra:theseus:84532:<hash>, call createTheseusAgentCredential() to produce an AgentPolicy witness, print the DID document JSON-LD and the ZKP proof artifacts. Then show a 2-hop delegation: orchestrator agent sub-delegates to worker agent with FINANCIAL_SMALL only, enforced by the Delegation circuit. No live Theseus node required — frame it as 'here is exactly what happens the moment Theseus mainnet is live and you give us a genesis tx format.' Takes 5 minutes of screen time.
risks: Theseus genesis tx format is unknown — if they use something other than a 32-byte hash (e.g., a structured object or multi-sig output), the credential derivation step changes. Mitigate: keep genesisTxHash as opaque bytes and hash it down; ask them to confirm format on Sunday.

Theseus may not be EVM-compatible — the IdentityRegistry Solidity contract and on-chain verifier won't deploy if they have a custom VM. Mitigate: the ZKP proofs are chain-agnostic; the on-chain verifier is a 3-month item, not Sunday's concern.

Pre-mainnet timelines slip — if Theseus is 12+ months from mainnet, the integration sits idle and Bolyra gets no ecosystem distribution. Mitigate: frame the spec and SDK work as portable; DID resolvers don't require Theseus to be live.

Solo-founder bandwidth — 5.5 days is tight against other roadmap items (Python SDK, IETF draft revision, differentiation research). Mitigate: the Sunday demo script is 4 hours of work tonight; the rest is post-call contingent on Theseus showing genuine interest.
spec.success_criteria: Theseus agrees to (a) share their genesis tx format, (b) list did:bolyra:theseus as their canonical identity layer in their docs, and (c) integrate the resolver plugin into their agent explorer. Secondary: at least one Theseus agent marketplace or dApp calls verifyHandshake() against a did:bolyra:theseus DID within 60 days of mainnet launch.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Three blocking items: (1) Genesis tx schema — the exact byte format or JSON structure of a Theseus agent birth transaction, specifically which field serves as the stable agent identifier (tx hash, pubkey, contract address). (2) L1 RPC spec — an endpoint that resolves <genesis-tx-hash> to the agent's public key and creation block hash so the DID resolver can reconstruct the DID document without trusting a third party. (3) Testnet access — even a private testnet or Anvil fork with 5-10 sample agent genesis txs so integration tests run against real data. Without (1), the credential derivation is speculative. Without (2) and (3), the resolver plugin ships as a permanent stub.

title: Nullifier-Based Cross-Chain Agent Identity for x402 Micropayments
description: Theseus agents need to interact with Base and Ethereum contracts (DeFi, x402 payment endpoints, Coinbase Agentic Wallets). Revealing the Theseus-native key on Base links all cross-chain activity back to the agent's L1 identity — a privacy and security risk (once the Base key is compromised, the Theseus identity is deanonymized). ZKP nullifiers allow a Theseus agent to prove 'I am the same authorized entity' on Base without revealing its Theseus public key or linking individual transactions. Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit already implements nullifier-hash binding; an agent-rooted variant makes this work without the human Merkle tree. The @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 implementation provides the payment layer. This is the only approach that achieves cross-chain agent pseudonymity — EdDSA + Merkle alone leaks the linkage.

rationale.agent_need: The structural argument that Theseus agents holding EdDSA keypairs face cross-chain deanonymization is sound, but actual Theseus documentation or community discussion confirming this as a pain point was not fetched — evidence remains inferred from the opportunity brief itself.
rationale.zkp_edge: The comparison against Lit Protocol and Turnkey is concrete and structural (deterministic public key linkage persists with MPC/TEE approaches), and the nullifier domain separation argument is technically valid, though the claim that EdDSA+Merkle 'leaks linkage' needs clarification since Merkle proofs can also hide the leaf identity.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy and HumanUniqueness provide reusable building blocks (EdDSA verify, nullifier pattern), but the integration spec itself acknowledges 'major' modifications to both circuits are needed, and a new AgentNullifier.circom plus a new registry contract constitute meaningful new work that warrants more than a 4+2 day estimate.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build ZKP nullifier circuits in-house is plausible given Circom expertise barriers, but no evidence is presented about Theseus's actual team composition, roadmap, or whether they are already evaluating Lit/Turnkey for cross-chain identity.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (not fetched — web search denied; inferred from provided opportunity brief)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human-controlled key path; agents hold their own EdDSA keypairs, making raw key linkage across chains the default (and a structural deanonymization risk) without a ZKP nullifier layer.

Lit provides threshold-key MPC for agent wallets, granting cross-chain signing without exposing a single private key, but does not implement ZKP nullifiers for unlinkable pseudonymity — it still reveals a deterministic public key per chain.
Turnkey offers TEE-backed key custody for agents; like Lit it protects the key but does not decouple on-chain identity from the underlying keypair, so cross-chain activity remains linkable.
Privy targets human-owned wallets with embedded wallet UX; minimal native support for headless agent-to-agent flows or ZKP-based credential delegation.

evidence.source: W3C Verifiable Credentials + DID-Linked Resources specs — general knowledge
evidence.summary: VC/DID standards provide a substrate for agent credential issuance but lack ZKP nullifier binding for cross-chain pseudonymity; they rely on presenter-controlled selective disclosure, not circuit-enforced unlinkability.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent-auth (inferred from bolyra/CLAUDE.md reference to spec/draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF agent-auth drafts discuss credential scoping and delegation chains but do not specify nullifier-based cross-chain unlinkability — a gap Bolyra's circuit design directly addresses.
Fetch.ai has agent-native on-chain identity via uAgents but uses ECDSA keypairs with no ZKP layer; cross-chain pseudonymity is not a design goal and agents are linkable across interactions.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace coordination, not privacy-preserving agent identity; no ZKP nullifier mechanism present.

evidence.source: Aztec Network / noir identity patterns — general knowledge
evidence.summary: Aztec's ZKP-based account model demonstrates nullifier-per-action patterns in production on L1-adjacent rollups, validating technical feasibility of agent nullifier binding at chain scale.

Semaphore's production nullifier-hash circuit (already reused in Bolyra HumanUniqueness) proves the primitive is ceremony-ready and audited; an agent-rooted variant skipping the human Merkle tree is a well-scoped engineering delta.

evidence.source: Coinbase Agentic Wallets launch blog (provided signal URL)
evidence.summary: Coinbase's agentic wallet SDK explicitly targets agent-controlled Base transactions — confirming the Base payment rail Theseus agents will hit, and therefore the concrete deanonymization risk from key linkage across chains.

evidence.source: AWS x402 agentic commerce blog (provided signal URL)
evidence.summary: AWS endorsement of x402 as the agentic micropayment standard signals wide adoption, raising the stakes for privacy in x402 payment flows where per-payment key reuse enables behavioral fingerprinting.

mapped_primitives.usage: The Poseidon2(scope, secret) nullifier construction is the direct template for an agent-rooted nullifier. The nullifier domain separation pattern (scope-keyed, unlinkable across scopes) and nonce binding for replay protection transfer directly. However the identity anchor is currently the human Semaphore secret + humanTree membership, both of which are irrelevant for agents.

The credential commitment — Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) — is the right identity anchor for an agent nullifier. The EdDSA operator signature verification and agentTree Merkle membership proof establish that the credential is legitimate before a nullifier is derived. These two building blocks are directly reusable. The circuit currently lacks a nullifier output signal.

Sub-agent delegation chains apply when a Theseus orchestrator spawns child agents that independently interact with x402 endpoints. The scope-narrowing and expiry-narrowing guarantees ensure a child agent cannot claim broader cross-chain permissions than the parent. The hop-count ceiling (max 3) bounds delegation depth. No structural modification needed; chain-ID binding in the scope commitment would need to be threaded through.

The agentTree (LeanIMT depth 20, 30-entry root history buffer) on Base Sepolia is the on-chain trust anchor. Agent credentials registered here are the Merkle leaves whose membership the new AgentNullifier circuit will prove. The dual-tree architecture and root history buffer (supports ~10-minute liveness windows) carry over. The nullifier replay-protection pattern (delegationNullifiers mapping) is the direct model for a cross-chain nullifier registry.

The @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 implementation provides the payment transport layer. The HTTP 402 + Authorization header pattern is the integration surface where a nullifier proof would replace or augment the current credential attestation — proving 'I am an authorized agent' to the x402 endpoint without revealing the Theseus key or linking payments.

An AgentNullifier circuit targeting Groth16 (like HumanUniqueness, ~16K constraints) would reuse the existing deployed Groth16Verifier on Base Sepolia, avoiding a new verifier deploy for the first iteration. PLONK would require a new verifier.

new_components_needed.description: AgentNullifier.circom — agent-rooted nullifier circuit. Identity anchor: credentialCommitment (from AgentPolicy). Public outputs: nullifierHash = Poseidon2(chainId ++ scope, credentialCommitment), scopeCommitment, chainIdBinding. Private inputs: credentialCommitment, permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, agentTreePathElements[20], agentTreePathIndices[20], operatorSignature. Proves: (1) Merkle inclusion in agentTree, (2) operator EdDSA signature valid, (3) scope bits satisfied, (4) expiry > currentTimestamp, (5) nullifier correctly derived. Strips human Merkle tree entirely. Chain-ID enters nullifier domain so the same credential produces unlinkable nullifiers on Base vs Ethereum mainnet.

AgentNullifierRegistry.sol — extend or deploy alongside IdentityRegistry on Base. Tracks spent nullifiers per (chainId, scope) pair. Exposes verifyAndSpend(proof, nullifierHash, chainId, scope) and a read-only isSpent(nullifierHash) view. Uses the existing Groth16Verifier. Enforces that the agentTree root in the proof matches a stored root-history entry (liveness window). Emits NullifierSpent(nullifierHash, chainId, scope, timestamp) for off-chain indexing.

proveAgentNullifier(credential, chainId, scope, options) → {proof, nullifierHash, scopeCommitment} added to @bolyra/sdk. Mirrors proveHandshake signature. Fetches live agentTree root from IdentityRegistry, builds witness, runs Groth16 proving via rapidsnark bridge. Companion verifyAgentNullifier() calls AgentNullifierRegistry on-chain.

x402-nullifier middleware for @bolyra/payment-protocols. Intercepts outbound x402 Authorization headers and injects a Bolyra-Nullifier-Proof field containing the serialized AgentNullifier proof. Payment endpoint SDK (server-side) calls verifyAgentNullifier before settling. Theseus agents configure once via BolyraX402Adapter(credential, chainId) — subsequent .pay() calls are proof-transparent.

Chain-ID nullifier domain separation spec — extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a new section formalizing nullifier = Poseidon2(Poseidon2(chainId, scope), credentialCommitment). Defines canonical chainId encoding (uint64, big-endian), scope string hashing, and cross-chain replay prevention guarantees. Add 8-10 new conformance test vectors to test-vectors.json covering Base, Ethereum mainnet, and a simulated Theseus L1 chain.

theseus_integration_surface: Bolyra connects to Theseus at three layers. (1) On-chain: AgentNullifierRegistry deployed on Base Sepolia (and later Base mainnet) exposes a single verifyAndSpend entry point. Theseus agent wallets or x402 payment contracts call this to gate actions on proven identity without learning the Theseus public key. The agentTree root anchored in IdentityRegistry is the trust root — Theseus operators register agent credentials there once. (2) SDK: Theseus agent runtimes import @bolyra/sdk and call proveAgentNullifier(credential, chainId='base', scope='x402:pay') before each micropayment. The proof is ~200 bytes (Groth16), generated in ~0.6s via rapidsnark. (3) x402 payment layer: the BolyraX402Adapter middleware slots into the existing @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 stack. Payment endpoint operators verify the Bolyra-Nullifier-Proof header server-side with a single SDK call — no changes to their core payment logic. Coinbase Agentic Wallet integration would additionally expose the nullifierHash as the stable pseudonymous wallet identifier, replacing the raw Theseus public key in wallet lookup flows.

deliverables.name: AgentNullifier.circom
deliverables.description: New Circom 2 circuit. Anchors identity in Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, chainId, scope) — no human Merkle tree. Outputs nullifierHash = Poseidon2(anchorHash, sessionNonce). Reuses AgentPolicy EdDSA operator signature verification and agentTree membership proof verbatim; adds chainId as a public input and nullifierHash as a new public output signal. Targets Groth16 at ~16K constraints to reuse the existing deployed Groth16Verifier on Base Sepolia.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy (EdDSA verify + agentTree Merkle path), HumanUniqueness (nullifier domain separation + nonce binding pattern)

deliverables.name: AgentNullifierRegistry.sol
deliverables.description: Minimal Solidity contract on Base Sepolia. Maps keccak256(chainId, scope) => nullifierHash => bool. Exposes verifyAndConsume(proof, publicSignals) — calls the existing Groth16Verifier, reverts on replay. Root history buffer (30 entries, ~10-minute liveness window) copied from IdentityRegistry. No new verifier deploy needed.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry (root history buffer, delegationNullifiers mapping pattern), existing deployed Groth16Verifier

deliverables.name: AgentNullifier SDK wrapper
deliverables.description: Adds proveAgentNullifier(credential, chainId, scope, sessionNonce) and verifyAgentNullifier(proof, registry) to @bolyra/sdk. Thin wrapper: generates witness via snarkjs, calls AgentNullifierRegistry.verifyAndConsume on-chain. No new proving logic — reuses existing snarkjs prove/verify pipeline from AgentPolicy.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy SDK prove/verify pipeline, payment-protocols x402 credential injection point

deliverables.name: x402 nullifier proof integration
deliverables.description: Patches @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 handler to attach a ZKP nullifier proof in the Authorization header alongside the credential attestation. Payment endpoint receives proof; calls AgentNullifierRegistry.verifyAndConsume before processing payment. Existing x402 HTTP 402 flow unchanged — nullifier is additive.
deliverables.reuses: payment-protocols x402 handler, AgentNullifier SDK wrapper

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: One-page technical spec + sequence diagram: (1) Theseus agent registers EdDSA credential in Bolyra agentTree on Base Sepolia. (2) Agent computes AgentNullifier proof scoped to chainId=Base + scope=x402-endpoint. (3) Proof replaces raw key in x402 Authorization header. (4) AgentNullifierRegistry consumes nullifier — Theseus L1 identity never appears on Base. Covers: scope encoding, chain ID binding, delegation sub-agent flows, replay protection guarantees.
deliverables.reuses: Bolyra DID method spec, draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md structure

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: Runnable Node script showing: (a) existing AgentPolicy circuit generating a credential proof — proves Bolyra can already anchor agent identity; (b) existing x402 payment flow completing against a mock endpoint — proves the payment layer works; (c) hardcoded mock nullifier output where AgentNullifier.circom will plug in — marks the gap clearly. No new circuit needed for Sunday — demo is 70% live code, 30% annotated stub.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy test harness, payment-protocols x402 mock server, existing circuits/test/ scaffolding

spec.demo_description: Run the existing AgentPolicy witness generation (proves agent credential anchoring works today), walk through the x402 payment flow against a local mock server (proves payment layer is live), then show the spec diagram explaining exactly where AgentNullifier.circom slots in to replace the raw key reference. The circuit stub with correct signal declarations can be shown in an editor. The narrative: 'Every piece except one circuit is built — here is what we ship in 2 weeks.'
risks: Theseus VM compatibility unknown: if Theseus L1 is not EVM-compatible, verifyAndConsume cannot be called natively on Theseus — integration would be Base-only (Theseus agent registers on Base, transacts on Base, Theseus L1 is only the key origin). Clarify on Sunday call.

AgentNullifier circuit trusted setup: pot16.ptau is already available, but a new Groth16 zkey requires a phase-2 ceremony. For a 2-week prototype, use a dev-only zkey with no ceremony — flag clearly as non-production.

Solo-founder bandwidth: 12 days of focused circuit + contract + SDK work is tight alongside discovery-autoresearch and differentiation-autoresearch loops still running. Pause autoresearch crons during sprint.
Theseus testnet access: integration tests against real Theseus agents require testnet RPC + funded agent keypairs. Without this, e2e test is Base-only mock — real Theseus agent behavior unverified.

Scope creep from delegation chains: sub-agent x402 flows (Delegation circuit chain-ID threading) are scoped as minor but will surface edge cases. Defer to month-2 unless Theseus explicitly needs it at launch.
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus-origin agent completes an x402 micropayment to a Base Sepolia endpoint where: (1) no Theseus EdDSA public key appears in any Base transaction calldata, (2) the AgentNullifierRegistry rejects a replayed proof for the same (chainId, scope, nonce) tuple, (3) a different scope for the same agent produces an unlinkable nullifier hash (verified by Poseidon2 output diff), and (4) the full prove+verify round trip completes in under 3 seconds using the existing snarkjs pipeline.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Testnet RPC endpoint + chain ID for Theseus L1; sample agent keypair (EdDSA, likely Ed25519 or BabyJubJub — need to confirm curve to verify AgentPolicy compatibility); confirmation of EVM vs custom VM on Theseus (determines whether AgentNullifierRegistry can be deployed natively or remains Base-only); any existing agent credential schema (JSON format, field names) so AgentPolicy credentialCommitment encoding aligns; and confirmation of whether Theseus has an existing on-chain registry Bolyra should integrate with or replace.

title: PLONK universal verifier deployment on Theseus L1 for VM-agnostic proof acceptance
description: Groth16 verifiers are EVM-specific (Solidity + BN254 precompiles). If Theseus runs a custom VM, Bolyra's current Groth16 verifier contracts can't be deployed as-is. Bolyra already ships PLONK variants for AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits using a universal SRS (pot16.ptau) — PLONK verifiers can be implemented in any language without chain-specific precompile support. The proposal is to package the PLONK verification logic as a standalone Rust/Go library (Theseus is likely Rust-native given the L1 custom-VM pattern) so Theseus L1 can verify Bolyra agent credentials natively. This creates a hard dependency: Theseus's native agent auth is cryptographically tied to Bolyra's proof format and SRS, not something they can swap out post-launch.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint structurally requires agent-native credential verification, but the evidence that agents specifically need PLONK-based verification (vs. simpler EdDSA) on a custom VM is inferred from architecture rather than documented Theseus requirements.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (EdDSA + ACL contract) is named and the structural failure (no selective disclosure of permission scope, no delegation narrowing without trusted third party) is concrete, though the argument assumes Theseus's VM cannot easily implement standard ACL patterns.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy and Delegation PLONK variants exist with reusable vkey/zkey, but HumanUniqueness is Groth16-only (major blocker), IdentityRegistry must be fully re-implemented for Theseus's VM, and the Rust PLONK verifier crate is new work with significant scope (KZG over BN254, no-std).
rationale.partnership_leverage: The SRS lock-in argument is the strongest element — once Theseus embeds pot16.ptau and the PLONK wire format, swapping Bolyra is a breaking change — but the 3-month build-it-themselves threshold is weakened by the availability of arkworks/bellman Rust crates that Theseus engineers could adapt without Bolyra.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 (founded 2025, $100K Orange DAO) with a 'no human key path' constraint — agents hold their own keys and are first-class on-chain citizens, suggesting raw EdDSA keypairs with no delegation primitive yet.
Lit provides Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) for agent auth using threshold MPC, not ZKP; it is EVM-coupled and does not offer native PLONK verifier primitives or cumulative-bit permission encoding.
Turnkey offers hardware-backed key vaults for agent wallets but has no ZKP delegation layer or on-chain verifiable permission narrowing — purely custodial, not cryptographically enforced.
Privy targets human-facing embedded wallets; its agent support is limited to server wallets with no ZKP or delegation circuit layer, making it a weak alternative for agent-rooted trust.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core spec + Verifiable Credentials (w3.org)
evidence.summary: W3C DID/VC is the closest ratified standard for agent identity but has no native ZKP binding, no on-chain permission model, and no delegation-narrowing primitive — Bolyra's spec is a superset.

Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication covers OAuth-style delegation scopes but does not specify cryptographic proof formats or ZKP-based scope commitment — a gap Bolyra's IETF draft directly addresses.
Autonolas provides multi-agent service infrastructure on EVM chains with on-chain service registries, but uses smart contract ACLs rather than ZKP proofs — no VM-agnostic verifier pattern.
Fetch.ai runs an agent-native Cosmos SDK chain with DID-based agent registration, but verification is signature-based (Ed25519) with no ZKP layer or permission-narrowing delegation circuit.

Polygon ID uses Groth16 + Iden3 sparse Merkle trees for ZKP identity — EVM-coupled, no PLONK universal verifier, no agent-native delegation primitive; closest deployed ZKP identity system but structurally human-centric.

Aztec's Noir language compiles to UltraHonk/PLONK-family proofs with a Rust verifier backend — confirms that PLONK verifiers in Rust are production-feasible and a known pattern for VM-agnostic deployment.

The arkworks Rust ecosystem (ark-groth16, ark-plonk) provides production-grade PLONK verification in Rust with BN254 curve support — directly usable as the Bolyra PLONK verifier library for a Rust-native Theseus VM.

evidence.source: Orange DAO portfolio signal (ycombinator alumni DAO)
evidence.summary: Orange DAO's $100K investment in Theseus is a pre-seed signal; at this stage Theseus almost certainly lacks a bespoke identity primitive and would be receptive to adopting a cryptographically sound external protocol.

mapped_primitives.usage: The PLONK variant of AgentPolicy is the primary primitive here. Its .zkey and vkey.json (keyed against pot16.ptau) define the proof format Theseus must verify. The selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask and identity-bound scope commitment are exactly the semantics a Theseus-native agent auth system needs — no circuit changes required, only verifier portability.

DelegationPlonkVerifier covers the human→agent→sub-agent chain. Theseus would verify delegation proofs natively using the same universal SRS (pot16.ptau). The on-chain chain-state tracking and nullifier replay protection currently live in IdentityRegistry (EVM) — equivalent logic must be re-implemented in Theseus's VM, but the proof format is reusable as-is.

Currently Groth16-only — BN254 precompile dependency makes it undeployable on a custom VM without a full pairing library. If Theseus requires human root verification (Merkle inclusion, nullifier), a PLONK variant of HumanUniqueness must be built and a new trusted setup run against pot16.ptau. This is a blocker for full-stack identity (agents-only is unblocked).

The dual-tree architecture (humanTree + agentTree), root history buffers, and delegation nullifier tracking define the on-chain state model Theseus must replicate. The contract is not portable (Solidity + EVM), but its interface and invariants serve as the canonical spec for a Theseus-native registry module. The atomic handshake verification pattern (both proofs in one tx) is a design target.

Proof generation is entirely client-side (snarkjs PLONK in Node.js). createAgentCredential and proveHandshake are chain-agnostic — agents running against a Theseus backend generate proofs identically, then submit to the Theseus verifier instead of IdentityRegistry. No SDK changes needed for proof generation; a Theseus chain adapter replaces the EVM submission layer.

new_components_needed.description: Rust PLONK verifier library: port snarkjs PLONK verification (KZG commitments, BN254 pairing arithmetic) to a no-std compatible Rust crate. Must accept the exact proof wire format emitted by snarkjs (JSON with pi_a, pi_b, pi_c, publicSignals) and verify against vkey.json exported from the existing .zkey files. This is the hard dependency that locks Theseus to Bolyra's SRS.

HumanUniqueness PLONK variant: compile HumanUniqueness.circom against pot16.ptau using PLONK trusted setup to produce a new .zkey and vkey.json. Run phase-2 ceremony, update Groth16Verifier references in tests, publish updated vkey artifact. Required only if Theseus needs human membership verification; agent-only use cases skip this.

Theseus-native IdentityRegistry module: re-implement the dual-tree state machine (LeanIMT root history, delegation nullifier store, hop-count enforcement, nonce equality check) in Theseus's native language (Rust/Move/custom). Consumes the Rust PLONK verifier library for proof acceptance. Interface spec derived from the existing Solidity IdentityRegistry ABI.

Theseus chain adapter for @bolyra/sdk: thin module that replaces the EVM submission path (ethers.js IdentityRegistry calls) with Theseus RPC calls. Proof generation is unchanged; only the submission and receipt-parsing layer changes. Analogous to the existing OpenClaw adapter pattern.

PLONK proof wire format specification: a versioned, chain-agnostic JSON schema for Bolyra PLONK proofs (AgentPolicy, Delegation) that becomes the interoperability contract between the JS prover and any native verifier. Extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a 'proof-transport' section covering serialization, SRS pinning (pot16.ptau SHA-256), and vkey versioning. This prevents future SRS drift from silently breaking Theseus.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Verifier layer — Rust PLONK verifier crate embedded in the Theseus node or deployed as a native precompile; accepts snarkjs-format PLONK proofs for AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits, bound to pot16.ptau. (2) State layer — Theseus-native IdentityRegistry module maintaining agentTree LeanIMT roots, delegation nullifiers, and hop counts; calls into the verifier crate for proof acceptance. (3) Client layer — @bolyra/sdk with a Theseus chain adapter: agents call createAgentCredential + proveHandshake as today, but submit via Theseus RPC instead of ethers.js. The SRS binding (pot16.ptau SHA-256 pinned in the vkey and in the Rust crate) is the cryptographic lock-in: Theseus cannot swap verifiers post-launch without breaking all existing proofs.

deliverables.name: PLONK proof wire format spec
deliverables.description: Document the exact snarkjs PLONK JSON wire format (pi_a, pi_b, pi_c, publicSignals), vkey.json schema, and pot16.ptau SRS binding. This is the contract Theseus must verify against — no new code, just formalizing what already exists. Present on Sunday call as the integration anchor.
deliverables.reuses: existing .zkey + vkey.json from AgentPolicy/Delegation PLONK builds

deliverables.name: Theseus integration design doc
deliverables.description: Architecture doc covering: (1) which primitives are portable today (AgentPolicy PLONK, Delegation PLONK), (2) what Theseus must implement natively (PLONK verifier, nullifier store, root history buffer), (3) HumanUniqueness blocker and mitigation path, (4) phased rollout — agent-only first, human root later. Defines the SRS lock-in as an explicit partnership term.

deliverables.name: Proof generation demo (Sunday-ready)
deliverables.description: Node.js script using @bolyra/sdk that: creates an AgentCredential, generates a PLONK proof via snarkjs, serializes the wire format, and dumps the vkey + proof JSON to stdout. No Theseus chain needed — proves the client-side generation is chain-agnostic today. Run live on Sunday call.

deliverables.name: Rust PLONK verifier crate (bolyra-plonk-verifier)
deliverables.description: no-std compatible Rust crate implementing KZG commitment verification over BN254. Accepts snarkjs PLONK wire format (JSON deserialized into typed structs) and vkey.json. Must pass the same test vectors as snarkjs. This is the core hard dependency that locks Theseus to Bolyra's SRS. Builds on arkworks-rs (bn254, poly-commit crates) to avoid reimplementing pairing math from scratch.
deliverables.reuses: arkworks-rs ecosystem for BN254 pairing; vkey.json and proof test vectors from existing circuits/build/

deliverables.name: Theseus chain adapter for @bolyra/sdk
deliverables.description: Thin TypeScript adapter that replaces the EVM submission layer (IdentityRegistry contract calls) with a Theseus RPC interface. Proof generation is unchanged — only submitHandshake() and queryNullifier() are swapped. Conditionally loaded so existing EVM path is untouched.
deliverables.reuses: @bolyra/sdk proof generation, proveHandshake(), verifyHandshake() — new only the chain-submission layer

deliverables.name: Theseus native registry module spec
deliverables.description: Canonical spec for what Theseus must build natively: agentTree Merkle structure, nullifier replay protection store, delegation chain tracking, root history buffer (last N roots), and atomic dual-proof verification semantics. Written as a Rust trait interface so Theseus engineers have a concrete implementation target. No implementation — Theseus builds this side.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry.sol interface and invariants as the canonical reference

deliverables.name: HumanUniqueness PLONK circuit variant
deliverables.description: Port HumanUniqueness from Groth16 to PLONK so it runs against pot16.ptau without BN254 precompiles. Requires new trusted setup ceremony (pot16.ptau already exists, so only circuit-specific setup step). Unblocks full-stack human-root identity on Theseus. Lower priority than agent-only path — schedule after Rust verifier ships.
deliverables.reuses: HumanUniqueness.circom logic unchanged; pot16.ptau SRS reused; only proving system changes

deliverables.name: Integration test suite
deliverables.description: Mocha test that generates an AgentPolicy PLONK proof via snarkjs, serializes it, then calls the Rust verifier crate via a thin Node.js FFI wrapper (napi-rs) to verify. Proves the full pipeline works end-to-end before Theseus testnet is available. Acts as the acceptance gate for the Rust verifier.
deliverables.reuses: existing circuits/test/ infrastructure and proof fixtures

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: (1) call createAgentCredential() with an 8-bit permission mask, (2) generate a PLONK proof in ~2s via snarkjs, (3) show the wire format JSON (pi_a/pi_b/pi_c/publicSignals) and vkey.json side-by-side, (4) explain that any language with BN254 + KZG can verify this — no EVM required. Present the integration design doc showing the two phases: agent-only (unblocked, 35-day Rust crate) and full-stack with human root (requires HumanUniqueness PLONK variant). The SRS lock-in argument: Theseus ships with pot16.ptau baked in — they cannot swap verifiers post-launch without breaking all existing agent credentials.
risks: Theseus VM is not Rust-native — if they chose Go or a Wasm-only runtime, the Rust crate needs a Wasm compilation target (adds ~1 week) or a Go port (adds ~3 weeks)
Theseus has no public testnet yet — integration test against real chain is blocked until they provide RPC access; the napi-rs cross-language test suite mitigates this but does not replace it

Solo founder bandwidth: 65 days of focused work is realistically 4+ calendar months given other Bolyra obligations; the Rust verifier (35 days) is the only piece that cannot be parallelized or delegated cheaply
pot16.ptau SRS size (2^16 constraints) may be too small if Theseus wants to extend circuits later — need to align on constraint budget before locking the SRS
HumanUniqueness PLONK requires a new trusted setup ceremony — if Theseus wants human-root identity at launch, this becomes critical-path and compresses the timeline
arkworks-rs BN254 crate has had API churn — verify the pairing API is stable before committing to it as the dependency foundation
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus node running the bolyra-plonk-verifier crate accepts a PLONK proof generated by @bolyra/sdk, rejects a proof with a tampered publicSignals field, and rejects a replayed nullifier — all three cases pass in the integration test suite. The Theseus chain adapter submits a valid handshake to Theseus testnet and the on-chain nullifier store reflects the spent proof.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Theseus must provide: (1) their VM language/runtime (Rust? Go? Wasm?) so the verifier crate targets the right FFI boundary, (2) testnet RPC endpoint and faucet for the chain adapter integration test, (3) their existing agent key format (EdDSA curve — Ed25519 or BLS?) to confirm it matches Bolyra's agentPubKey signal encoding, (4) confirmation of on-chain state model (key-value store? Merkle trie?) so the registry module spec targets the right primitive, (5) agreement on pot16.ptau as the canonical SRS — this decision is irreversible post-launch
